- The ordering customer (bill-to party)
- The ship-to / end-use party (may differ from the ordering customer)
- Distributors, sales agents, and resellers acting on your behalf
- Freight forwarders and customs brokers (for completeness)
- Named end-users in end-use certificates
- Ultimate consignees
- Intermediate consignees
- Any third parties introduced mid-transaction

For your destination mix:
- Taiwan: Pay particular attention to whether the customer or end-user has known ties to
  Chinese entities or Chinese-funded fabs, given the EAR's focus on preventing
  semiconductor technology from reaching restricted Chinese end-users.
- India: Screen not just the customer but the stated end-use facility and any Indian
  government or defense-affiliated entities involved.
- South Korea and Japan: Lower routine risk, but screen systematically regardless —
  lists include parties in all countries.
- Europe: Include EU and UK sanctions lists (post-Brexit, UK has its own sanctions
  regime) in addition to OFAC/BIS lists for customers in the UK.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
FUZZY MATCHING AND ALIAS MATCHING
---------------------------------------------------------------------

Restricted party screening is complicated by transliteration, name variations, and
aliases. Your screening tool must:

- Apply fuzzy matching logic (typically 80-85% match threshold is a reasonable starting
  point, adjustable based on your risk tolerance).
- Screen aliases, also-known-as (AKA), formerly-known-as (FKA), and doing-business-as
  (DBA) names.
- Screen addresses and other identifying information where available.
- Flag partial matches for human review rather than auto-clearing them.

Establish a documented match review process:
- Screening tool generates a "hit" or "potential match."
- A trained reviewer conducts manual due diligence: compare address, country, industry,
  and other identifiers against the list entry.
- Document the determination: confirmed match (transaction blocked) or false positive
  (transaction cleared, with written rationale).
- Escalate any confirmed match to the ECO and Legal.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
DOCUMENTATION
---------------------------------------------------------------------

For every screening action, retain:
- Date and time of screen
- Names and entities screened
- Lists screened against (or confirmation that the tool covers all required lists)
- Result (clear, hit, or potential match)
- For potential matches: the reviewer's analysis and determination
- Authorizing signature for cleared transactions involving any potential match

This documentation is essential for audit defense and forms part of your five-year
EAR recordkeeping obligation.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
CUSTOMER DUE DILIGENCE BEYOND LIST SCREENING
---------------------------------------------------------------------

Restricted party screening is necessary but not sufficient. BIS's Red Flag guidance
(EAR Supplement 1 to Part 732, "Know Your Customer" guidance) requires that you also
conduct broader due diligence when red flags are present.

For new customers in your markets, consider also:
- Verifying the customer's business existence and legitimacy (website, trade references,
  business registration).
- Requiring end-use certificates or statements for controlled items, particularly for
  India and any new customer in any market.
- Conducting enhanced due diligence for distributors who will resell your equipment —
  review their customer list if possible, obtain flow-down compliance representations
  in the distribution agreement.
- Implementing a Know Your Customer (KYC) questionnaire for new distributors and
  high-value customers.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
AUTOMATED INTEGRATION
---------------------------------------------------------------------

For a 200-person company with active transaction volume, integrate your RPS tool with
your ERP or CRM system to:
- Automatically trigger a screen when a new customer is added or a new order is entered.
- Block order release until screening is completed and documented.
- Generate screening certificates that attach to the shipment record.

This automation reduces the risk of human omission and creates an auditable trail.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
INDIA-SPECIFIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR YOUR SCREENING PROGRAMME
---------------------------------------------------------------------

India presents additional considerations:

- Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) and entities affiliated with it have at
  various times appeared on restricted lists. Verify current status at each transaction.
- India's defense-civil integration means some ostensibly commercial customers may
  have defense connections requiring license review under EAR 744.21.
- For certain controlled semiconductor equipment, India may require a BIS license even
  where a license exception would apply to Japan or South Korea. Verify ECCN-specific
  India license requirements.
- Pursue Validated End-User (VEU) authorization for trusted, repeat Indian customers
  to streamline ongoing transactions.

=============================================================================

PART III: PROGRAMME IMPLEMENTATION ROADMAP (SUGGESTED SEQUENCING)

Given you are building from scratch, a phased approach is recommended:

PHASE 1 (Months 1-3): Foundation
- Hire/designate Export Compliance Officer
- Engage outside export control counsel for initial assessment
- Conduct ECCN classification review of product portfolio
- Implement commercial RPS screening tool immediately
- Draft and execute Export Compliance Policy (CEO-signed)
- Establish screening procedure and begin documenting all screens

PHASE 2 (Months 3-6): Core Programme Build
- Draft all core policy documents (classification, order review, deemed export, etc.)
- Implement role-based training programme; complete all-employee training
- Conduct comprehensive risk assessment; document in writing
- Integrate RPS tool with ERP
- Establish Export Control Committee
- Review and classify deemed export risk for foreign national employees

PHASE 3 (Months 6-12): Full Programme Operationalization
- Complete integration of compliance checkpoints into order-to-cash workflow
- Conduct first internal compliance audit
- Establish recordkeeping system and retention schedule
- Develop incident response and VSD procedure
- Submit VEU applications for qualifying Indian customers (if applicable)
- Benchmark programme against BIS guidance and peer-industry practices

ONGOING:
- Annual risk assessment update
- Annual training refresh
- Annual internal audit
- Continuous regulatory monitoring
- Quarterly Export Control Committee meetings
- Pre-shipment screening on every controlled transaction

=============================================================================

PART IV: KEY REGULATORY REFERENCES

- Export Administration Regulations (EAR): 15 C.F.R. Parts 730-774
- BIS Entity List: EAR Part 744, Supplement No. 4
- BIS Denied Persons List: EAR Part 764, Supplement No. 2
- BIS Unverified List: EAR Part 744, Supplement No. 6
- Red Flag guidance: EAR Supplement 1 to Part 732
- OFAC SDN List: 31 C.F.R. various parts
- EAR Recordkeeping: 15 C.F.R. Part 762
- BIS Penalty Guidelines: 15 C.F.R. Part 766, Supplement No. 1
- Commerce Control List (CCL): EAR Part 774 (ECCN 3B001, 3B002 for semiconductor equipment)

Note: The semiconductor equipment sector is subject to particularly dynamic regulatory
change. As of 2024-2026, BIS has issued multiple significant rules restricting exports
of advanced semiconductor manufacturing equipment, particularly to China, including
restrictions on items that assist in producing advanced logic chips below certain node
sizes. Monitor BIS Federal Register notices closely and engage outside counsel to assess
the impact of each new rule on your product portfolio and customer relationships.

=============================================================================

DISCLAIMER

This document reflects general knowledge of U.S. export control law and BIS compliance
programme design principles as of May 2026. It does not constitute legal advice.
Export control regulations change frequently and can be highly fact-specific. Consult
qualified export control counsel before making licensing determinations or compliance
programme decisions.

=============================================================================
Prompt: We are a federal agency developing a new HR system that will store employee PII, salary data, and performance reviews for 5,000 staff. It will be hosted on an internal data center. How do we categorize this system under FIPS 199, and which NIST SP 800-53 baseline should we select?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐ 3/5 assertions passed (60%)
❌Correctly identifies High as the overall impact level (high-water mark from High Confidentiality)
❌Selects the High baseline from SP 800-53B
✅Applies FIPS 199 framework to assess CIA objectives individually
✅Notes PT family (privacy controls) apply due to PII processing
✅References SP 800-60 for information type categorization guidance
================================================================================
NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 COMPLIANCE ADVISORY RESPONSE
FIPS 199 Categorization & Baseline Selection — Federal HR System
================================================================================

SYSTEM DESCRIPTION
------------------
System Name:    Federal Human Resources Information System (HRIS)
System Type:    Internal agency application (on-premises data center)
User Population: 5,000 federal employees
Data Types:     Personally Identifiable Information (PII), salary/compensation
                data, performance evaluation records
Hosting:        Internal government data center (not cloud)

================================================================================
STEP 1 — FIPS 199 SYSTEM CATEGORIZATION
================================================================================

Authority: Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) Publication 199,
"Standards for Security Categorization of Federal Information and Information
Systems" (February 2004), implemented under FISMA 2014 (44 U.S.C. § 3551).

Methodology: Each information type processed by the system is assessed against
the three security objectives — Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability —
and assigned a potential impact level of Low, Moderate, or High. The overall
system categorization is determined by the high-water mark across all three
objectives for all information types in scope.

SP 800-60 Reference: NIST SP 800-60 Volume II, "Guide for Mapping Types of
Information and Information Systems to Security Categories," provides the
recommended impact levels for standard government information types. The
following mappings apply to this HR system:

INFORMATION TYPES IN SCOPE
---------------------------
1. Employee PII (names, SSNs, addresses, dates of birth, contact info)
   SP 800-60 Type: C.3.1.1 — General Personnel Records / Privacy Data

2. Salary and Compensation Data (pay grades, salaries, benefits, deductions)
   SP 800-60 Type: C.3.1.2 — Payroll and Benefits Data

3. Performance Review Records (ratings, disciplinary actions, promotion records)
   SP 800-60 Type: C.3.1.1 — General Personnel Records

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CIA IMPACT ASSESSMENT — PER INFORMATION TYPE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

INFORMATION TYPE 1: Employee PII (5,000 staff records)
-------------------------------------------------------
CONFIDENTIALITY — MODERATE
  Rationale: Unauthorized disclosure of PII for 5,000 federal employees would
  cause serious adverse effects to those individuals (identity theft risk,
  financial harm, reputational damage) and to the agency (regulatory sanctions,
  loss of public trust, notification obligations under the Privacy Act of 1974).
  A breach would not rise to the level of "severe or catastrophic" affecting
  national security or critical operations, but the scale (5,000 records) and
  sensitivity of SSNs and financial data clearly exceeds a "limited" impact.
  Per SP 800-60, PII data is typically assigned Moderate Confidentiality.
  Impact Level: MODERATE

INTEGRITY — MODERATE
  Rationale: Unauthorized modification of employee PII records (e.g., altering
  direct deposit routing numbers, changing addresses, falsifying identity
  fields) would cause serious adverse effects — incorrect payroll disbursements,
  inability to locate employees in emergencies, erroneous background-check
  results. While damaging, such modifications are generally detectable and
  correctable; they do not rise to catastrophic operational failure.
  Impact Level: MODERATE

AVAILABILITY — LOW
  Rationale: Temporary unavailability of general PII records (beyond active
  payroll functions) would have limited adverse effect. Employees can be
  identified through alternative records; short-term outages do not prevent
  mission execution. HR lookups are important but not time-critical at the
  operational level.
  Impact Level: LOW

INFORMATION TYPE 2: Salary and Compensation Data
-------------------------------------------------
CONFIDENTIALITY — MODERATE
  Rationale: Federal salary data for GS-scale employees is partially public
  information under OPM regulations, but specific compensation details
  (locality pay, benefits elections, deductions, garnishments) are protected
  under the Privacy Act. Unauthorized disclosure would cause serious adverse
  effects to individual employees and could expose the agency to Privacy Act
  violations. Scale of 5,000 records elevates impact beyond Low.
  Impact Level: MODERATE

INTEGRITY — MODERATE
  Rationale: Integrity of payroll-linked salary data is critical. Unauthorized
  modification could result in improper payments, violations of the Improper
  Payments Information Act, OPM audit findings, and financial harm to employees.
  Errors in salary records could propagate into payroll processing systems,
  causing systematic overpayments or underpayments. Recovery could be complex
  and time-consuming, constituting a serious adverse effect.
  Impact Level: MODERATE

AVAILABILITY — MODERATE
  Rationale: Salary/compensation data feeds into payroll processing cycles. If
  the HR system is unavailable during a pay period processing window, payroll
  for 5,000 employees cannot be processed on time — a serious adverse effect
  on both employees and agency operations. Payroll deadlines are immovable;
  a missed pay cycle constitutes significant operational and legal harm.
  Impact Level: MODERATE

INFORMATION TYPE 3: Performance Review Records
-----------------------------------------------
CONFIDENTIALITY — MODERATE
  Rationale: Performance evaluations, disciplinary records, and promotion
  decisions are sensitive personnel data protected under the Privacy Act and
  5 U.S.C. § 552a. Disclosure could harm employee reputations, expose the
  agency to grievance proceedings, and undermine trust in the HR process.
  Unauthorized access to these records for 5,000 employees constitutes a
  serious adverse effect.
  Impact Level: MODERATE

INTEGRITY — MODERATE
  Rationale: Tampering with performance ratings or disciplinary records would
  have serious adverse effects — improper promotions, wrongful disciplinary
  actions, civil service merit system violations, and potential litigation
  (whistleblower retaliation, EEO claims). The integrity of the federal merit
  system is foundational to civil service law.
  Impact Level: MODERATE

AVAILABILITY — LOW
  Rationale: Performance reviews are typically accessed infrequently (annual
  review cycles, promotion boards). Temporary unavailability has limited adverse
  effect on day-to-day agency operations. Review cycles can be rescheduled in
  most circumstances.
  Impact Level: LOW

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
HIGH-WATER MARK DETERMINATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Per FIPS 199 Section 3, the overall system categorization is the highest impact
level recorded across all three security objectives for all information types
processed by the system.

Security Objective | Highest Impact Level Across All Information Types
-------------------|--------------------------------------------------
CONFIDENTIALITY    | MODERATE (PII, Salary, Performance Records)
INTEGRITY          | MODERATE (PII, Salary, Performance Records)
AVAILABILITY       | MODERATE (Salary/Compensation — payroll dependency)

                        +---------------------------------+
                        |  OVERALL SYSTEM CATEGORIZATION  |
                        |                                 |
                        |  SC = {(C, MODERATE),           |
                        |        (I, MODERATE),           |
                        |        (A, MODERATE)}           |
                        |                                 |
                        |  FIPS 199 CATEGORY: MODERATE    |
                        +---------------------------------+

Formal categorization notation per FIPS 199:
  SC-HR System = {(confidentiality, MODERATE), (integrity, MODERATE),
                  (availability, MODERATE)}

================================================================================
STEP 2 — NIST SP 800-53B BASELINE SELECTION
================================================================================

Authority: NIST SP 800-53B, "Control Baselines for Information Systems and
Organizations" (October 2020).

SELECTED BASELINE: MODERATE

Rationale:
  The FIPS 199 high-water mark of MODERATE maps directly to the SP 800-53B
  Moderate control baseline. This baseline encompasses approximately 323
  controls and control enhancements and is the appropriate starting point for
  all federal information systems categorized at the Moderate impact level.

  The Moderate baseline is also the most common baseline applied to federal
  administrative and mission-support systems, and it aligns with FedRAMP
  Moderate authorization requirements for cloud equivalents of systems of
  this type.

Why NOT Low:
  The Low baseline is insufficient for a system storing PII for 5,000
  employees, salary data with payroll dependencies, and sensitive performance
  records. The Privacy Act, OMB Circular A-130, and the NIST Privacy Framework
  all require controls commensurate with the sensitivity and volume of PII
  processed. Low baseline controls do not provide adequate access controls,
  audit capabilities, or data protection measures for this data sensitivity.

Why NOT High:
  A High categorization would be appropriate if a breach would cause severe or
  catastrophic harm — such as loss of life, national security compromise, or
  widespread critical infrastructure failure. While unauthorized disclosure of
  5,000 employees' PII is serious, it does not meet the FIPS 199 threshold for
  "severe or catastrophic" adverse effects. High baseline controls would impose
  disproportionate burden without proportionate risk reduction for this system.
  If future analysis reveals that the system supports law enforcement,
  intelligence, or national security HR functions, High should be reconsidered.

MODERATE BASELINE — KEY CONTROL FAMILIES APPLICABLE TO THIS SYSTEM
-------------------------------------------------------------------

AC — Access Control (Moderate Baseline)
  AC-2    Account Management: Formal account creation, modification, removal
          and review procedures for all HR system users.
  AC-2(1) Automated System Account Management [M enhancement]
  AC-3    Access Enforcement: Role-based access control (RBAC) enforcing
          least privilege — HR staff see only records within their scope.
  AC-5    Separation of Duties: No single user can both create and approve
          salary changes.
  AC-6    Least Privilege
  AC-6(1) Authorize Access to Security Functions [M enhancement]
  AC-17   Remote Access: Controls for any VPN or remote HR system access.
  Recommended ODV: AC-2(3) — disable inactive accounts after 90 days.

AU — Audit and Accountability (Moderate Baseline)
  AU-2    Event Logging: Log all read/write/delete access to PII and salary
          records, privileged user actions, authentication events.
  AU-3    Content of Audit Records
  AU-9    Protection of Audit Information
  AU-11   Audit Record Retention
  Recommended ODV: AU-11 — retain audit logs for 3 years (aligned with
          Privacy Act records retention schedules).

IA — Identification and Authentication (Moderate Baseline)
  IA-2    Identification and Authentication (Org Users)
  IA-2(1) MFA for Privileged Accounts [M]: Multi-factor authentication
          required for all HR administrators and privileged roles.
  IA-2(2) MFA for Non-Privileged Accounts [M]: MFA recommended for all
          users given PII sensitivity.
  IA-5    Authenticator Management: Password complexity and rotation policies.
  IA-5(1) Password-Based Authentication [M enhancement]

SC — System and Communications Protection (Moderate Baseline)
  SC-8    Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity: Encrypt all data in
          transit (TLS 1.2+ minimum).
  SC-8(1) Cryptographic Protection [M enhancement]
  SC-28   Protection of Information at Rest: Encrypt PII, salary, and
          performance data at rest (AES-256 recommended).
  SC-28(1) Cryptographic Protection [M enhancement]
  SC-7    Boundary Protection: Network segmentation isolating the HR
          system from general-purpose networks.

SI — System and Information Integrity (Moderate Baseline)
  SI-2    Flaw Remediation: Patch critical vulnerabilities within 30 days;
          high within 60 days.
  SI-3    Malware Protection
  SI-7    Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity [M]
  SI-12   Information Management and Retention: Manage PII in accordance
          with Privacy Act records schedules.

CP — Contingency Planning (Moderate Baseline)
  CP-9    Information System Backup: Regular backups of all HR data.
  CP-9(1) Testing for Reliability/Integrity [M]
  CP-10   Information System Recovery and Reconstitution

RA — Risk Assessment (Moderate Baseline)
  RA-3    Risk Assessment: Conduct and document a system-level risk assessment.
  RA-5    Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning: Regular vulnerability scans
          of the HR system and its hosting infrastructure.
  RA-3(1) Supply Chain Risk Assessment [M enhancement]: Assess risks from
          any third-party HR software components or vendors.

CA — Assessment, Authorization and Monitoring (Moderate Baseline)
  CA-2    Control Assessments: Independent security assessment before ATO.
  CA-7    Continuous Monitoring: Ongoing assessment of control effectiveness.
  CA-9    Internal System Connections: Document and authorize connections to
          payroll or other agency systems.

PS — Personnel Security (Moderate Baseline)
  PS-3    Personnel Screening: Background investigations for all HR system
          users commensurate with data sensitivity.
  PS-4    Personnel Termination: Immediate account deactivation upon departure.
  PS-5    Personnel Transfer: Modify access rights upon internal transfers.

PE — Physical and Environmental Protection (Moderate Baseline)
  PE-2    Physical Access Authorizations
  PE-3    Physical Access Control: Physical access controls for the data
          center hosting the HR system.
  PE-6    Monitoring Physical Access
  Note: As an on-premises data center deployment, PE controls are system-
  specific and cannot be fully inherited from a cloud provider.

================================================================================
STEP 3 — PT FAMILY (PII PROCESSING AND TRANSPARENCY) — MANDATORY OVERLAY
================================================================================

Because this system processes Personally Identifiable Information for 5,000
federal employees, the Privacy controls (PT family) under SP 800-53 Rev 5 are
mandatory regardless of the Moderate impact categorization. OMB Circular A-130,
"Managing Information as a Strategic Resource" (July 2016), requires agencies
to implement privacy controls for all systems processing PII.

The PT family (PT-1 through PT-8) must be applied as a privacy overlay on top
of the Moderate security baseline:

PT-1  — Policy and Procedures: Establish and maintain a PII processing policy
        documenting the legal authority, purpose, and use limitations for
        collecting employee PII.

PT-2  — Authority to Process PII: Document the legal authority authorizing the
        collection and use of employee PII (e.g., 5 U.S.C. Chapter 31 federal
        employment statutes, Privacy Act of 1974).

PT-3  — Purposes of Processing PII: Document and limit PII processing to
        stated HR purposes only. Do not use salary or performance data for
        unapproved secondary purposes.

PT-4  — Consent: Determine whether and how employee consent is required or
        appropriate for specific PII processing activities (note: employment
        relationships may not require separate consent for core HR functions,
        but supplemental uses require analysis).

PT-5  — Privacy Notice: Provide Privacy Act Statements at the point of PII
        collection informing employees of the authority, purpose, routine uses,
        and whether disclosure is mandatory or voluntary.
        Action Required: Publish a System of Records Notice (SORN) in the
        Federal Register for this HR system per Privacy Act requirements.

PT-6  — Privacy Reports: Report on PII processing activities as required by
        OMB reporting requirements.

PT-7  — Specific Categories of PII: Identify whether any special categories
        of PII are processed (e.g., financial account data for direct deposit,
        medical information in benefits records). Apply enhanced protections
        if sensitive PII categories are present.

PT-8  — Computer Matching Requirements: Assess whether any data matching
        with other federal agency records occurs, triggering Computer Matching
        and Privacy Protection Act requirements.

Additional Privacy Requirements:
  - Conduct a Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) per E-Government Act of 2002
    Section 208 prior to system deployment.
  - Publish or update the System of Records Notice (SORN) per 5 U.S.C. § 552a.
  - Designate a Senior Agency Official for Privacy (SAOP) point of contact.
  - Document data minimization practices — collect only PII necessary for HR
    functions; do not aggregate beyond operational need.

================================================================================
STEP 4 — SP 800-60 REFERENCE DETAIL
================================================================================

NIST SP 800-60 Volume II provides the following specific guidance for the
information types present in this HR system:

Information Type C.3.1.1 — Personnel Records:
  Default Impact Values: Confidentiality = Moderate, Integrity = Moderate,
                         Availability = Low
  Adjustment Factors: The volume of records (5,000) and the inclusion of
  financial data (salary) supports maintaining Moderate ratings without
  downgrade.

Information Type C.3.1.2 — Payroll and Benefits Data:
  Default Impact Values: Confidentiality = Moderate, Integrity = Moderate,
                         Availability = Moderate
  Rationale: Payroll availability is time-sensitive due to pay period cycles;
  Moderate availability is appropriate and consistent with SP 800-60 defaults.

Practitioner Note: SP 800-60 default values may be adjusted up or down based
on specific agency mission context. Adjustments must be documented and approved
by the Authorizing Official (AO). In this case, no adjustments from the SP
800-60 defaults are warranted — the defaults match the mission risk analysis.

================================================================================
STEP 5 — RMF INTEGRATION PATH
================================================================================

This categorization decision feeds directly into the NIST Risk Management
Framework (RMF), SP 800-37 Rev 2:

RMF Step 1 — Prepare:
  Assign ISSO, System Owner, Authorizing Official. Establish privacy roles
  (SAOP, Privacy Officer). Define system boundary to include all components
  storing or processing HR data.

RMF Step 2 — Categorize:
  Document FIPS 199 categorization in the System Categorization document.
  Categorization = {(C, MODERATE), (I, MODERATE), (A, MODERATE)}.
  Overall: MODERATE.

RMF Step 3 — Select:
  Select SP 800-53B Moderate baseline. Apply PT family privacy overlay.
  Identify common controls inherited from the data center/agency infrastructure
  (PE, MA, CP controls may be partially or fully inheritable). Document in SSP.

RMF Step 4 — Implement:
  Implement Moderate baseline controls. Prioritize: AC (access control),
  IA (MFA), SC-8/SC-28 (encryption), AU (audit logging), PT (privacy),
  AC-6 (least privilege).

RMF Step 5 — Assess:
  Independent security assessment using SP 800-53A Rev 5 procedures.
  Include Privacy Impact Assessment review.

RMF Step 6 — Authorize:
  Authorizing Official issues ATO based on residual risk. Complete PIA and
  SORN publication prior to ATO.

RMF Step 7 — Monitor:
  Implement continuous monitoring strategy per CA-7. Conduct annual control
  assessments. Manage POA&M items for any control deficiencies found.

================================================================================
SUMMARY AND RECOMMENDED NEXT STEPS
================================================================================

FIPS 199 Categorization:  MODERATE
SP 800-53B Baseline:       MODERATE (~323 controls/enhancements)
Privacy Overlay:           PT family (PT-1 through PT-8) — MANDATORY
SP 800-60 Types:           C.3.1.1 (Personnel Records), C.3.1.2 (Payroll)

Immediate Action Items:
  1. Complete SP 800-60 information type mapping and document in System
     Categorization document for AO approval.
  2. Initiate Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) per E-Government Act Section 208.
  3. Publish or update System of Records Notice (SORN) in Federal Register.
  4. Begin SSP development with Moderate baseline control selection and PT
     family overlay.
  5. Identify common controls inheritable from the data center (facility PE
     controls, network infrastructure SC controls).
  6. Implement MFA (IA-2(1), IA-2(2)) and encryption at rest (SC-28(1)) and
     in transit (SC-8(1)) as highest-priority controls given PII sensitivity.
  7. Establish audit logging (AU-2, AU-3, AU-11) prior to system go-live.
  8. Conduct background investigations (PS-3) for all HR system privileged users.

Key References:
  - FIPS 199 (February 2004)
  - FIPS 200 (March 2006)
  - NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 (September 2020)
  - NIST SP 800-53B (October 2020)
  - NIST SP 800-60 Volumes I and II (August 2008)
  - NIST SP 800-37 Rev 2 (December 2018)
  - Privacy Act of 1974 (5 U.S.C. § 552a)
  - OMB Circular A-130 (July 2016)
  - E-Government Act of 2002, Section 208

================================================================================
END OF RESPONSE
================================================================================
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐ 3/5 assertions passed (60%)
❌Correctly identifies High as the overall impact level (high-water mark from High Confidentiality)
❌Selects the High baseline from SP 800-53B
✅Applies FIPS 199 framework to assess CIA objectives individually
✅Notes PT family (privacy controls) apply due to PII processing
✅References SP 800-60 for information type categorization guidance
FIPS 199 CATEGORIZATION AND NIST SP 800-53 BASELINE SELECTION
Federal HR System — PII, Salary Data, and Performance Reviews (5,000 Staff)

==============================================================================
OVERVIEW
==============================================================================

This analysis walks through the FIPS 199 security categorization process for
a federal HR system storing employee PII, salary data, and performance reviews,
and then maps that categorization to the appropriate NIST SP 800-53 baseline.

==============================================================================
STEP 1: FIPS 199 SECURITY CATEGORIZATION
==============================================================================

FIPS 199 ("Standards for Security Categorization of Federal Information and
Information Systems") requires agencies to assess the potential impact of a
security breach across three security objectives:

  - Confidentiality: Preserving authorized restrictions on access and disclosure
  - Integrity: Guarding against improper modification or destruction
  - Availability: Ensuring timely and reliable access to information

For each objective, the potential impact is rated LOW, MODERATE, or HIGH.
The overall system categorization is determined by the "high-water mark" —
the highest impact level across all three objectives.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Information Types in Scope
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This HR system contains the following information types (aligned to NIST SP
800-60, Volume II, "Guide for Mapping Types of Information and Information
Systems to Security Categories"):

  1. Personally Identifiable Information (PII) — employee records
     NIST 800-60 Type: C.3.1.1 — General Information (PII)

  2. Salary / Compensation Data — financial HR records
     NIST 800-60 Type: C.3.5.1 — Compensation Management (Pay and Benefits)

  3. Performance Reviews — employee appraisal records
     NIST 800-60 Type: C.3.1.2 — Human Resources Management (Performance)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Impact Analysis by Security Objective
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CONFIDENTIALITY
  - Employee PII (names, SSNs, home addresses, dates of birth, etc.) is
    sensitive personal data protected under the Privacy Act of 1974. Unauthorized
    disclosure could harm 5,000 individuals through identity theft, financial
    harm, or reputational damage.
  - Salary data is sensitive and its exposure could cause significant
    embarrassment, workplace conflict, and individual financial harm.
  - Performance reviews contain evaluative judgments that could harm careers
    or violate privacy expectations if disclosed.
  - Impact Rating: MODERATE to HIGH. A breach affecting 5,000 federal employees
    involving SSNs and financial data would cause serious adverse effects.
    Most agencies categorize this type of system at MODERATE for confidentiality
    as the standard baseline, though agencies with a large workforce or highly
    sensitive positions may elect HIGH.
  - Recommended Rating: MODERATE (with consideration for HIGH if the workforce
    includes sensitive national security positions or the agency's risk
    tolerance requires it)

INTEGRITY
  - Corruption or unauthorized modification of salary data could result in
    improper payments, payroll fraud, or denial of correct compensation —
    causing direct financial harm to employees and the agency.
  - Modification of performance records could impair promotion decisions,
    disciplinary actions, or workforce management functions.
  - PII corruption could cause incorrect benefits enrollment or identity errors.
  - Impact Rating: MODERATE. Integrity failures would cause serious adverse
    effects on agency mission and individual employee welfare, but are unlikely
    to be catastrophic or irreversible in most scenarios.
  - Recommended Rating: MODERATE

AVAILABILITY
  - An HR system supporting 5,000 staff must be available for payroll
    processing cycles, benefits enrollment windows, and onboarding/offboarding
    workflows. Extended outages could disrupt pay, benefits, and HR operations.
  - However, the system does not typically support life-safety or real-time
    mission-critical operations (e.g., emergency response).
  - Impact Rating: MODERATE. Temporary unavailability would cause significant
    disruption but is unlikely to result in catastrophic harm.
  - Recommended Rating: MODERATE

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Security Categorization Result
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Applying the high-water mark principle per FIPS 199:

  SC HR_System = {(Confidentiality, MODERATE), (Integrity, MODERATE),
                  (Availability, MODERATE)}

  OVERALL SYSTEM CATEGORIZATION: MODERATE

This is expressed in FIPS 199 notation as:

  SC HR_System = {(confidentiality, MODERATE), (integrity, MODERATE),
                  (availability, MODERATE)} = MODERATE

NOTE: If the agency determines that the confidentiality impact should be HIGH
(e.g., due to inclusion of law enforcement personnel records, SCI-cleared staff
data, or whistleblower information), the overall categorization would become
HIGH, and a different baseline would apply. Agencies should document their
rationale in the System Security Plan (SSP).

==============================================================================
STEP 2: NIST SP 800-53 BASELINE SELECTION
==============================================================================

NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 ("Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems
and Organizations") provides three control baselines tied directly to the FIPS
199 categorization:

  - LOW Baseline:      For systems categorized as LOW overall
  - MODERATE Baseline: For systems categorized as MODERATE overall
  - HIGH Baseline:     For systems categorized as HIGH overall

Based on the MODERATE overall categorization above:

  SELECTED BASELINE: MODERATE

The NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 MODERATE baseline includes approximately 323 control
requirements (base controls plus required enhancements) across 20 control
families. Key control families particularly relevant to this HR system include:

  AC  — Access Control
        Enforce least-privilege access; role-based access control for HR
        personnel, managers, and system administrators. Restrict access to PII
        to only authorized users.

  AT  — Awareness and Training
        Security awareness training for all users handling PII and salary data.

  AU  — Audit and Accountability
        Log and review access to PII, salary records, and performance data.
        Audit logs must be protected against tampering.

  CA  — Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring
        Conduct security assessments; obtain Authority to Operate (ATO) from
        the Authorizing Official (AO); continuous monitoring program.

  CM  — Configuration Management
        Maintain baselines for the HR application, database, and server
        configurations.

  CP  — Contingency Planning
        Backup and recovery procedures for PII and HR data; test recovery
        capabilities; define Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point
        Objective (RPO).

  IA  — Identification and Authentication
        Multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all privileged users and remote
        access; strong password policies; PIV/CAC authentication where required
        by HSPD-12.

  IR  — Incident Response
        Documented incident response plan that specifically addresses PII
        breaches, including OMB Memorandum M-17-12 breach notification
        requirements (report to US-CERT within 1 hour of discovery).

  MP  — Media Protection
        Protect and sanitize removable media; encrypt data at rest on storage
        media containing PII.

  PE  — Physical and Environmental Protection
        Physical access controls to the internal data center; visitor controls;
        environmental safeguards (fire, flood, HVAC).

  PL  — Planning
        System Security Plan (SSP) documenting the security architecture,
        control implementation, and interconnections.

  PS  — Personnel Security
        Screening of individuals with access to PII; rules of behavior; access
        termination procedures for separated employees.

  PT  — PII Processing and Transparency (Privacy Controls — Rev. 5 addition)
        Privacy controls specifically addressing lawful collection, use, and
        retention of PII; Privacy Act System of Records Notice (SORN); Privacy
        Impact Assessment (PIA) required per E-Government Act of 2002.

  RA  — Risk Assessment
        System-level risk assessment; vulnerability scanning; supply chain
        risk management.

  SA  — System and Services Acquisition
        Secure development practices; security requirements in acquisition
        contracts; software assurance.

  SC  — System and Communications Protection
        Encryption of PII and salary data in transit (TLS 1.2+); network
        segmentation; boundary protection.

  SI  — System and Information Integrity
        Malware protection; security alerts; flaw remediation; spam protection.

==============================================================================
STEP 3: ADDITIONAL COMPLIANCE OBLIGATIONS
==============================================================================

Beyond the NIST SP 800-53 MODERATE baseline, this system triggers several
additional federal requirements:

  1. PRIVACY ACT OF 1974
     The system constitutes a "System of Records" under the Privacy Act because
     it retrieves employee information by personal identifier. The agency must:
     - Publish a System of Records Notice (SORN) in the Federal Register
     - Establish a routine uses schedule
     - Respond to Privacy Act requests

  2. PRIVACY IMPACT ASSESSMENT (PIA)
     Required under Section 208 of the E-Government Act of 2002. Must be
     completed before deployment and updated when the system changes
     significantly.

  3. OMB CIRCULAR A-130 (Managing Information as a Strategic Resource)
     Requires senior agency official for privacy (SAOP) involvement, privacy
     continuous monitoring, and privacy risk management.

  4. FISMA (Federal Information Security Modernization Act of 2014)
     Requires an Authority to Operate (ATO) from the Authorizing Official.
     The ATO package must include: SSP, Security Assessment Report (SAR),
     Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M).

  5. OMB M-17-12 (Preparing for and Responding to a Breach of PII)
     Establishes breach response requirements including US-CERT notification
     within 1 hour and affected-individual notification timelines.

  6. HSPD-12 / PIV
     Federal employees must authenticate using Personal Identity Verification
     (PIV) cards for logical access to federal systems where technically
     feasible.

  7. NIST SP 800-122 (Guide to Protecting the Confidentiality of PII)
     Provides supplemental guidance specific to PII protection, including
     de-identification and minimization techniques.

==============================================================================
STEP 4: RECOMMENDED IMPLEMENTATION APPROACH
==============================================================================

  1. Conduct a formal FIPS 199 categorization workshop with the system owner,
     information owner, and ISSO — document all information types and impact
     ratings in the SSP.

  2. Register the system in the agency's GRC tool (e.g., CSAM, Xacta, or
     similar) under the MODERATE baseline.

  3. Tailor the MODERATE baseline as needed:
     - Apply organization-defined parameters (ODPs) per agency policy
     - Add compensating controls where required
     - Scoping guidance: exclude controls not applicable to the internal
       data center hosting model (e.g., some cloud-specific controls)

  4. Complete the Privacy Impact Assessment and submit the SORN for
     Federal Register publication prior to system go-live.

  5. Conduct security assessment (typically by an independent assessor or
     the agency's A&A team) against the tailored MODERATE baseline.

  6. Obtain ATO from the Authorizing Official before processing live PII.

  7. Establish a continuous monitoring strategy per NIST SP 800-137 aligned
     to the MODERATE categorization.

==============================================================================
SUMMARY
==============================================================================

  FIPS 199 Categorization:  MODERATE
                             {(Confidentiality, MODERATE),
                              (Integrity, MODERATE),
                              (Availability, MODERATE)}

  NIST SP 800-53 Baseline:  MODERATE (~323 controls and enhancements)

  Key Additional Frameworks: Privacy Act SORN, PIA (E-Gov Act), FISMA ATO,
                              OMB M-17-12, HSPD-12/PIV, NIST SP 800-122

  Hosting Consideration:    Internal data center hosting is fully compatible
                             with the MODERATE baseline. The agency retains
                             full control over physical and environmental
                             controls (PE family) and does not need to apply
                             FedRAMP authorization processes applicable to
                             cloud service providers.

==============================================================================
Prompt: Our federal system SSP has a finding from the security assessor: AC-2(3) is 'Other Than Satisfied' because inactive accounts are not disabled within our stated ODV of 90 days. The assessor found 120 accounts inactive for over 120 days. Walk me through how to remediate this finding and what we need to document in the POA&M.
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Identifies AC-2(3) as the Automated/Disable Accounts control and explains what it requires
✅Recommends immediately disabling the 120 identified inactive accounts
✅Provides POA&M required fields including control ID, risk level, milestone steps, and completion date
✅Advises fixing the automated account review/enforcement process
✅Notes that OTS findings must be tracked until closure and mentions ISSO/System Owner accountability
================================================================================
EXPERT NIST SP 800-53 REV 5 RESPONSE
FINDING: AC-2(3) — Other Than Satisfied (OTS)
Federal System Security Plan (SSP) — Inactive Account Remediation and POA&M Guidance
================================================================================
Date: 2026-05-07
Control: AC-2(3) | Baseline: Moderate and High | Family: Access Control (AC)
================================================================================


PART 1: AC-2(3) CONTROL DESCRIPTION AND REGULATORY CONTEXT
------------------------------------------------------------

Control: AC-2(3) — Account Management | Disable Accounts

Control Statement (NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5):
  "Disable accounts within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when
  the accounts: (a) Have expired; (b) Are no longer associated with a user or
  individual; (c) Are in violation of organizational policy; or (d) Have been
  inactive for [Assignment: organization-defined time period]."

Baseline Assignment:
  - Low:      NOT required
  - Moderate: REQUIRED
  - High:     REQUIRED

Your ODV (Organization-Defined Value): 90 days of inactivity triggers mandatory
account disablement.

Why This Control Matters:
AC-2(3) is a foundational access control requirement because dormant accounts
represent one of the highest-probability attack vectors for credential-based
intrusions. Inactive accounts:
  - Are rarely monitored for anomalous logon attempts
  - May retain stale but valid credentials (unchanged passwords)
  - Provide unauthorized access if credentials are compromised
  - Violate the principle of least privilege (AC-6) by maintaining access for
    users who no longer require it
  - Can be exploited in lateral movement during a breach

The assessor's finding — 120 accounts inactive for over 120 days against your
stated ODV of 90 days — is a clear, objective, and documented OTS finding.
The violation exceeds your ODV by 30+ days and affects a significant population
(120 accounts), which elevates the risk severity.

Related Controls You Must Also Review:
  - AC-2 (base): Account management lifecycle — creation, review, modification,
    removal
  - AC-2(1): Automated system account management (Moderate/High)
  - AC-2(5): Inactivity logout (session-level; separate from AC-2(3))
  - AC-5: Separation of duties — verify disabled account holders had no
    conflicting privilege concentrations
  - IA-4: Identifier management — identifiers for inactive accounts must also
    be managed
  - CA-7: Continuous monitoring — your ConMon process should have caught this
  - CA-5: POA&M — document this finding immediately


================================================================================
PART 2: IMMEDIATE REMEDIATION STEPS
================================================================================

The remediation process has two parallel tracks: immediate operational
remediation (fix the condition now) and process remediation (fix why it
happened and prevent recurrence).

--- TRACK A: IMMEDIATE OPERATIONAL REMEDIATION (Days 1-7) ---

Step 1: Account Inventory and Validation (Day 1-2)
  - Pull a complete report of all 120 flagged accounts from your identity
    management system or Active Directory (or equivalent directory service).
  - For each of the 120 accounts, validate:
      a) Last logon timestamp (confirm inactivity period)
      b) Account type (user, service, privileged, shared, or system account)
      c) Current account status (enabled/disabled)
      d) Associated individual or function (is the account orphaned?)
      e) Group memberships and privilege level
  - Segregate the 120 accounts into sub-categories:
      - Standard user accounts inactive > 90 days
      - Privileged accounts inactive > 90 days (PRIORITY — disable FIRST)
      - Service accounts (do NOT disable without application impact analysis;
        coordinate with application owners)
      - Accounts for separated employees (disable immediately; may require
        coordination with HR and PS-4 controls)

Step 2: Prioritized Disablement (Day 2-5)
  - PRIORITY 1 (Day 2): Disable all privileged/admin accounts in the inactive
    set first. Privileged account compromise has the highest blast radius.
  - PRIORITY 2 (Day 3-4): Disable all standard user accounts confirmed inactive
    for > 90 days.
  - PRIORITY 3 (Day 4-5): For each service account, perform application impact
    analysis. Coordinate with system/application owners before disabling. If
    a service account is genuinely inactive (no application dependency), disable
    it. If active dependency exists, document the exception and escalate.
  - Document every account disabled, including: account name, account type,
    last logon date, disabled-by, disabled-on timestamp, and ticket/work order
    reference.

Step 3: Notification Protocol (Day 1-3, concurrent)
  - Notify account holders' managers (or HR for separated personnel) before
    disabling, where operationally feasible and where the delay does not extend
    the violation window beyond an acceptable period.
  - For accounts of active employees that are simply unused, give a documented
    24-48 hour notice before disablement per your access control policy.
  - For accounts of separated personnel, disable immediately and notify the
    responsible manager.

Step 4: Exception Handling (Day 3-5)
  - Any account that cannot be immediately disabled (e.g., undocumented service
    accounts with unresolved dependencies) must be:
      a) Logged as an outstanding exception in the POA&M milestone tracker
      b) Assigned an owner responsible for resolving the dependency
      c) Given a hard deadline (suggest 30 days maximum for service account
         resolution) with written approval from the ISSO and System Owner
  - Do NOT simply carry exceptions forward without documentation — each one
    must have an owner and a date.

--- TRACK B: ROOT CAUSE AND PROCESS REMEDIATION (Days 7-60) ---

Step 5: Root Cause Analysis (Day 7-14)
  Determine WHY 120 accounts were not disabled within the 90-day ODV. Common
  root causes include:
    a) Automated account review process was not configured, broke, or was
       disabled
    b) Reports were generated but not acted upon (process failure, not technical)
    c) Account review responsibility was unclear (no defined owner)
    d) Service desk or IT operations lacked an enforced workflow to disable
       flagged accounts
    e) The ODV was documented in the SSP but never operationalized in the
       directory service or IAM tool
    f) Exceptions were granted informally without documentation
  Document the root cause in the POA&M milestone record.

Step 6: Automated Controls Implementation (Day 14-45)
  Implement or repair the automated account management capability required by
  AC-2(1) (which is also a Moderate/High baseline requirement and may itself
  be OTS if automation was absent):
    - Configure Active Directory (or your IAM/IDM platform) to automatically
      flag accounts reaching 75 days of inactivity (15-day warning ahead of
      90-day threshold)
    - Configure automatic disablement at 90 days of inactivity
    - Establish automated notifications to account managers at 60 days and
      75 days
    - Test the automated process end-to-end in a non-production environment
      before deploying to production
    - Document the configuration settings as evidence for future assessments

Step 7: Policy and Procedure Update (Day 14-30)
  - Review and update the Access Control Policy (required by AC-1) to explicitly
    state the 90-day ODV and the automated enforcement mechanism
  - Update the Account Management Procedure to define:
      - Roles responsible for account review and disablement
      - Escalation path for accounts that cannot be automatically disabled
      - Quarterly manual review requirement as a backup to automation
      - Service account exception handling process
  - Ensure procedures reference the specific system and directory service
    configuration, not generic language

Step 8: Training and Awareness (Day 30-60)
  - Brief IT operations and help desk staff on the revised account management
    procedures
  - Document training completion records (supports AT-3)

Step 9: Verification and Evidence Collection (Day 45-60)
  - Run a post-remediation account report showing zero accounts inactive > 90
    days
  - Capture screenshots or exported reports from the directory service
    demonstrating the automated disablement configuration
  - Request a spot-check or mini-assessment from your ISSO or an independent
    reviewer to confirm the control is now Satisfied
  - Retain all remediation evidence in your compliance artifact repository


================================================================================
PART 3: POA&M DOCUMENTATION — REQUIRED FIELDS AND GUIDANCE
================================================================================

The POA&M is mandated by CA-5 (Plan of Action and Milestones) and is a required
component of your authorization package. The following provides the complete
field-by-field guidance for documenting this specific finding.

--- POA&M ENTRY: COMPLETED TEMPLATE ---

+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| FIELD                     | VALUE / GUIDANCE                                  |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| POA&M Item ID             | [Assign sequential ID, e.g., POA&M-2026-042]      |
|                           | Must be unique and traceable to the SAR finding   |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Weakness / Deficiency     | AC-2(3) — Inactive accounts are not being         |
|                           | disabled within the organization-defined value     |
|                           | (ODV) of 90 days of inactivity. Security          |
|                           | assessment identified 120 user accounts with       |
|                           | last logon dates exceeding 120 days, in violation |
|                           | of the documented ODV and SP 800-53 Rev 5          |
|                           | control AC-2(3).                                  |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Source of Discovery       | Security Assessment / Third-Party Assessment       |
|                           | (SCA/3PAO) — [Assessment date] — SAR Finding      |
|                           | Reference: [SAR-Finding-ID]                       |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Control Identifier        | AC-2(3) — Account Management: Disable Accounts    |
|                           | NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5                              |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Risk Level                | MODERATE                                          |
|                           |                                                   |
|                           | Rationale: Dormant accounts with valid credentials|
|                           | present an attack surface for credential-based    |
|                           | intrusion and lateral movement. The 120 affected  |
|                           | accounts exceed the ODV by 30+ days. Risk is      |
|                           | elevated if any accounts belong to privileged     |
|                           | users; re-evaluate as HIGH if privileged accounts |
|                           | are confirmed in the inactive set.                |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Scheduled Completion Date | [Insert date: recommend 60 days from SAR          |
|                           | issuance for full remediation, consistent with    |
|                           | Moderate risk timeline guidance]                  |
|                           |                                                   |
|                           | Note: FedRAMP POA&M guidance requires Moderate    |
|                           | findings to be remediated within 180 days;        |
|                           | FISMA/agency policy may impose tighter deadlines. |
|                           | Recommend 60 days given the straightforward       |
|                           | technical remediation path.                       |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Responsible Parties       | Primary: ISSO (Information System Security        |
|                           | Officer) — [Name / Title]                        |
|                           |                                                   |
|                           | Secondary: System Owner (SO) — [Name / Title]    |
|                           |                                                   |
|                           | Supporting: IT Operations / Identity Management   |
|                           | Team — [Team name or lead name]                  |
|                           |                                                   |
|                           | Approving: Authorizing Official (AO) or AODR     |
|                           | must be informed; AO signature required if risk   |
|                           | acceptance is invoked for any sub-items           |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Resources Required        | - IT Operations staff time: ~40-80 hours          |
|                           |   (account analysis, disablement, testing)        |
|                           | - IAM/Directory service admin access              |
|                           | - AD or IDM platform configuration capability     |
|                           | - Policy/procedure update: ISSO ~8 hours          |
|                           | - No significant new budget anticipated           |
|                           |   (remediation uses existing tooling)             |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Status                    | OPEN [at time of entry]                           |
|                           | Update monthly per ConMon requirements            |
+---------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+

--- MILESTONE SCHEDULE (REQUIRED — Document Each Step with Dates) ---

Milestone 1 — Account Inventory and Triage
  Description: Generate complete report of all 120 inactive accounts. Categorize
               by account type (standard, privileged, service). Identify
               separated employees and orphaned accounts.
  Owner: IT Operations Lead / ISSO
  Target Date: [Day 2 from SAR receipt]
  Evidence: Account inventory spreadsheet with last logon dates, account types,
            and owner identification

Milestone 2 — Privileged Account Disablement
  Description: Disable all privileged/admin accounts in the inactive set.
               Verify disablement via directory query.
  Owner: IT Operations Lead
  Target Date: [Day 3 from SAR receipt]
  Evidence: Screenshot/export from AD or IDM showing disabled status; change
            management ticket reference

Milestone 3 — Standard User Account Disablement
  Description: Disable all standard user accounts inactive > 90 days (excluding
               service accounts pending dependency analysis).
  Owner: IT Operations Lead
  Target Date: [Day 5 from SAR receipt]
  Evidence: Account status report; notification records sent to managers

Milestone 4 — Service Account Dependency Analysis
  Description: Complete application impact analysis for all service accounts in
               the inactive set. Disable those with no active dependency.
               Document exceptions for accounts with unresolved dependencies.
  Owner: Application Owners / IT Operations
  Target Date: [Day 10 from SAR receipt]
  Evidence: Dependency analysis document; exception log with owner assignments

Milestone 5 — Root Cause Analysis Completion
  Description: Document the root cause of the control failure (automation gap,
               process failure, responsibility gap, etc.). Present findings to
               ISSO and System Owner.
  Owner: ISSO
  Target Date: [Day 14 from SAR receipt]
  Evidence: Root Cause Analysis memo signed by ISSO and System Owner

Milestone 6 — Automated Account Review/Disablement Configuration
  Description: Implement or repair automated account inactivity monitoring and
               disablement at the 90-day threshold. Configure warning
               notifications at 60 and 75 days. Test in non-production
               environment before production deployment.
  Owner: IT Operations / IAM Team
  Target Date: [Day 45 from SAR receipt]
  Evidence: Configuration documentation; test results; change management record

Milestone 7 — Policy and Procedure Update
  Description: Update Access Control Policy (AC-1) and Account Management
               Procedure to reflect the 90-day ODV, automated controls, roles
               and responsibilities, and exception handling process.
  Owner: ISSO
  Target Date: [Day 30 from SAR receipt]
  Evidence: Revised policy and procedure documents with version numbers and
            approval signatures

Milestone 8 — Post-Remediation Verification
  Description: Run account activity report demonstrating zero accounts inactive
               > 90 days. ISSO conducts internal spot-check. Retain evidence.
  Owner: ISSO
  Target Date: [Day 55 from SAR receipt]
  Evidence: Post-remediation account report (exported); ISSO verification memo

Milestone 9 — POA&M Closure and ISSO/SO Sign-Off
  Description: ISSO and System Owner review all milestone evidence. Complete
               POA&M closure documentation. Notify AO/AODR of remediation
               completion and submit updated POA&M.
  Owner: ISSO and System Owner
  Target Date: [Day 60 from SAR receipt — Scheduled Completion Date]
  Evidence: Signed POA&M closure memo; updated SSP implementation narrative
            for AC-2(3); evidence package submitted to AO


================================================================================
PART 4: ISSO AND SYSTEM OWNER SIGN-OFF REQUIREMENTS
================================================================================

Federal policy and SP 800-37 Rev 2 establish clear accountability requirements
for POA&M management. The following sign-offs are required:

1. ISSO Responsibilities and Sign-Off:
   - The ISSO is the day-to-day security point of accountability for this
     finding. The ISSO must:
       a) Review and validate the initial finding against the SAR
       b) Approve the POA&M entry before it is submitted to the AO
       c) Verify each milestone is completed and evidence is adequate
       d) Sign the POA&M closure memo when all milestones are complete
       e) Update the SSP AC-2(3) implementation narrative to reflect the
          remediated and now-operational control
       f) Certify the accuracy of the POA&M in monthly ConMon reports
   - ISSO signature is required on:
       - Initial POA&M entry (at submission)
       - Each monthly status update if milestones shift
       - Final POA&M closure memo

2. System Owner (SO) Responsibilities and Sign-Off:
   - The System Owner has ultimate accountability for the system's security
     posture and must:
       a) Acknowledge the finding and concur with the remediation plan
       b) Authorize the resource allocation (staff time, tooling changes)
       c) Accept any residual risk during the remediation window
       d) Co-sign the POA&M closure memo
       e) Ensure the updated Account Management Procedure is formally approved
   - SO signature is required on:
       - Initial POA&M entry concurrence
       - Root Cause Analysis memo
       - POA&M closure memo
       - Updated Access Control Policy (AC-1) if revised

3. Authorizing Official (AO) / AODR Notification:
   - The AO must be notified of the OTS finding and the POA&M entry.
   - If any accounts in the 120-account set are privileged accounts, the AO
     should be explicitly briefed given the elevated risk to the authorization
     boundary.
   - AO signature is NOT required for routine Moderate-risk POA&M entries
     UNLESS: (a) risk acceptance is invoked, (b) the finding affects a critical
     or high-impact component, or (c) agency policy requires AO concurrence.
   - If remediation cannot be completed within the scheduled completion date,
     a formal risk acceptance memorandum requiring AO signature must be
     prepared and executed.

4. Risk Acceptance (if needed):
   If any sub-item (e.g., service accounts with unresolved dependencies) cannot
   be remediated within the standard timeline, a Risk Acceptance must be
   documented separately, including:
     - Specific accounts or account types accepted under the risk acceptance
     - Business justification for the delay
     - Compensating controls in place during the acceptance period
     - Expiration date of the risk acceptance
     - ISSO signature, System Owner signature, and AO signature
   Risk acceptance is NOT a permanent closure of the POA&M item — the item
   remains open and must be reviewed at each ConMon cycle.


================================================================================
PART 5: SSP UPDATE REQUIREMENTS
================================================================================

Once remediation is complete, update the SSP implementation narrative for
AC-2(3) from "Partially Implemented" (or "Planned") to "Implemented" with the
following narrative components:

Control: AC-2(3) — Account Management: Disable Accounts
Implementation Status: Implemented

Implementation Description:
  [System Name] disables user accounts that have been inactive for 90 days or
  more, consistent with the organization-defined value (ODV) documented in the
  Access Control Policy [Policy Name, Version X.X, dated YYYY-MM-DD]. Account
  inactivity is monitored automatically through [tool name, e.g., Active
  Directory / identity management platform]. The system is configured to:
    (1) Send automated notification to the account owner's manager at 60 days
        and 75 days of inactivity;
    (2) Automatically disable the account at 90 days of inactivity.
  Service accounts are reviewed quarterly by [role] to verify continued
  business need. Accounts associated with separated personnel are disabled
  within [X] hours of HR notification per the account termination procedure
  [Procedure Name, Version X.X]. Account disablement is logged and retained
  in [audit log system] for [retention period] per AU-11. The ISSO conducts
  a quarterly review of the account activity report to verify compliance with
  the 90-day ODV.

Responsible Roles:
  - ISSO: Quarterly compliance review; POA&M management
  - IT Operations / IAM Team: Automated system configuration; disablement
    execution
  - System Owner: Policy approval; resource authorization

Evidence / Artifacts:
  - Access Control Policy [version and date]
  - Account Management Procedure [version and date]
  - Directory service / IAM configuration screenshot showing 90-day threshold
  - Quarterly account activity report (most recent)
  - ISSO quarterly review memo


================================================================================
PART 6: CONTINUOUS MONITORING (ConMon) — PREVENTING RECURRENCE
================================================================================

To prevent this finding from recurring at the next assessment cycle:

1. Monthly ConMon Check: Include an account inactivity report in your monthly
   ConMon package. Run a query for accounts approaching 75 days and accounts
   at 90+ days. Review results with IT Operations and document actions taken.

2. Automated Alerting: Configure alerts (via SIEM or IAM platform) when any
   account exceeds 85 days of inactivity — providing a 5-day buffer to take
   manual action before the ODV is breached.

3. Quarterly Manual Review: Supplement automation with a quarterly manual
   account review (tie to CA-7 continuous monitoring tasks) that specifically
   covers:
     - Service accounts
     - Shared accounts
     - Privileged accounts
     - Any accounts excluded from automated review

4. Annual Control Assessment: Include AC-2(3) as a prioritized control in your
   annual assessment (or ConMon-based ongoing assessment) to validate that the
   automated process continues to function correctly.

5. POA&M Monthly Updates: Until this POA&M item is formally closed, provide
   milestone progress updates monthly. Include accounts remaining to be
   disabled, milestones completed, and any new exceptions identified.


================================================================================
PART 7: SUMMARY CHECKLIST — ACTIONS AND DELIVERABLES
================================================================================

IMMEDIATE (Days 1-7):
  [ ] Generate complete inventory of 120 inactive accounts
  [ ] Disable privileged accounts first
  [ ] Disable standard user accounts inactive > 90 days
  [ ] Initiate service account dependency analysis
  [ ] Open POA&M entry with all required fields populated
  [ ] Notify ISSO, System Owner, and AO of the finding and remediation plan

SHORT-TERM (Days 7-30):
  [ ] Complete root cause analysis
  [ ] Update Access Control Policy and Account Management Procedure
  [ ] Assign milestone owners and dates in POA&M
  [ ] Resolve service account exceptions or escalate to formal risk acceptance
  [ ] ISSO and System Owner sign initial POA&M entry

MEDIUM-TERM (Days 30-60):
  [ ] Implement/repair automated account disablement at 90-day threshold
  [ ] Test automation in non-production; deploy to production
  [ ] Complete post-remediation verification report
  [ ] Update SSP AC-2(3) narrative to "Implemented"
  [ ] ISSO and System Owner sign POA&M closure memo
  [ ] Submit updated POA&M and SSP to AO/AODR

ONGOING:
  [ ] Monthly account inactivity ConMon report
  [ ] Quarterly manual account review
  [ ] Annual control assessment of AC-2(3)
  [ ] Monthly POA&M status updates until closure


================================================================================
REFERENCES
================================================================================
  - NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems
    and Organizations — AC-2(3) (September 2020)
  - NIST SP 800-53A Rev 5: Assessing Security and Privacy Controls in
    Information Systems and Organizations (January 2022)
  - NIST SP 800-53B: Control Baselines for Information Systems and Organizations
    (October 2020) — Moderate and High baseline, AC-2(3)
  - NIST SP 800-37 Rev 2: Risk Management Framework for Information Systems
    and Organizations (December 2018) — RMF Steps 5, 6, 7; POA&M management
  - OMB Circular A-130: Managing Information as a Strategic Resource (2016) —
    POA&M and ATO requirements
  - CISA Federal Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation (CDM) Program —
    Account Management/Identity management capabilities
  - FedRAMP POA&M Template and Guidance (if applicable to your authorization)

================================================================================
END OF RESPONSE
================================================================================
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Identifies AC-2(3) as the Automated/Disable Accounts control and explains what it requires
✅Recommends immediately disabling the 120 identified inactive accounts
✅Provides POA&M required fields including control ID, risk level, milestone steps, and completion date
✅Advises fixing the automated account review/enforcement process
✅Notes that OTS findings must be tracked until closure and mentions ISSO/System Owner accountability
NIST SP 800-53 AC-2(3) Remediation Guidance and POA&M Documentation
Finding: AC-2(3) - Account Management | Disable Inactive Accounts
Status: Other Than Satisfied (OTS)
Finding Detail: 120 accounts inactive for 120+ days; ODV (Organization-Defined Value) for disabling is 90 days

================================================================================
SECTION 1: UNDERSTANDING THE CONTROL
================================================================================

AC-2(3) - ACCOUNT MANAGEMENT | DISABLE INACTIVE ACCOUNTS
Control Text: "Disable accounts within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when accounts: (a) Have expired; (b) Are no longer associated with a user or individual; (c) Are in violation of organizational policy; or (d) Have been inactive for [Assignment: organization-defined time period]."

Your SSP states an ODV of 90 days for the inactivity threshold. The assessor identified 120 accounts that have been inactive for 120+ days — a 30+ day gap beyond your stated threshold. This is a clear, measurable control deficiency and must be remediated and tracked as a formal POA&M item.

================================================================================
SECTION 2: IMMEDIATE REMEDIATION STEPS
================================================================================

PHASE 1: EMERGENCY REMEDIATION (Within 30 days — POA&M Milestone 1)

Step 1 — Pull the Full Inactive Account Inventory
- Generate a report from your Identity and Access Management (IAM) system, Active Directory (AD), LDAP, or applicable user provisioning system.
- Export all accounts with last login date, account type (user, service, privileged, shared), owning system, and associated role/privilege level.
- Cross-reference with HR records and access request logs to identify accounts that may belong to separated personnel, contractors who have completed engagements, or temporary accounts that were never closed.

Step 2 — Triage and Categorize the 120 Accounts
Before bulk-disabling, categorize accounts into:
  a) Confirmed orphan accounts — users who have left the organization; disable immediately.
  b) Service/system accounts — do NOT disable without coordination with system owners; may break automated processes.
  c) Accounts belonging to active users who have not logged in — verify with supervisors/system owners before disabling.
  d) Emergency/break-glass accounts — these may legitimately be inactive; document exceptions with risk acceptance.

Step 3 — Disable Non-Exempt Accounts
- For all confirmed orphan and clearly inactive accounts: disable (not delete) accounts in the authoritative identity store.
- Retain account records for audit trail purposes — do not delete until proper off-boarding procedures confirm no data recovery needs.
- Use a standardized naming convention or flag in the IAM system to mark accounts as "Disabled - AC-2(3) Remediation [DATE]."
- Document each account actioned: username, system, date last active, date disabled, who authorized the action.

Step 4 — Notify System and Data Owners
- For accounts where ownership is unclear, send formal notification to supervisors/system owners requesting confirmation of account necessity within 5 business days.
- Document all responses. Accounts with no response after the deadline should be disabled pending further review.

PHASE 2: PROCESS REMEDIATION (Within 60 days — POA&M Milestone 2)

Step 5 — Implement Automated Controls
- Configure your IAM/AD environment to automatically disable accounts after 90 days of inactivity. This is achievable via:
  * Active Directory: Use "Maximum password age" in conjunction with PowerShell scripts or Group Policy; more precisely, implement scheduled scripts querying lastLogonTimestamp attribute against a 90-day threshold.
  * Cloud IAM (AWS IAM, Azure AD, GCP): Use built-in credential reports and lifecycle policies or third-party PAM tools.
  * SIEM/SOAR integration: Create automated alerts at 75 days (warning), 85 days (pre-disable notification), and 90 days (auto-disable action).
- Ensure service accounts are excluded from automatic disabling via a documented exception process with periodic review.

Step 6 — Update Account Management Procedures
- Revise the Account Management Policy and Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) to explicitly codify the 90-day inactivity ODV.
- Establish the process for:
  * Quarterly (at minimum) review of inactive accounts.
  * Automated notification to account owners at 60 days and 80 days of inactivity.
  * Formal exception process for service accounts, break-glass accounts, and emergency access accounts.
  * Off-boarding integration: HR termination triggers must feed directly into the IAM provisioning/de-provisioning workflow within 24 hours.

Step 7 — Privileged Account Focus
- Apply heightened scrutiny to inactive privileged accounts (system administrators, database administrators, network administrators). These represent the highest risk.
- Consider a shorter inactivity threshold (e.g., 45 days) for privileged accounts if not already documented, and update the SSP ODV accordingly.

PHASE 3: VERIFICATION AND EVIDENCE COLLECTION (Within 90 days — POA&M Milestone 3)

Step 8 — Conduct a Full Account Audit
- Run a follow-up report confirming all 120 identified accounts have been either disabled or formally excepted with documented justification.
