- Verify that no new accounts have exceeded the 90-day threshold since remediation began.
- Retain all reports as evidence artifacts.

Step 9 — Update the SSP
- Ensure the System Security Plan accurately reflects the implemented automated controls, the 90-day ODV, and the exception management process.
- If your ODV has changed, update it in the SSP and obtain re-approval from the Authorizing Official (AO) or their designated representative.

================================================================================
SECTION 3: POA&M DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENTS
================================================================================

Under NIST SP 800-53A and OMB Memorandum M-02-01 (as updated), findings assessed as "Other Than Satisfied" must be tracked in a formal Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M). Below is the required documentation structure.

POA&M ENTRY — REQUIRED FIELDS AND RECOMMENDED CONTENT:

1. POA&M ID
   Assign a unique identifier per your organization's POA&M numbering scheme (e.g., POA&M-2026-AC-2(3)-001).

2. Control Identifier
   AC-2(3) — Account Management | Disable Inactive Accounts

3. Weakness / Vulnerability Description
   "The organization's information system does not automatically disable or enforce disabling of inactive user accounts within the organization-defined value (ODV) of 90 days. A security assessment identified 120 user accounts with no recorded logon activity for a period exceeding 120 days (30+ days beyond the stated ODV). The failure to enforce this control creates risk of unauthorized access through dormant credentials that may be compromised, forgotten, or associated with personnel no longer authorized to access the system."

4. Point of Contact (POC)
   Name and title of the system owner or ISSO responsible for remediation.

5. Resources Required
   - IAM administrator time (estimated: X hours for initial account audit and disablement)
   - Potential licensing for PAM/IAM tooling if automated enforcement is not available natively
   - System owner coordination time for service account review
   - Estimated cost: [populate per your resource model]

6. Scheduled Completion Date
   Provide a realistic date — typically 90 to 180 days for full remediation of process-level findings. Immediate disablement of the 120 accounts should be a near-term milestone (within 30 days).

7. Milestones with Completion Dates
   Milestone 1 (30 days): Complete triage and disable all 120 identified inactive accounts. Generate and retain evidence report. Deliverable: Account Disable Evidence Report.
   Milestone 2 (60 days): Implement automated inactivity enforcement controls. Update Account Management SOP and Policy. Deliverable: Updated SOP; screenshot/config export of automated rule.
   Milestone 3 (90 days): Conduct follow-up account audit confirming zero accounts exceed 90-day threshold. Update SSP AC-2(3) control implementation narrative. Deliverable: Clean account audit report; updated SSP section.
   Milestone 4 (180 days): Conduct first quarterly review under the new process to verify sustained compliance. Deliverable: Quarterly account review report.

8. Changes to Milestones
   Leave blank initially. Update with explanations if milestones are missed or adjusted, including new completion dates and justification.

9. Status
   Initial status: "Open — In Progress"
   Update as: "Open — Ongoing," then "Closed" only when all milestones are complete and the authorizing official concurs.

10. Identified Risk
    Risk Level: HIGH
    Rationale: Dormant accounts with valid credentials represent a significant attack surface. Compromised inactive accounts are commonly exploited in lateral movement and privilege escalation attacks. The 120 identified accounts exceed the ODV by 30+ days, indicating a systemic process failure, not an isolated incident.

11. Risk Acceptance / Mitigation Strategy
    "The organization will mitigate this risk through immediate disablement of identified inactive accounts (short-term) and implementation of automated enforcement controls aligned to the 90-day ODV (long-term). No risk acceptance is requested at this time. Compensating controls in place during remediation include [e.g., privileged account session monitoring via SIEM, network segmentation limiting lateral movement, multi-factor authentication on remote access channels]."

12. Supporting Evidence / Artifacts (attach or reference)
    - Inactive account report (pre-remediation) dated [date]
    - Account disable confirmation report dated [date]
    - Updated Account Management SOP version [X.X] dated [date]
    - Automated control configuration screenshot/export dated [date]
    - Follow-up clean account audit report dated [date]
    - Updated SSP AC-2(3) section, version [X.X] dated [date]

================================================================================
SECTION 4: SSP UPDATE REQUIREMENTS
================================================================================

Your SSP implementation statement for AC-2(3) must be updated to accurately reflect:

1. The ODV: Explicitly state "90 days" as the inactivity threshold triggering account disablement.

2. Implementation description: Describe the automated mechanism (e.g., "Active Directory scheduled task queries lastLogonTimestamp attribute nightly; accounts exceeding 90 days of inactivity are automatically disabled. System owners receive automated notification at 75 days.").

3. Exception process: Describe how service accounts, emergency accounts, and other non-standard accounts are handled with appropriate compensating controls and periodic review.

4. Roles and responsibilities: Identify who owns the account review process (typically the ISSO, system administrator, or IAM team) and the frequency of manual oversight review (quarterly recommended).

================================================================================
SECTION 5: ONGOING COMPLIANCE — PREVENTING RECURRENCE
================================================================================

To prevent this finding from recurring at the next assessment:

1. Establish a formal quarterly account review as a standing governance process. Document minutes and sign-off from the system owner.

2. Integrate HR off-boarding with IAM provisioning. Account disablement should be triggered by HR separation events within 24 hours, independent of the 90-day inactivity timer.

3. Define and document service account exceptions explicitly in the SSP. Each exception should have a named owner, a documented business justification, and a periodic review date.

4. Include AC-2(3) compliance metrics in your continuous monitoring program (ConMon). Monthly or quarterly reports on inactive account counts should be reviewed by the ISSO and reported to the AO as part of ongoing authorization reporting.

5. Conduct tabletop or process walkthrough of account management procedures annually to ensure staff are trained on the SOP and automated controls are functioning as intended.

================================================================================
SECTION 6: COMMON PITFALLS TO AVOID
================================================================================

- Do not close the POA&M item until automated controls are operational AND a clean follow-up audit report has been generated. Premature closure will result in a repeat finding.
- Do not disable service accounts without system owner sign-off. Inadvertent disablement of service accounts can cause system outages.
- Do not conflate "disabled" with "deleted." Disabled accounts preserve audit trail integrity; deletion may violate records retention requirements.
- Ensure the 90-day threshold is measured from last logon, not last password change or account creation date — these are different attributes in most directory services.
- Document everything. The assessor will want to see not just that the 120 accounts were disabled, but that a sustainable process now exists to prevent recurrence.

================================================================================

This guidance is based on NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 (AC-2(3)), NIST SP 800-53A Rev. 5 assessment procedures, OMB Circular A-130, and federal POA&M management best practices.
Prompt: We need to implement multi-factor authentication for our federal system. Which specific SP 800-53 Rev 5 controls require MFA, what baselines do they appear in, and what does Executive Order 14028 (Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity) require regarding phishing-resistant MFA?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Identifies IA-2(1) as MFA for privileged accounts and states it appears in Low/Moderate/High baselines
✅Identifies IA-2(2) for non-privileged accounts with correct baseline assignment
✅References EO 14028 or OMB M-22-09 phishing-resistant MFA requirement
✅Explains phishing-resistant MFA (PIV/FIDO2) vs phishing-susceptible (SMS/TOTP) distinction
✅Mentions SP 800-63B or identity assurance levels (AAL) in context of MFA requirements
NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 — Multi-Factor Authentication Requirements for Federal Systems
Executive Order 14028 / OMB M-22-09 Phishing-Resistant MFA Guidance
==============================================================================

OVERVIEW
--------
Implementing multi-factor authentication (MFA) for a federal system involves several
controls within the IA (Identification and Authentication) family of NIST SP 800-53
Rev 5. The specific controls required depend on the system's FIPS 199 impact level
(Low, Moderate, or High) and the user population (privileged vs. non-privileged,
network vs. local access). Overlaid on the baseline requirements, Executive Order 14028
and OMB Memorandum M-22-09 impose additional mandates for phishing-resistant MFA that
go beyond the standard SP 800-53 baseline language.

This response addresses:
  1. The specific IA-2 control enhancements that mandate MFA
  2. Baseline assignments for each enhancement (L/M/H per SP 800-53B)
  3. EO 14028 and OMB M-22-09 phishing-resistant MFA requirements
  4. The PIV/FIDO2 vs. SMS/TOTP distinction
  5. SP 800-63B Authenticator Assurance Level (AAL) alignment
  6. Implementation guidance and SSP narrative considerations


PART 1 — SP 800-53 Rev 5 IA-2 CONTROLS THAT REQUIRE MFA
=========================================================

The base control IA-2 (Identification and Authentication — Organizational Users)
requires that the system uniquely identify and authenticate organizational users. MFA
is mandated through specific enhancements to IA-2. The relevant enhancements are:


1. IA-2(1) — Multi-Factor Authentication to Privileged Accounts
   -------------------------------------------------------------
   Baseline Assignment: LOW | MODERATE | HIGH (all three baselines)

   Control Statement:
   Implement multi-factor authentication for access to privileged accounts.

   Scope: All privileged (administrative) accounts, regardless of access method
   (network, local, or console). "Privileged" means accounts with elevated permissions
   such as system administrators, database administrators, and security administrators.

   Key Implementation Points:
   - Both factors must be from different categories: something you know, something you
     have, or something you are.
   - This enhancement applies to ALL privileged account access — not just network access.
   - Required at the LOW baseline, making it mandatory for every federal information
     system regardless of categorization.
   - Post-EO 14028, the acceptable authenticator types are further constrained (see
     Part 3 below).

   SP 800-53B Baseline: Required at Low, Moderate, and High.


2. IA-2(2) — Multi-Factor Authentication to Non-Privileged Accounts
   -----------------------------------------------------------------
   Baseline Assignment: LOW | MODERATE | HIGH (all three baselines)

   Control Statement:
   Implement multi-factor authentication for access to non-privileged accounts.

   Scope: All non-privileged (standard user) accounts. In the Rev 5 version of this
   enhancement, the requirement applies broadly — the historical limitation to "network
   access" from Rev 4 has been revised so that Rev 5 focuses on the account type
   rather than the access vector.

   Key Implementation Points:
   - MFA applies to the full population of organizational users, not just administrators.
   - Systems must ensure that all users authenticate with at least two factors prior to
     gaining access.
   - Required at the LOW baseline in SP 800-53 Rev 5, representing a significant
     strengthening from Rev 4 where IA-2(2) was only required at Moderate and above.

   SP 800-53B Baseline: Required at Low, Moderate, and High.


3. IA-2(6) — Access to Accounts — Separate Device
   ------------------------------------------------
   Baseline Assignment: HIGH only

   Control Statement:
   Implement multi-factor authentication for [Selection: local; network] access to
   [Selection: privileged accounts; non-privileged accounts] such that:
     (a) One of the factors is provided by a device separate from the system gaining
         access; and
     (b) The device meets [Assignment: organization-defined strength of mechanism
         requirements].

   Scope: High-impact systems only. This enhancement goes beyond IA-2(1) and IA-2(2)
   by requiring that at least one authentication factor be provided by a physically
   separate device — typically a hardware token, smart card, or mobile authenticator
   app on a separate device.

   Key Implementation Points:
   - The "separate device" requirement prevents an attacker who has compromised the
     workstation from also controlling the second factor.
   - PIV/CAC cards (smart cards) inherently satisfy this requirement because the
     cryptographic operations occur on the card itself, separate from the workstation.
   - FIDO2 hardware security keys (e.g., YubiKey) also satisfy this requirement.
   - Organization-defined strength of mechanism requirements should reference NIST SP
     800-63B Authenticator Assurance Level 3 (AAL3) for High-impact systems.

   SP 800-53B Baseline: Required at High only. Organizations with Moderate systems
   should consider this enhancement based on risk; it is also a strong candidate for
   supplemental control selection when threat modeling indicates insider threat or
   advanced persistent threat (APT) actors.


4. IA-2(8) — Access to Accounts — Replay Resistant
   -------------------------------------------------
   Baseline Assignment: MODERATE | HIGH

   Control Statement:
   Implement replay-resistant authentication mechanisms for access to [Selection:
   privileged accounts; non-privileged accounts].

   Scope: Moderate and High impact systems. This enhancement requires that the
   authentication mechanism cannot be successfully replayed by an adversary who
   captures the authentication exchange.

   Key Implementation Points:
   - Replay resistance is satisfied by authenticators that use nonces, timestamps, or
     cryptographic challenge-response protocols — meaning each authentication exchange
     produces a unique, time-limited credential.
   - PIV/CAC cards and FIDO2 keys inherently provide replay resistance through
     asymmetric cryptography.
   - One-time password (OTP) tokens (hardware TOTP) provide partial replay resistance
     because the OTP is time-limited, but they are susceptible to real-time phishing
     (attacker intercepts and immediately replays the OTP). See Part 3.
   - Kerberos with pre-authentication, TLS mutual authentication, and SAML assertions
     with short validity windows also satisfy replay resistance in specific deployment
     contexts.
   - SMS-based OTP does NOT provide meaningful replay resistance — it is vulnerable
     to SIM-swapping and SS7 protocol attacks, and it is not considered replay-
     resistant in the context of this control.

   SP 800-53B Baseline: Required at Moderate and High. Not included in Low baseline
   but may be selected as a supplemental control based on risk.


5. IA-2(12) — Acceptance of PIV Credentials
   ------------------------------------------
   Baseline Assignment: LOW | MODERATE | HIGH (all three baselines)

   Control Statement:
   Accept and electronically verify Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credentials
   from other federal agencies.

   Scope: All federal information systems. This control flows from Homeland Security
   Presidential Directive 12 (HSPD-12), which mandates government-wide use of PIV
   credentials (FIPS 201 compliant), and OMB M-11-11, which required all federal
   agencies to accept PIV credentials from employees of other agencies.

   Key Implementation Points:
   - The system must be technically capable of validating PIV credentials — this
     requires integrating with a PIV-capable identity provider or implementing a
     Certificate Validation Authority (CVA).
   - PIV validation involves checking:
       (a) Certificate path validation against the Federal PKI (FPKI) trust anchor
       (b) Certificate revocation (OCSP or CRL checks against the issuing CA)
       (c) Cardholder Unique Identifier (CHUID) validation or Card Authentication
           Certificate (CAC) validation
   - For cloud-hosted or SaaS systems, PIV acceptance is typically implemented through
     an agency identity provider (IdP) that federates PIV authentication (e.g., using
     SAML 2.0 or OIDC) rather than requiring direct PIV card integration at the
     application layer.
   - IA-2(12) is a universally required control: it appears in the Low, Moderate, and
     High baselines and cannot be scoped out for federal systems.

   SP 800-53B Baseline: Required at Low, Moderate, and High.


SUMMARY TABLE — MFA-Related IA-2 Enhancements and Baseline Assignments
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Control     | Description                          | Low | Moderate | High
------------|--------------------------------------|-----|----------|-----
IA-2        | Identification & Authentication      |  Y  |    Y     |  Y
IA-2(1)     | MFA — Privileged Accounts            |  Y  |    Y     |  Y
IA-2(2)     | MFA — Non-Privileged Accounts        |  Y  |    Y     |  Y
IA-2(6)     | MFA — Separate Device                |  N  |    N     |  Y
IA-2(8)     | Replay-Resistant Authentication      |  N  |    Y     |  Y
IA-2(12)    | Acceptance of PIV Credentials        |  Y  |    Y     |  Y

Note: "Y" = included in SP 800-53B baseline; "N" = not in baseline (may be added
through risk-based supplementation or overlay requirements).

Additional Supporting Controls (not MFA mandates but directly relevant):
  - IA-5(2) [M/H] — PKI-Based Authentication: requirements for PKI authenticators,
    certificate validation, key storage (must be hardware-protected for AAL3).
  - IA-11 [M/H] — Re-authentication: requires re-authentication after defined periods
    of inactivity or when roles/privileges change.
  - IA-12 [M/H] — Identity Proofing: requires in-person or supervised remote identity
    proofing at IAL2 or IAL3 (per SP 800-63A) before issuing authenticators.


PART 2 — RELATED AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT CONTROL: IA-5
========================================================

While IA-2 establishes the MFA requirement, IA-5 (Authenticator Management) governs
how authenticators are issued, protected, revoked, and maintained. Key enhancements:

IA-5(2) — PKI-Based Authentication [Moderate/High]
   Requires:
   (a) Validation of certificates through construction and verification of a
       certification path to an accepted trust anchor including checking certificate
       status information.
   (b) Enforcement of authorized access to the private key.
   (c) Mapping of the authenticated identity to the account of the individual or group.
   (d) Implementation of a local cache of revocation data to support path discovery
       and validation for systems unable to access revocation information via the network.

This control is directly satisfied by PIV/CAC deployment. The private key must reside
in a hardware-protected cryptographic module (the PIV card itself, satisfying FIPS
140-3 Level 2 or higher requirements).

IA-5(1) — Password-Based Authentication [Low/Moderate/High]
   For systems that still use passwords as one factor, SP 800-63B Section 5 guidance
   applies:
   - Minimum 8 characters for memorized secrets (federal agencies typically set 15+)
   - No mandatory composition rules (per SP 800-63B — complexity requirements are
     counterproductive to security)
   - Check passwords against known compromised password lists
   - No mandatory periodic rotation unless compromise is suspected
   - Support paste functionality in password fields


PART 3 — EXECUTIVE ORDER 14028 AND OMB M-22-09 PHISHING-RESISTANT MFA REQUIREMENTS
====================================================================================

Executive Order 14028, "Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity" (May 12, 2021), Section 3,
mandates that federal agencies adopt a Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) and modernize their
cybersecurity practices, including MFA. The implementing guidance is OMB Memorandum
M-22-09, "Moving the U.S. Government Toward Zero Trust Cybersecurity Principles"
(January 26, 2022).


Key EO 14028 Directives for MFA:
---------------------------------
Section 3(b)(i): Within 180 days, agencies must employ multi-factor authentication
where the authentication is resistant to phishing attacks.

The EO explicitly distinguishes between "multi-factor authentication" (the general
capability) and "phishing-resistant multi-factor authentication" (the specific security
property required for federal systems).


OMB M-22-09 Phishing-Resistant MFA Mandate:
--------------------------------------------
M-22-09 establishes the following specific requirements:

1. ALL federal staff, contractors, and partners must use phishing-resistant MFA.

2. Phishing-resistant MFA is defined as authentication that uses cryptographic
   techniques that prevent the authenticator output from being used to authenticate
   to a different party (i.e., the authenticator is bound to the specific service
   and cannot be replayed to a fraudulent site).

3. Deadline: Agencies must meet the MFA requirements within the fiscal year
   2024 milestones defined in the memorandum.

4. Approved phishing-resistant MFA methods (per M-22-09):
   APPROVED:
   - PIV/CAC smart cards (FIPS 201 / HSPD-12)
   - FIDO2 / WebAuthn hardware security keys (e.g., YubiKey, Google Titan Key)
   - Platform authenticators using FIDO2 (e.g., Windows Hello for Business with
     hardware TPM, Apple Face ID/Touch ID tied to Passkeys)

   NOT ACCEPTABLE as phishing-resistant MFA (even though they are "MFA"):
   - SMS-based one-time passwords (OTP) — vulnerable to SIM swapping, SS7 attacks,
     and real-time adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing
   - Voice call OTP — same vulnerabilities as SMS
   - Email-based OTP — susceptible to account compromise and AiTM phishing
   - TOTP/HOTP authenticator apps (e.g., Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator
     in OTP mode) — the 6-digit code can be intercepted and replayed by AiTM tools
     (e.g., Evilginx2, Modlishka) in real time; NOT phishing-resistant
   - Push notifications without number matching — susceptible to MFA fatigue attacks

5. Internal-facing systems: Even internal/intranet systems must use phishing-resistant
   MFA for agency staff. M-22-09 does not permit exceptions for internal applications.

6. External-facing systems: Agencies must offer phishing-resistant MFA as an option
   to members of the public where technically feasible.


CISA Guidance — Implementing Phishing-Resistant MFA (2022):
------------------------------------------------------------
CISA has issued accompanying guidance reinforcing the M-22-09 requirements:
- Tier 1 (Preferred): Hardware PIV/CAC or FIDO2 hardware security keys
- Tier 2 (Acceptable for limited use cases): FIDO2 platform authenticators with
  biometric binding on government-managed devices
- NOT Acceptable: Any OTP-based method (SMS, TOTP, email) for systems subject to
  M-22-09

Key Distinction — Why TOTP Fails the Phishing-Resistant Test:
TOTP codes (the 6-digit codes from apps like Google Authenticator) are:
  - Not bound to the relying party (RP) — the same code could be submitted to a
    fraudulent site
  - Interceptable by real-time AiTM phishing proxies — the attacker captures the
    TOTP and immediately forwards it to the legitimate service
  - Not providing replay resistance in the M-22-09 / EO 14028 sense

PIV and FIDO2 are phishing-resistant because:
  - The cryptographic signature is bound to the specific origin/relying party
    (the domain or service). A signature generated for agency.gov cannot be used
    to authenticate to evil-agency.gov
  - The private key never leaves the hardware device
  - Authentication is a challenge-response protocol — there is no replayable
    shared secret


PART 4 — SP 800-63B AUTHENTICATOR ASSURANCE LEVELS (AAL) ALIGNMENT
====================================================================

NIST SP 800-63B (Digital Identity Guidelines — Authentication and Lifecycle Management,
Rev 3, June 2017; Rev 4 Draft under development) defines three Authenticator Assurance
Levels. Federal systems must map to the appropriate AAL based on impact level and risk:

AAL1 (Single Factor):
  - Not sufficient for any federal system covered by IA-2(1) or IA-2(2)
  - Suitable only for low-risk, public-facing informational applications without
    personal data

AAL2 (Multi-Factor, not necessarily phishing-resistant):
  - Requires two or more authentication factors
  - Approved authenticators include: OTP hardware/software tokens, PIV, FIDO2,
    look-up secrets, memorized secret + OTP
  - Satisfies SP 800-53 IA-2(1) and IA-2(2) at baseline level
  - Does NOT satisfy M-22-09 phishing-resistant MFA requirement if using TOTP/OTP
  - Appropriate for: Moderate impact systems not handling particularly sensitive data

AAL3 (Hardware-based, Phishing-Resistant, Verifier Impersonation Resistant):
  - Requires a hardware cryptographic authenticator
  - Requires verifier impersonation resistance (the authenticator is bound to the
    specific RP)
  - Approved authenticators: PIV/CAC, FIDO2 hardware security keys (roaming
    authenticators), FIDO2 platform authenticators with hardware TPM at specified
    assurance level
  - Satisfies M-22-09 phishing-resistant MFA requirement
  - Required for: High-impact systems, systems processing sensitive PII, privileged
    access to High systems, any system subject to IA-2(6)

Mapping Summary:
  System Baseline | Minimum AAL | Phishing-Resistant Required?
  Low             | AAL2        | Yes (per M-22-09, all federal systems)
  Moderate        | AAL2        | Yes (per M-22-09)
  High            | AAL3        | Yes (per M-22-09 and IA-2(6) requirement)

Note: M-22-09 effectively raises the floor for all federal systems to phishing-resistant
AAL2 or AAL3, superseding the plain AAL2 baseline reading of IA-2(1)/(2).


PART 5 — PIV vs. FIDO2 vs. SMS/TOTP: PRACTICAL COMPARISON
===========================================================

Criterion               | PIV/CAC        | FIDO2 HW Key   | TOTP App       | SMS OTP
------------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|--------
Phishing-resistant      | YES            | YES            | NO             | NO
Replay-resistant        | YES            | YES            | Partial        | NO
Verifier impersonation  | YES (origin    | YES (origin    | NO             | NO
resistant               |  bound)        |  bound)        |                |
Hardware-protected key  | YES (FIPS 140) | YES            | NO             | NO
Federal mandate (M-22)  | APPROVED       | APPROVED       | NOT APPROVED   | NOT APPROVED
HSPD-12 compliant       | YES            | NO*            | NO             | NO
Suitable for PIV-req.   | YES            | NO             | NO             | NO
systems (IA-2(12))      |                |                |                |
Works without network   | YES (offline   | YES (offline   | YES (offline   | NO
                        |  card reader)  |  USB/NFC)      |  TOTP)         |
Phishing sim. training  | Immune         | Immune         | Vulnerable     | Vulnerable

*FIDO2 keys are not HSPD-12/FIPS 201 PIV credentials, but they satisfy phishing-
resistant MFA per M-22-09. For systems requiring PIV per HSPD-12 and IA-2(12), only
PIV/CAC satisfies both requirements simultaneously. Agencies may use FIDO2 as a
supplemental or alternative phishing-resistant method where PIV is not technically
feasible (e.g., mobile devices, developer APIs).


PART 6 — IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE FOR FEDERAL SYSTEMS
=====================================================

Step 1: Determine System Impact Level
  - Conduct FIPS 199 categorization and document in the system categorization form
  - High-impact systems must implement IA-2(1), IA-2(2), IA-2(6), IA-2(8), IA-2(12)
  - Moderate systems must implement IA-2(1), IA-2(2), IA-2(8), IA-2(12)
  - Low systems must implement IA-2(1), IA-2(2), IA-2(12)
  - All systems, regardless of baseline, must implement phishing-resistant MFA per M-22-09

Step 2: Select Phishing-Resistant Authenticator(s)
  Option A — PIV/CAC (preferred for federal employees and contractors):
    - Deploy PIV card readers at workstations
    - Integrate with agency Active Directory/LDAP using PKINIT or smart card logon
    - For web applications, implement PIV authentication via agency IdP (e.g., GSA
      Login.gov, agency ICAM solution)
    - Validate certificates against FPKI trust anchors
    - Implement OCSP checking for real-time revocation validation (configure caching
      for continuity of operations)

  Option B — FIDO2 Hardware Security Keys:
    - Deploy WebAuthn/FIDO2 at the application or IdP layer
    - Register keys to user accounts with attestation verification
    - Enforce enterprise-attestation or AAL3-level authenticators via RP policy
    - Suitable for developer workflows, API access, cloud services, and mobile
    - Document in SSP as supplemental phishing-resistant MFA method

  Option C — Hybrid (PIV primary, FIDO2 fallback):
    - PIV for primary workstation authentication
    - FIDO2 for remote access, cloud services, and mobile scenarios
    - Centralize both through agency IdP using SAML 2.0 or OIDC federation

Step 3: Address Legacy Systems
  - Legacy systems that cannot natively support PIV or FIDO2 must use a PIV-enabled
    IdP or proxy to front-end the authentication (e.g., Shibboleth, Okta, Azure AD
    with PIV federation)
  - Document compensating controls in the SSP if a short-term legacy exception exists
  - Include a Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) item with a specific remediation
    date for any system not yet meeting phishing-resistant MFA requirements

Step 4: Document in the SSP (System Security Plan)
  For each MFA control, the SSP narrative should address:

  IA-2(1) Implementation Statement (example structure):
    "The [System Name] implements multi-factor authentication for all privileged
    accounts using PIV/CAC smart cards (FIPS 201) as the primary authentication
    mechanism, supplemented by FIDO2 hardware security keys for privileged access
    from mobile or remote environments where PIV card readers are unavailable.
    Privileged account authentication is managed through [Agency IdP Name] using
    [SAML 2.0 / PKINIT / smart card logon]. Certificate validation is performed
    against the Federal PKI Common Policy Root CA. OCSP responses are cached locally
    for [X hours] to support operations during network outages. This implementation
    satisfies AAL3 requirements per NIST SP 800-63B and phishing-resistant MFA
    requirements per OMB M-22-09."

Step 5: Assessment Considerations (SP 800-53A)
  Assessors will examine, interview, and test:
  - Examine: SSP narratives, PIV/FIDO2 configuration documentation, IdP configuration,
    certificate validation policies, identity proofing records (IA-12)
  - Interview: ISSO, system administrator, identity management personnel
  - Test: Attempt authentication without second factor (should fail); verify PIV
    certificate validation (including revocation checking); test FIDO2 attestation
    enforcement; verify that SMS/TOTP methods are not enabled for accounts subject
    to phishing-resistant MFA requirements


PART 7 — COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST SUMMARY
======================================

Requirement                                         | Source        | All Baselines?
----------------------------------------------------|---------------|---------------
Implement MFA for privileged accounts               | IA-2(1)       | Yes (L/M/H)
Implement MFA for non-privileged accounts           | IA-2(2)       | Yes (L/M/H)
Accept PIV credentials from other agencies          | IA-2(12)      | Yes (L/M/H)
Implement replay-resistant authentication           | IA-2(8)       | M/H only
Implement MFA via separate device                   | IA-2(6)       | H only
MFA must be phishing-resistant (PIV/FIDO2)          | EO 14028 /    | Yes — ALL
                                                    | OMB M-22-09   | federal systems
SMS and TOTP OTP are NOT acceptable                 | CISA / M-22-09| Yes — prohibited
PIV certificates must be validated via FPKI         | IA-5(2)       | M/H
Identity proofing at IAL2/IAL3 before issuing creds | IA-12         | M/H
High-impact systems require AAL3 authenticators     | SP 800-63B    | H only

========================

   Organizations (September 2020, updated December 2020)
   Controls: IA-2, IA-2(1), IA-2(2), IA-2(6), IA-2(8), IA-2(12), IA-5, IA-5(2)

Response generated using NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Compliance Skill
Skill: nist-800-53 | Eval: eval-118 | Date: 2026-05-07
==============================================================================
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4/5 assertions passed (80%)
✅Identifies IA-2(1) as MFA for privileged accounts and states it appears in Low/Moderate/High baselines
❌Identifies IA-2(2) for non-privileged accounts with correct baseline assignment
✅References EO 14028 or OMB M-22-09 phishing-resistant MFA requirement
✅Explains phishing-resistant MFA (PIV/FIDO2) vs phishing-susceptible (SMS/TOTP) distinction
✅Mentions SP 800-63B or identity assurance levels (AAL) in context of MFA requirements
NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Multi-Factor Authentication Requirements and Executive Order 14028
=====================================================================================

OVERVIEW
--------
Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is a critical security control for federal systems. NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 addresses MFA requirements primarily within the Identification and Authentication (IA) control family, with additional references in Access Control (AC) and other families. Executive Order 14028 strengthens these requirements with specific mandates for phishing-resistant MFA across federal systems.


PART 1: SP 800-53 REV 5 CONTROLS REQUIRING MFA
-----------------------------------------------

1. IA-2: Identification and Authentication (Organizational Users)
   The primary MFA control. Requires that the information system uniquely identifies and authenticates organizational users.

   Key enhancement sub-controls requiring MFA:

   IA-2(1) - Multi-Factor Authentication to Privileged Accounts
   - Requires MFA for access to privileged accounts (e.g., system administrators, root accounts)
   - Baseline applicability: LOW, MODERATE, HIGH
   - One of the most universally required MFA controls across all baselines

   IA-2(2) - Multi-Factor Authentication to Non-Privileged Accounts
   - Requires MFA for access to non-privileged accounts
   - Baseline applicability: MODERATE, HIGH
   - Not required in the LOW baseline but strongly recommended

   IA-2(6) - Access to Accounts — Separate Device
   - Requires MFA using a device separate from the system gaining access
   - Baseline applicability: Not in any baseline by default (organization-defined)
   - Often implemented in HIGH environments and by policy overlay

   IA-2(8) - Phishing-Resistant Multi-Factor Authentication to Privileged Accounts
   - Requires phishing-resistant MFA specifically for privileged accounts
   - Phishing-resistant methods include: PIV/CAC smart cards, FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys
   - Baseline applicability: HIGH (introduced prominently in Rev 5)
   - Directly supports EO 14028 mandates

   IA-2(12) - Acceptance of PIV Credentials
   - Requires systems to accept Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credentials
   - PIV credentials inherently provide MFA (something you have + something you know)
   - Baseline applicability: LOW, MODERATE, HIGH
   - Applies to all federal systems — aligns with HSPD-12 requirements

   IA-2(13) - Out-of-Band Authentication
   - Requires out-of-band authentication for non-privileged accounts in certain scenarios
   - Baseline applicability: Organization-defined; common in HIGH environments

2. IA-5: Authenticator Management
   While not exclusively an MFA control, IA-5 governs the management of authenticators used in MFA solutions.

   IA-5(1) - Password-Based Authentication
   - Sets minimum complexity and management requirements for passwords used as one factor
   - Baseline applicability: LOW, MODERATE, HIGH

   IA-5(2) - Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)-Based Authentication
   - Requires validation of PKI certificates, directly supports smart card/PIV MFA
   - Baseline applicability: MODERATE, HIGH

   IA-5(6) - Protection of Authenticators
   - Requires protection of authenticators (tokens, smart cards, etc.)
   - Baseline applicability: Organization-defined

   IA-5(7) - No Embedded Unencrypted Static Authenticators
   - Prevents embedding of static credentials, supports MFA integrity
   - Baseline applicability: MODERATE, HIGH

3. IA-8: Identification and Authentication (Non-Organizational Users)
   Addresses MFA requirements for external users, contractors, and partners.

   IA-8(1) - Acceptance of PIV Credentials from Other Agencies
   - Requires acceptance of PIV credentials from other federal agencies
   - Baseline applicability: LOW, MODERATE, HIGH

   IA-8(2) - Acceptance of External Authenticators
   - Requires acceptance of external authenticators meeting NIST SP 800-63 standards
   - Baseline applicability: LOW, MODERATE, HIGH

   IA-8(4) - Use of Defined Profiles
   - Systems must use profiles as defined in SP 800-63 for identity assurance
   - Baseline applicability: LOW, MODERATE, HIGH

4. AC-17: Remote Access
   AC-17(3) - Managed Access Control Points
   - Requires the use of MFA for remote access sessions
   - Baseline applicability: MODERATE, HIGH
   - Critical for VPN and remote desktop scenarios

5. AC-2: Account Management and AC-3: Access Enforcement
   These controls, while not explicitly mandating MFA, require implementation of mechanisms (including MFA) to enforce least privilege and access controls, especially for privileged accounts.


PART 2: BASELINE APPLICABILITY SUMMARY TABLE
--------------------------------------------

Control             | LOW | MODERATE | HIGH | Notes
--------------------|-----|----------|------|-------------------------------
IA-2(1)             |  X  |    X     |  X   | MFA for privileged accounts
IA-2(2)             |     |    X     |  X   | MFA for non-privileged accounts
IA-2(6)             |     |          |      | Org-defined, often HIGH
IA-2(8)             |     |          |  X   | Phishing-resistant MFA, privileged
IA-2(12)            |  X  |    X     |  X   | PIV credential acceptance
IA-5(2)             |     |    X     |  X   | PKI-based authentication
IA-8(1)             |  X  |    X     |  X   | PIV from other agencies
IA-8(2)             |  X  |    X     |  X   | External authenticators (SP 800-63)
AC-17(3)            |     |    X     |  X   | MFA for remote access

Note: LOW baseline systems still require MFA for privileged accounts (IA-2(1)) and PIV
acceptance (IA-2(12)), making MFA universally applicable across all federal systems.


PART 3: EXECUTIVE ORDER 14028 REQUIREMENTS FOR PHISHING-RESISTANT MFA
----------------------------------------------------------------------

Executive Order 14028, "Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity," signed May 12, 2021, established sweeping cybersecurity mandates for the federal government. Key MFA-related requirements include:

3.1 Core MFA Mandate (Section 3 — Modernizing Federal Government Cybersecurity)
   - Section 3(b)(ii): Federal agencies must adopt multi-factor authentication within 180 days of the order
   - MFA must be implemented across all federal information systems, including both privileged and non-privileged accounts
   - The mandate applies to cloud services, on-premises systems, and hybrid environments

3.2 Phishing-Resistant MFA Specifically
   EO 14028 does not use the exact phrase "phishing-resistant" directly in every clause, but the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) and CISA subsequently clarified through implementing guidance (OMB M-22-09) that:

   OMB Memorandum M-22-09 (Zero Trust Architecture Strategy, January 2022) — implementing EO 14028:
   - Requires agencies to use phishing-resistant MFA methods, specifically:
     * PIV/CAC (Personal Identity Verification / Common Access Card) smart cards
     * FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware security keys
   - Explicitly prohibits reliance on weaker MFA methods (SMS OTP, voice call OTPs, push notifications without number matching) as the sole MFA mechanism for high-value assets
   - Agencies must achieve phishing-resistant MFA for:
     * All privileged users (system admins, privileged access workstations)
     * All federal employees accessing federal systems
     * Where technically feasible, all non-privileged users

3.3 Phishing-Resistant Methods Defined
   Under EO 14028 / OMB M-22-09 / CISA guidance, phishing-resistant MFA is defined as:
   - Authentication that cryptographically verifies both the authenticator and the relying party (the system being accessed)
   - Resistant to real-time phishing attacks (adversary-in-the-middle attacks)
   - Acceptable methods:
     * PIV/CAC smart cards (FIPS 201-compliant)
             - Hardware-based, certificate-backed
        - Satisfies AAL3 under SP 800-63B
     * FIDO2/WebAuthn with hardware security keys (e.g., YubiKey)
        - Cryptographic binding to the origin/relying party
        - Resistant to credential theft and real-time phishing
        - Satisfies AAL2 or AAL3 depending on implementation
   - Non-phishing-resistant methods (acceptable for limited use cases only):
     * TOTP (time-based one-time passwords via authenticator apps) — susceptible to real-time phishing
     * SMS/voice OTP — susceptible to SIM swapping and real-time phishing

3.4 CISA Binding Operational Directives
   CISA BOD 22-02 and subsequent guidance under EO 14028 authority:
   - Agencies must enumerate all internet-accessible systems and enforce MFA
   - Specific timelines were established for remediation of systems lacking MFA
   - Privileged accounts must use phishing-resistant MFA first, then extended to all users

3.5 Timeline and Accountability
   - 60 days: Agencies to document MFA deployment plans
   - 180 days: Implement MFA across all systems
   - 1 year: Achieve phishing-resistant MFA for privileged users
   - Ongoing: OMB oversight and agency reporting requirements


PART 4: INTEGRATION OF SP 800-53 AND EO 14028 FOR FEDERAL SYSTEM IMPLEMENTATION
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

4.1 Alignment
   - IA-2(8) (Phishing-Resistant MFA for Privileged Accounts) directly codifies EO 14028 / M-22-09 requirements in SP 800-53 Rev 5 control language
   - IA-2(12) (PIV Acceptance) supports the PIV-based phishing-resistant pathway
   - SP 800-63B AAL2/AAL3 provides the technical standards underpinning both SP 800-53 and EO 14028 guidance

4.2 Implementation Recommendations for Federal System MFA
   Step 1: Identify all privileged accounts — implement phishing-resistant MFA immediately (IA-2(1), IA-2(8))
   Step 2: Enable PIV/CAC acceptance for all organizational users (IA-2(12))
   Step 3: Extend MFA to all non-privileged accounts (IA-2(2))
   Step 4: Enforce MFA for all remote access sessions (AC-17(3))
   Step 5: Manage and protect authenticators per IA-5 requirements
   Step 6: Accept external credentials per IA-8 for cross-agency/partner access
   Step 7: Document in SSP and validate during ATO process

4.3 Key Supporting Standards
   - NIST SP 800-63B: Authentication and Lifecycle Management (defines AAL1/2/3)
   - FIPS 201-3: PIV standard for federal employees and contractors
   - NIST SP 800-157: Guidelines for Derived PIV Credentials (mobile devices)
   - OMB M-22-09: Federal Zero Trust Architecture Strategy
   - CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model: Implementation guidance


SUMMARY
-------
For federal systems:

1. All baselines (LOW, MODERATE, HIGH): IA-2(1) requires MFA for privileged accounts; IA-2(12) requires PIV acceptance universally.

2. MODERATE and HIGH baselines additionally require: IA-2(2) for non-privileged accounts, IA-5(2) for PKI authentication, AC-17(3) for remote access MFA.

3. HIGH baseline additionally requires: IA-2(8) for phishing-resistant MFA on privileged accounts.

4. EO 14028 / OMB M-22-09 mandate phishing-resistant MFA (PIV/CAC or FIDO2/WebAuthn) for all privileged users and, where feasible, all federal employees — going beyond baseline checkbox compliance to require cryptographically strong, origin-binding authentication methods that defeat real-time phishing and adversary-in-the-middle attacks.

Agencies that implement PIV/CAC or FIDO2 hardware keys for all users simultaneously satisfy both the SP 800-53 control requirements and the EO 14028 / M-22-09 phishing-resistant MFA mandates.
Prompt: We are writing SSP control narratives for our Moderate impact federal system. Please write a complete implementation narrative for SC-8(1) — Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity / Cryptographic Protection — that would satisfy a 3PAO assessor.
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Produces a structured SSP narrative format (not just general guidance)
✅Specifies TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3 as the required protocol and notes TLS 1.0/1.1 are prohibited
✅Mentions FIPS 140-2 or FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules
✅Includes responsible roles (ISSO, system administrator, or equivalent)
✅References related controls SC-13 (cryptographic protection) and/or SC-28(1) (data at rest)
================================================================================
SYSTEM SECURITY PLAN (SSP) CONTROL NARRATIVE
NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 — SC-8(1): Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity |
Cryptographic Protection
================================================================================

SYSTEM NAME:        [Organization Federal Information System Name]
SYSTEM IDENTIFIER:  [System Identifier / FISMA UID]
IMPACT LEVEL:       Moderate (FIPS 199 High-Water Mark)
SSP VERSION:        1.0
DATE:               2026-05-07
PREPARED BY:        Information System Security Officer (ISSO)
REVIEWED BY:        System Owner / Authorizing Official Designated Representative

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CONTROL INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Control ID:           SC-8(1)
Control Family:       System and Communications Protection (SC)
Control Title:        Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity | Cryptographic
                      Protection
SP 800-53B Baseline:  Moderate, High
Control Type:         System-Specific (see Control Designation section)
Enhancement Of:       SC-8 — Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity
Related Controls:     SC-8, SC-12, SC-13, SC-17, SC-28(1), AC-17, IA-3, IA-8,
                      MA-4, SI-8

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CONTROL STATEMENT (NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SC-8:    Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure
         of information and to detect changes to information during transmission
         unless otherwise protected by [Assignment: organization-defined
         alternative physical safeguards].

SC-8(1): Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure
         of information and detect changes to information during transmission.

Note: SC-8(1) removes the alternative of physical safeguards available in the
base SC-8 control. For Moderate impact systems, cryptographic protection is
mandatory for all data-in-transit — physical alternatives are not acceptable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPLEMENTATION STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  [X] Implemented
  [ ] Partially Implemented
  [ ] Planned
  [ ] Not Applicable
  [ ] Alternative Implementation

Implementation Date:     [Date]
Planned Completion Date: N/A

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CONTROL DESIGNATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Designation:  System-Specific / Hybrid

Rationale: The base cryptographic policy (SC-8(1) policy and standards) is
inherited as a Common Control from the organization's enterprise Information
Security Policy and the Network Security Standards document maintained by the
Enterprise Security Operations Center (ESOC). However, specific configuration
and enforcement of cryptographic protocols within the application layer, API
endpoints, and inter-service communications are System-Specific responsibilities
implemented and maintained by the system team.

  Common (Inherited) Component:
    - Enterprise TLS configuration standards and approved cipher suite lists
      (inherited from [Organization] Enterprise SC-8 Common Control)
    - Enterprise PKI / Certificate Authority infrastructure (inherited from
      SC-12, SC-17 common control provider)
    - Boundary protection device TLS termination policy (inherited from
      network/infrastructure team)

  System-Specific Component:
    - TLS configuration on application servers, APIs, and microservices
    - Certificate lifecycle management for system-specific certificates
    - Verification that all data paths use approved cryptographic protocols
    - Prohibition and removal of deprecated protocol support (TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1,
      SSL v2/v3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPLEMENTATION DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. OVERVIEW

The [System Name] implements cryptographic protection for all information
transmitted across internal and external networks in accordance with SC-8(1).
All data in transit — including user-to-application, application-to-database,
service-to-service, and administrative communications — is protected using
FIPS-validated cryptographic modules and NIST-approved algorithms. This
implementation satisfies the Moderate baseline requirement that cryptographic
mechanisms, rather than alternative physical safeguards, be used to protect
transmission confidentiality and integrity.

2. CRYPTOGRAPHIC STANDARDS AND FIPS VALIDATION

2.1 FIPS 140-2 / FIPS 140-3 Validated Modules

All cryptographic operations protecting data in transit are performed using
FIPS 140-2 or FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules, consistent with
the requirements of SC-13 (Cryptographic Protection) and FIPS 140-3 (March
2019 transition).

  a. Web and Application Layer (External-Facing):
     - Module: OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (FIPS 140-2, Certificate #[CMVP #])
       OR
     - Module: Microsoft Windows CNG (Cryptographic Next Generation) FIPS 140-2
       Validated (Certificate #[CMVP #])
     - These modules are validated under NIST's Cryptographic Module Validation
       Program (CMVP) and listed on the NIST CMVP active certificates list.

  b. Cloud Infrastructure Layer (if applicable — e.g., AWS GovCloud):
     - AWS uses FIPS 140-2 validated endpoints (e.g., s3.us-gov-west-1.amazonaws.com
       FIPS endpoints; AWS Key Management Service FIPS module).
     - Verified via AWS FIPS compliance documentation and CMVP certificate
       references published by the CSP.

  c. Database and Backend Communications:
     - Native TLS libraries within [Database Platform, e.g., PostgreSQL 15 /
       Microsoft SQL Server 2019] configured to use the OS-level FIPS-validated
       cryptographic provider.

2.2 Approved Algorithms (per SC-13 / NIST SP 800-175B Rev 1)

  Confidentiality: AES-256-GCM (preferred), AES-128-GCM
  Integrity:       HMAC-SHA-256, HMAC-SHA-384
  Key Exchange:    ECDHE (secp256r1 / P-256, secp384r1 / P-384), DHE-2048 (minimum)
  Authentication:  RSA-2048 (minimum), ECDSA P-256 / P-384
  Certificates:    SHA-256 or stronger signature hash; RSA 2048-bit minimum key
                   length or ECC 256-bit minimum

3. TLS PROTOCOL VERSION REQUIREMENTS AND ENFORCEMENT

3.1 Approved Protocol Versions

  APPROVED:
    - TLS 1.3  (preferred for all new deployments and where supported)
    - TLS 1.2  (acceptable; required minimum for all in-scope communication paths)

  PROHIBITED — Explicitly Disabled:
    - TLS 1.1  (disabled; deprecated per NIST SP 800-52 Rev 2, RFC 8996)
    - TLS 1.0  (disabled; deprecated per NIST SP 800-52 Rev 2, RFC 8996)
    - SSL v3   (disabled; vulnerable to POODLE attack, RFC 7568)
    - SSL v2   (disabled)

3.2 Enforcement Mechanism

TLS version restrictions are enforced at multiple layers:

  a. Load Balancer / Reverse Proxy (e.g., AWS ALB, NGINX, F5):
     - SSL/TLS policy configured to [Organization]-defined approved policy
       (e.g., "ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06" or equivalent).
     - Cipher suites restricted to approved list; weak ciphers (RC4, 3DES,
       NULL, EXPORT-grade) are explicitly disabled.
     - Configuration managed via [Infrastructure-as-Code tool, e.g., Terraform]
       and enforced through configuration management baselines (see CM-6, CM-7).

  b. Application Server Layer:
     - Web/application servers (e.g., Apache HTTP Server, Nginx, IIS) configured
       with SSLProtocol directives or equivalent restricting to TLS 1.2 and 1.3.
     - Verified via automated configuration scanning (see Section 7, Evidence).

  c. Database / Backend Services:
     - Database connections require TLS; unencrypted connections are rejected.
     - Connection strings and drivers configured to enforce TLS with certificate
       validation (not "accept any certificate" mode).

  d. API Gateway:
     - All REST and SOAP API calls require TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3.
     - API Gateway (e.g., AWS API Gateway, Kong) configured with matching TLS
       policy; HTTP (unencrypted) endpoints are disabled.

  e. Inter-Service / Microservice Communications (Internal):
     - Internal service mesh (e.g., Istio / AWS App Mesh) enforces mutual TLS
       (mTLS) for east-west traffic between microservices.
     - mTLS certificates issued by internal Certificate Authority through the
       enterprise PKI (SC-12, SC-17).

4. SCOPE OF CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION

The following communication paths are within scope for SC-8(1) and are
confirmed to use approved cryptographic protection:

  Path                                      Protocol     Notes
  ----------------------------------------- ------------ -------------------------
  User browser to web application           TLS 1.2/1.3  Public internet; HSTS enforced
  Mobile client to API Gateway              TLS 1.2/1.3  Certificate pinning applied
  Application to database server            TLS 1.2/1.3  Client cert auth (mTLS)
  Application to external APIs / 3rd party  TLS 1.2/1.3  Validated at integration testing
  Admin/SSH access (ISSO / admins)          TLS / SSH-2  SSH-2 with approved algorithms
  Remote maintenance connections (MA-4)     TLS 1.2/1.3  Encrypted VPN + TLS
  Batch file transfer (SFTP / FTPS)         SSH-2 / TLS  Replaces FTP (FTP is prohibited)
  Monitoring / logging data streams         TLS 1.2/1.3  To SIEM collector endpoints
  Cloud storage API calls                   TLS 1.2/1.3  FIPS endpoints used
  Inter-AZ / inter-region replication       TLS 1.2/1.3  CSP-managed encryption in transit

5. CERTIFICATE MANAGEMENT

  a. Public-Facing Certificates:
     - Issued by [Trusted CA, e.g., DigiCert Federal SSP, IdenTrust] consistent
       with the Federal PKI (FPKI) framework.
     - Certificate validity: Maximum 398 days (per CA/Browser Forum Baseline
       Requirements); renewal tracked in the certificate inventory.

  b. Internal / Service Certificates:
     - Issued by the organization's internal CA (subordinate to the enterprise
       root CA) under the SC-12 / SC-17 common control.
     - Certificate inventory maintained in [Certificate Management Tool, e.g.,
       Venafi, AWS Certificate Manager, HashiCorp Vault PKI].

  c. Certificate Revocation:
     - OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) and/or CRL (Certificate
       Revocation List) checked for all certificate validations.
     - Revocation procedures documented in [PKI Policy / CPS document].

  d. Cipher Suite Order (TLS 1.3 — negotiated by protocol):
     TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
     TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
     TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 (if supported)

  e. Cipher Suite Order (TLS 1.2 — explicit configuration):
     TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
     TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
     TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
     TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
     [All CBC-mode and RC4 cipher suites disabled]

6. RELATIONSHIP TO RELATED CONTROLS

SC-8(1) is implemented as part of an integrated cryptography and communications
protection strategy that includes the following related controls:

  SC-8   (Base Control): The base control requirement is fully satisfied by the
         cryptographic implementation described herein. No physical alternative
         safeguard is used or claimed.

  SC-12  (Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management): Cryptographic keys
         used for TLS sessions are managed through the enterprise PKI and
         automated certificate management platform. Key generation uses approved
         random number generators within FIPS-validated modules. Session keys
         are ephemeral (via ECDHE/DHE key exchange), providing forward secrecy.

  SC-13  (Cryptographic Protection): All algorithms and modules referenced in
         this narrative comply with SC-13 requirements — only FIPS-validated
         modules and NIST-approved algorithms are used. SC-8(1) and SC-13 are
         implemented in concert; SC-13 defines the cryptographic standards that
         SC-8(1) applies to data in transit.

  SC-17  (Public Key Infrastructure Certificates): Certificates used for TLS
         authentication are issued in compliance with SC-17, using either the
         Federal PKI (for external-facing systems) or the organization's internal
         CA (for internal services).

  SC-28(1) (Protection of Information at Rest | Cryptographic Protection):
         Data at rest is protected separately by SC-28(1), which uses AES-256
         encryption via FIPS-validated modules for all sensitive data stored
         within the system's databases, file systems, and backup media. SC-8(1)
         and SC-28(1) together provide end-to-end cryptographic coverage —
         SC-8(1) covers data in motion; SC-28(1) covers data at rest. This
         layered approach ensures no sensitive information exists in plaintext
         at any point in its lifecycle within the authorization boundary.

  AC-17  (Remote Access): Remote administrative access is encrypted per SC-8(1).
         VPN tunnels use IKEv2/IPsec with AES-256 and SHA-384.

  MA-4   (Nonlocal Maintenance): Maintenance sessions conducted remotely are
         performed exclusively over encrypted channels (TLS 1.2/1.3 or SSH-2)
         consistent with this control.

  IA-3   (Device Identification and Authentication): mTLS client certificates
         used for inter-service authentication are provisioned and managed
         consistently with this control.

7. CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT INTEGRATION

TLS configurations are treated as security-relevant configuration parameters
(CM-6) and are:

  - Documented in the system's approved baseline configuration (CM-2).
  - Restricted from unauthorized modification through change control (CM-3).
  - Scanned for compliance deviations via automated vulnerability scanning
    tools (RA-5) and configuration compliance tools (CM-6, CM-7).
  - Deviation from the approved TLS configuration generates a finding that is
    tracked in the Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) if not remediated
    within [30] days (consistent with RA-5 organizational parameters).

8. CONTINUOUS MONITORING

SC-8(1) implementation is verified on an ongoing basis through:

  a. Automated TLS/SSL scanning: [Tool, e.g., Qualys SSL Labs Enterprise,
     Tenable.io, AWS Security Hub] scans all externally and internally exposed
     TLS endpoints [frequency: monthly minimum, or upon significant change].
     Scans confirm:
       - TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are not negotiable
       - No weak or deprecated cipher suites are offered
       - Certificate validity and chain of trust
       - HSTS header presence (for HTTPS endpoints)

  b. Web Application Vulnerability Scanning (RA-5): DAST scans include checks
     for insecure protocol negotiation and certificate issues.

  c. Penetration Testing (CA-8): Annual third-party penetration test includes
     TLS configuration assessment as part of network and application testing.

  d. SIEM Alerting: TLS handshake failures and anomalous cipher negotiation
     attempts are logged and alerted via the SIEM (AU-2, AU-12, SI-4).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
RESPONSIBLE ROLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Role                              Responsibility
--------------------------------- -----------------------------------------------
Information System Owner (ISO)    Accountable for overall SC-8(1) compliance;
                                  approves cryptographic standards deviations

Information System Security       Authors and maintains this SSP narrative;
Officer (ISSO)                    reviews TLS scan results; manages POA&M items
                                  related to SC-8(1)

System/Application Administrator  Implements and maintains TLS configuration on
                                  application servers, load balancers, and APIs;
                                  manages certificate renewals

Network Security Engineer         Configures and maintains boundary protection
                                  devices, load balancers, and VPN endpoints;
                                  enforces TLS policy at network layer

Cloud/Infrastructure Engineer     Maintains IaC templates enforcing TLS policy;
                                  manages cloud service FIPS endpoint configuration

Enterprise PKI Team               Issues, renews, and revokes certificates;
                                  maintains CA infrastructure (SC-12, SC-17)

Security Operations (SOC)         Monitors TLS-related alerts; responds to
                                  certificate expiration alerts and anomalous
                                  TLS events

Third-Party Assessor (3PAO)       Assesses SC-8(1) implementation during annual
                                  security assessment (CA-2) per SP 800-53A

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
EVIDENCE AND ARTIFACTS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The following artifacts are available to a 3PAO assessor for examination,
interview, and test activities (per SP 800-53A Rev 5 assessment procedures):

Artifact                                    Type        Location / Reference
------------------------------------------- ----------- ---------------------------

                                                        Security Policy, Section [X]
                                                        Version [X], dated [Date]

TLS/SSL Configuration Standards             Examine     Network Security Standards
Document                                                Document, Section [X]
                                                        (approved [Date])

System-Specific TLS Configuration           Examine     System Security Plan,
Baseline (CM-2)                                         Appendix [X] — Baseline
                                                        Configuration

Load Balancer / Proxy TLS Config            Examine /   [IaC repo path] or [config
(e.g., Nginx ssl.conf, ALB policy)          Test        file location]; confirms
                                                        TLS 1.0/1.1 disabled

TLS Scan Results (Qualys SSL / Tenable)     Examine /   [Artifact repository] —
                                            Test        Most recent scan report,
                                                        dated [Date]; A or A+ rating

FIPS 140-2/3 CMVP Certificate References    Examine     NIST CMVP listing:
                                                        https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/
                                                        cryptographic-module-validation-
                                                        program/validated-modules
                                                        Certificate #[CMVP #]

Certificate Inventory / Management          Examine     [Venafi / ACM / Vault]
Records                                                 dashboard export, dated [Date]

Penetration Test Report (Annual)            Examine     [3PAO Firm Name] Penetration
                                                        Test Report, dated [Date],
                                                        Section [X] — TLS Assessment

SIEM Alert Configuration for TLS           Examine /   [SIEM Platform] alert rule
Events                                      Interview   configuration, Rule ID [X]

mTLS Configuration (inter-service)          Examine /   Service mesh configuration
                                            Test        (e.g., Istio PeerAuthentication
                                                        policy); demonstrates
                                                        STRICT mode enforced

Vulnerability Scan Results (RA-5)          Examine     [Scanning tool] output
                                                        confirming no MEDIUM or HIGH
                                                        TLS-related findings open

Interview — ISSO / System Admin             Interview   Confirm awareness of TLS
                                                        requirements; demonstrate
                                                        configuration change process

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ASSESSOR NOTES — 3PAO ASSESSMENT GUIDANCE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Assessment Method Mapping (per SP 800-53A Rev 5, SC-8(1)-1):

  Examine:  SC-8(1) policy; procedures addressing transmission confidentiality
            and integrity; system design documentation; system configuration
            settings and associated documentation; system audit records; other
            relevant documents or records.

  Interview: Organizational personnel with information security responsibilities;
             system/network administrators.

  Test:     Mechanisms implementing transmission confidentiality and integrity
            protection; FIPS-validated cryptographic mechanisms.

Key Assessor Test Procedures:

  1. Use SSL/TLS testing tool (e.g., testssl.sh, Qualys SSL Labs, nmap --script ssl*)
     to verify that TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 are NOT negotiable on any in-scope
     endpoint. A finding of TLS 1.0 or 1.1 being accepted = OTS finding.

  2. Verify cipher suites offered do not include RC4, 3DES, NULL, EXPORT, or
     any anonymous (aNULL) cipher suites.

  3. Confirm that all cipher suites offered use ECDHE or DHE key exchange
     (forward secrecy). RSA key exchange (non-ephemeral) = OTS finding.

  4. Verify FIPS 140-2 or 140-3 CMVP certificate numbers are current and active
     on the NIST CMVP website for all cryptographic modules in use.

  5. Test at least three representative endpoints covering: (a) external
     user-facing HTTPS, (b) API endpoint, (c) internal service-to-service path.

  6. Review certificate attributes: key length, signature algorithm, validity
     period, revocation mechanism. SHA-1 signatures = OTS finding.

  7. Verify HTTP (port 80) is either disabled or redirects to HTTPS with HSTS.
     HTTP accepted without redirect = OTS finding.

  8. Review IaC or configuration management records to confirm TLS settings
     are version-controlled and subject to change control (CM-3).

  9. Confirm the SC-8(1) implementation links to SC-13 (only approved algorithms
     used) and SC-28(1) (complementary at-rest encryption in place).

  10. Verify there are no active POA&M items related to deprecated TLS versions
      or weak cipher suites that have exceeded their scheduled completion date.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ORGANIZATION-DEFINED VALUES (ODVs)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  ODV Parameter                             Assigned Value
  ----------------------------------------- ----------------------------------
  Minimum TLS protocol version              TLS 1.2
  Preferred TLS protocol version            TLS 1.3
  Prohibited protocol versions              SSL v2, SSL v3, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1
  Minimum RSA key length                    2048 bits
  Minimum ECC key length (curve)            256 bits (P-256 / secp256r1)
  FIPS validation requirement               FIPS 140-2 or FIPS 140-3 (active
                                            CMVP certificate required)
  TLS scan frequency                        Monthly (or upon significant change)
  Certificate maximum validity period       398 days (external); 825 days
                                            (internal, per PKI policy)
  OCSP/CRL checking                         Required for all certificate
                                            validations

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Version  Date        Author        Description
-------- ----------- ------------- -------------------------------------------

                                   implementation; TLS 1.2/1.3, FIPS 140-2/3,
                                   SC-13 and SC-28(1) linkage documented.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  - NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5, SC-8 and SC-8(1) — Transmission Confidentiality
    and Integrity | Cryptographic Protection (September 2020)

    Organizations (October 2020)

    and Information Systems

    Principles (January 2022)
