================================================================================
END OF SC-8(1) CONTROL NARRATIVE
================================================================================
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Produces a structured SSP narrative format (not just general guidance)
✅Specifies TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3 as the required protocol and notes TLS 1.0/1.1 are prohibited
✅Mentions FIPS 140-2 or FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules
✅Includes responsible roles (ISSO, system administrator, or equivalent)
✅References related controls SC-13 (cryptographic protection) and/or SC-28(1) (data at rest)
SSP CONTROL NARRATIVE
System Security Plan (SSP) — Control Implementation Description

Control:        SC-8(1) — Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity | Cryptographic Protection
Impact Level:   Moderate
Date:           2026-05-07

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CONTROL STATEMENT (REFERENCE)

SC-8(1): The information system implements cryptographic mechanisms to prevent
unauthorized disclosure of information and detect changes to information during
transmission unless otherwise protected by [organization-defined alternative
physical safeguards].

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

RESPONSIBLE ROLES

Control Owner:          Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
Implementation Owner:   Infrastructure / Platform Engineering Team
Review Frequency:       Annual and upon significant change


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PART A — CONTROL SUMMARY

This system implements FIPS 140-2/140-3 validated cryptographic mechanisms on
all transmission paths to protect the confidentiality and integrity of federal
information in transit. Cryptographic protection applies to all data flows
between end users and the system, between system components, and between this
system and external services or interconnected systems. No unencrypted
transmission of sensitive federal data is permitted across any network boundary,
including internal network segments carrying CUI or other sensitive information.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PART B — IMPLEMENTATION NARRATIVE

1. CRYPTOGRAPHIC STANDARDS AND FIPS COMPLIANCE

   The organization enforces the use of FIPS 140-2 validated (or FIPS 140-3
   validated where available) cryptographic modules for all transmission
   encryption across the system boundary. This requirement is codified in the
   organization's Cryptographic Policy and enforced through system configuration
   baselines.

   Approved algorithms in use include:
   - Symmetric encryption:  AES-256 (GCM or CBC mode with HMAC-SHA-256) for
                            bulk data encryption in transit.
   - Asymmetric / key exchange: ECDHE (P-256 or P-384 curves) or RSA-2048+
                                for session key establishment.
   - Integrity / authentication: HMAC-SHA-256 or HMAC-SHA-384; digital
                                 signatures using RSA-2048+ or ECDSA P-256.
   - Hash functions:        SHA-256 or SHA-384 (SHA-1 is prohibited).

   Use of MD5, RC4, DES, 3DES, export-grade ciphers, and SSL 3.0 / TLS 1.0 /
   TLS 1.1 is explicitly prohibited and blocked at the infrastructure level.


2. EXTERNAL-FACING TRANSMISSION PATHS (USER TO SYSTEM)

   All user-facing interfaces — including the web application portal, REST APIs,
   and any mobile or thick-client endpoints — enforce TLS 1.2 at minimum, with
   TLS 1.3 preferred. This is enforced at the Application Load Balancer / TLS
   termination layer.

   TLS configuration enforces:
   a. Strong cipher suites only (e.g., TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 for TLS 1.3;
      TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 for TLS 1.2 fallback).
   b. Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) through ECDHE key exchange, ensuring
      session keys cannot be retroactively decrypted if long-term keys are
      compromised.
   c. Server certificate validation using a certificate issued by a NIST-
      approved or FedRAMP-authorized Certificate Authority (CA), with
      certificates following a 2-year maximum validity cycle.
   d. HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) headers with a minimum max-age of
      31,536,000 seconds, preventing protocol downgrade attacks.
   e. HTTP is redirected to HTTPS at the load balancer layer; plain HTTP
      connections are rejected and logged.

   TLS cipher suite and protocol enforcement is validated quarterly using
   automated scanning tools (e.g., SSL Labs API or equivalent government-
   approved tooling) and upon any change to TLS termination configuration.


3. INTERNAL / EAST-WEST TRANSMISSION PATHS (COMPONENT TO COMPONENT)

   Data flows between internal system components — including microservices,
   application servers, database servers, message queues, and caching layers —
   are encrypted in transit using mutual TLS (mTLS) 1.2/1.3 enforced by the
   service mesh (e.g., Istio, Linkerd, or equivalent). This ensures that both
   the calling and receiving services authenticate each other and that the
   channel is encrypted, preventing lateral-movement-based interception even
   within the private network.

   Specific internal paths and their cryptographic controls:
   a. Application tier to database tier: TLS 1.2+ enforced at the database
      driver/connector level; certificates issued by the internal private CA
      managed by the organization. Database connections using cleartext are
      rejected by firewall policy.
   b. Application tier to cache (e.g., Redis / ElastiCache): TLS enforced at
      the client configuration; in-transit encryption enabled at the managed
      service level.
   c. Application tier to message broker (e.g., Kafka, SQS, RabbitMQ): TLS
      1.2+ enforced; messages containing CUI additionally apply envelope
      encryption at the application layer prior to queuing.
   d. Container-to-container communication within Kubernetes clusters: Network
      policies restrict pod-to-pod traffic; mTLS enforced via the service mesh
      for all sensitive service communication.

   Where physical separation of network segments (e.g., dedicated private
   subnets, VLANs with no routing to untrusted segments) is employed as an
   alternative or complementary safeguard, this is documented in the system's
   network diagram and the relevant segment is identified in the SSP as
   operating under [organization-defined alternative physical safeguards].
   However, even in those cases, TLS encryption is applied as defense-in-depth.


4. INTERCONNECTIONS WITH EXTERNAL SYSTEMS AND THIRD PARTIES

   All connections to interconnected systems — federal agencies, cloud service
   providers (CSPs), SaaS vendors, or other system interconnections governed by
   Interconnection Security Agreements (ISAs) / Memoranda of Understanding
   (MOUs) — traverse encrypted channels:

   a. Encrypted VPN tunnels (IPsec IKEv2 with AES-256-GCM and SHA-256
      integrity) or dedicated encrypted private circuits are used for
      site-to-site connections to partner agency networks.
   b. API-based integrations with external CSPs or SaaS use TLS 1.2+;
      API gateway configurations reject connections using deprecated protocols.
   c. SFTP or FTPS (TLS-based) with AES-256 is used for any scheduled bulk
      file transfers; plain FTP is prohibited and blocked by firewall ACL.
   d. Email containing CUI uses S/MIME or encrypted secure file transfer
      portals; unencrypted SMTP for CUI is prohibited.


5. CERTIFICATE AND KEY MANAGEMENT

   The organization maintains a Certificate Management Procedure that governs
   the full lifecycle of all certificates used for transmission encryption:

   a. Certificate issuance: Certificates are issued by a NIST SP 800-57-
      compliant CA. Public-facing certificates use a publicly trusted CA;
      internal certificates use the organization's private CA integrated with
      the enterprise PKI.
   b. Key length and algorithm: RSA keys are minimum 2048-bit; ECC keys use
      P-256 or P-384 curves.
   c. Certificate validity: Maximum validity period is 2 years for internal
      certificates; 1 year for public-facing certificates, aligned with current
      CA/Browser Forum and NIST guidance.
   d. Certificate renewal: Automated renewal processes (e.g., ACME protocol /
      Let's Encrypt for public certs, or enterprise SCEP/EST for internal certs)
      are in place to renew certificates at least 30 days before expiration.
      Expiration monitoring alerts are configured with a 60-day and 14-day
      notification threshold.
   e. Certificate revocation: CRL and/or OCSP stapling is configured to enable
      revocation checking. Compromised certificates are revoked within 24 hours
      of confirmed compromise.
   f. Private key protection: Private keys for TLS certificates are stored in
      a Hardware Security Module (HSM) or a FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated
      secrets management solution (e.g., HashiCorp Vault with HSM backing, AWS
      CloudHSM, or Azure Key Vault HSM tier). Keys are never stored in plaintext
      in configuration files, source code repositories, or environment variables.
   g. Key rotation: Session keys are ephemeral (PFS ensures new keys per
      session). Long-term signing and encryption keys follow the organization's
      key rotation schedule (minimum annual rotation for RSA/ECC keys, or upon
      suspected compromise).


6. ENFORCEMENT AND PREVENTION OF NON-ENCRYPTED TRANSMISSIONS

   Technical controls are implemented to prevent non-encrypted transmissions:

   a. Network Security Groups (NSGs) / firewall rules block inbound and
      outbound cleartext protocol ports (TCP/21 for FTP, TCP/23 for Telnet,
      TCP/80 for HTTP as a destination, TCP/143 for IMAP cleartext, etc.) at
      the network perimeter and internal segment boundaries.
   b. Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules are configured to block HTTP
      requests and enforce HTTPS.
   c. Configuration management tooling (e.g., Ansible, Terraform) enforces
      TLS-enabled configurations as code and flags any non-compliant
      configuration as a policy violation in the CI/CD pipeline prior to
      deployment.
   d. Compliance-as-code / SIEM alerting triggers an alert and creates a
      security incident ticket if a network flow is detected on a prohibited
      cleartext protocol port involving a system asset.


7. OPERATING SYSTEM AND APPLICATION HARDENING SUPPORTING SC-8(1)

   System hardening baselines (aligned with DISA STIGs or CIS Benchmarks at
   Level 2, as applicable) include specific settings that support transmission
   encryption enforcement:

   a. SSH is configured to use only SSHv2; SSHv1 is disabled. SSH ciphers are
      restricted to AES-128-CTR, AES-192-CTR, AES-256-CTR, AES-128-GCM,
      AES-256-GCM; weak ciphers (arcfour, 3des-cbc, blowfish) are disabled.
   b. Operating system TLS libraries (e.g., OpenSSL) are maintained at current
      patch levels and configured to enforce minimum TLS 1.2.
   c. Application frameworks are configured with TLS-only modes; application
      configuration is reviewed during security code review to confirm no
      cleartext fallback options are enabled.


8. MONITORING, AUDITING, AND CONTINUOUS MONITORING

   The effectiveness of cryptographic transmission controls is continuously
   monitored:

   a. Network traffic analysis tools (IDS/IPS with SSL inspection capability
      on perimeter flows) monitor for anomalous unencrypted traffic or use of
      deprecated TLS versions.
   b. Automated TLS/SSL configuration scanning is performed quarterly and after
      changes to identify any protocol or cipher suite weaknesses.
   c. Certificate expiration monitoring is automated; alerts are sent to the
      security operations team at 60-day and 14-day intervals before expiration.
   d. SIEM dashboards include metrics for TLS protocol version distribution
      across detected sessions; deviations from expected TLS 1.2/1.3 usage
      trigger investigation.
   e. Annual penetration testing explicitly includes testing for SSL/TLS
      weaknesses (e.g., BEAST, POODLE, DROWN, ROBOT attack susceptibility)
      and validates that no downgrade attacks are possible.
   f. Results of TLS scanning and certificate audits are retained as artifacts
      in the continuous monitoring repository and are made available to the AO
      and assessors upon request.


9. POLICIES AND PROCEDURES SUPPORTING THIS CONTROL

   The following organizational policies and procedures directly govern SC-8(1)
   implementation:

   - Cryptographic Policy (CP-001): Defines approved algorithms, key lengths,
     and prohibited ciphers in alignment with NIST SP 800-175B.
   - System and Communications Protection Policy (SCP-001): Establishes the
     organization's requirement for encryption of all sensitive data in transit.
   - Certificate Management Procedure (CMP-001): Documents the lifecycle
     management of all cryptographic certificates used by the system.
   - Network Security Policy (NSP-001): Defines prohibited cleartext protocols
     and firewall rule requirements.
   - Configuration Management Policy (CMP-002): Ensures TLS configurations
     are included in baseline configurations subject to change control.

   All policies are reviewed annually and approved by the CISO.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PART C — EVIDENCE ARTIFACTS (FOR 3PAO ASSESSMENT)

The following artifacts should be provided to the 3PAO assessor to substantiate
this control implementation:

1. Network architecture / data flow diagrams annotating all transmission paths
   and the encryption protocol applied to each.
2. TLS/SSL configuration scan reports (e.g., SSL Labs API output or equivalent)
   for all external-facing endpoints, dated within the last 90 days.
3. Firewall / Network Security Group rule exports demonstrating that cleartext
   protocol ports are blocked.
4. Current certificate inventory (hostname, CA, algorithm, key length,
   expiration date) for all system certificates.
5. FIPS 140-2/140-3 certificate numbers for cryptographic modules in use
   (reference NIST CMVP database listings).
6. Configuration management baseline excerpts showing TLS enforcement settings
   for load balancers, web servers, database connectors, and service mesh.
7. Organization's Cryptographic Policy and Certificate Management Procedure.
8. Evidence of mTLS enforcement in service mesh (e.g., Istio PeerAuthentication
   policy configuration screenshots or exports).
9. HSM or secrets manager configuration evidence showing private key storage
   protection.
10. Penetration test report excerpt covering TLS/SSL testing results (most
    recent annual test).
11. SIEM alert rules and sample alert evidence for cleartext protocol detection.
12. Certificate expiration monitoring alert configuration and evidence.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PART D — RELATED CONTROLS

SC-8         Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity (base control)
SC-8(2)      Pre/Post Transmission Handling
SC-12        Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management
SC-13        Cryptographic Protection
SC-17        Public Key Infrastructure Certificates
SC-28        Protection of Information at Rest
IA-3         Device Identification and Authentication (supports mTLS)
SA-9         External Information System Services (ISAs/MOUs)
AU-2 / AU-12 Audit Events / Audit Record Generation (logging of TLS events)
CM-6         Configuration Settings (enforcing TLS baselines)
CM-7         Least Functionality (disabling cleartext protocols)


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PART E — CONTROL ORIGINATION AND INHERITANCE

Control Origination:    Hybrid
  - Inherited (from CSP/FedRAMP Authorized IaaS/PaaS, if applicable):
      The underlying infrastructure provider's FedRAMP authorization inherits
      physical layer protections and some network-layer encryption services
      (e.g., encrypted VPC peering, underlying TLS libraries on managed
      services). Specific inherited capabilities are documented in the
      Customer Responsibility Matrix (CRM) provided by the CSP.
  - System-Specific (this system's responsibility):
      Application-layer TLS configuration, certificate management, cipher
      suite enforcement, mTLS for internal service communication, prohibition
      of cleartext protocols via firewall policy, and all monitoring and
      auditing activities described above are the responsibility of this
      system's operators.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PART F — PARAMETER VALUES (ORGANIZATION-DEFINED)

Per SC-8(1), the organization has defined the following parameter:

  [organization-defined alternative physical safeguards]:
      Dedicated private network segments (air-gapped or VLAN-isolated) within
      the organization's classified enclave for intra-enclave processing paths
      that are physically secured and access-controlled. These segments are
      documented in the network architecture diagram (Appendix [X]) and are
      subject to physical access controls per PE-3. Cryptographic encryption
      is applied as an additional layer even on these segments.

  Note: For Moderate impact systems, physical safeguards alone are NOT accepted
  as a substitute for cryptographic protection on any path that traverses a
  shared or untrusted network segment. Cryptographic protection is therefore
  applied universally across this system's transmission paths.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

IMPLEMENTATION STATUS:  Implemented
ASSESSMENT RESULT (PLACEHOLDER):  [To be completed by 3PAO]
DATE OF LAST REVIEW:    2026-05-07
NEXT SCHEDULED REVIEW:  2027-05-07

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Prompt: We are preparing for our first FedRAMP Moderate authorization. We currently have ISO 27001:2022 certification. What is the relationship between SP 800-53 Moderate controls and ISO 27001:2022 Annex A, what gaps will we likely have, and how does the RMF process work for FedRAMP?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Describes the ISO 27001 to SP 800-53 relationship including approximate control overlap
✅Identifies FIPS 140-2/3 cryptography as a gap (not covered by ISO 27001)
✅Identifies 3PAO requirement and US-CERT incident reporting as FedRAMP-specific gaps
✅Describes all 7 RMF steps (Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor) in context of FedRAMP
✅Distinguishes between Agency ATO and JAB P-ATO authorization paths
================================================================================
NIST SP 800-53 EXPERT RESPONSE
Topic: FedRAMP Moderate Authorization for ISO 27001:2022-Certified Organizations
================================================================================

QUESTION: We are preparing for our first FedRAMP Moderate authorization. We
currently have ISO 27001:2022 certification. What is the relationship between SP
800-53 Moderate controls and ISO 27001:2022 Annex A, what gaps will we likely
have, and how does the RMF process work for FedRAMP?

================================================================================
SECTION 1 — ISO 27001:2022 ANNEX A → SP 800-53 MODERATE CONTROL FAMILY MAPPING
================================================================================

ISO 27001:2022 and NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 share substantial conceptual overlap,
but they differ in scope, prescriptiveness, and the specific controls each
requires. Your ISO 27001:2022 certification provides a meaningful starting point,
but it is not a substitute for the SP 800-53 Moderate baseline. The table below
maps each ISO 27001:2022 Annex A control domain to the corresponding SP 800-53
Rev 5 control family (or families) and identifies the nature of the alignment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ISO 27001:2022 Annex A Domain          | SP 800-53 Rev 5 Family / Controls
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5.1–5.14 Organizational Controls       | PL (Planning), PM (Program Mgmt),
  (Policies, roles, asset mgmt,        |   PS (Personnel Security),
  info classification, supplier        |   RA (Risk Assessment),
  security, threat intelligence,       |   SA (System & Services Acquisition),
  incident management)                 |   SR (Supply Chain Risk Management)
                                       |
5.15–5.18 Access Control               | AC (Access Control): AC-1 through
  (Access rights, privileged           |   AC-25; particularly AC-2, AC-3,
  access, information access           |   AC-5, AC-6, AC-17
  restriction, authentication)         |
                                       |
5.19–5.22 Supplier Relationships       | SR-1 through SR-12 (Supply Chain
  (Supplier security policy,           |   Risk Management); SA-9 (External
  managing supplier services,          |   System Services)
  cloud services)                      |
                                       |
5.23–5.28 Incident Management          | IR-1 through IR-10 (Incident
  (Planning, reporting, response,      |   Response); CA-2 (Assessments);
  lessons learned)                     |   AU (Audit & Accountability)
                                       |
5.29–5.30 Business Continuity          | CP-1 through CP-13 (Contingency
  (ICT continuity, redundancy)         |   Planning); CP-7, CP-9, CP-10
                                       |
5.31–5.36 Legal / Compliance           | CA (Assessment, Authorization,
  (Legal requirements, IP, privacy,    |   Monitoring); PT (PII Processing
  evidence, compliance review)         |   & Transparency); RA-5
                                       |
5.37 Documented Operating Procedures   | CM-1 (Config Policy); PL-2 (SSP)
                                       |
6.1–6.8 People Controls                | AT-1 through AT-6 (Awareness &
  (Screening, terms, awareness,        |   Training); PS-1 through PS-9
  training, disciplinary, remote       |   (Personnel Security); AC-17
  working, reporting)                  |
                                       |
7.1–7.14 Physical Controls             | PE-1 through PE-23 (Physical &
  (Physical perimeter, entry,          |   Environmental Protection)
  securing offices, monitoring,        |
  delivery areas, equipment)           |
                                       |
8.1–8.2 Operational Security           | CM-2, CM-6 (Configuration);
  (User endpoint devices,              |   SC-28 (Data at Rest)
  privileged access workstations)      |
                                       |
8.3–8.8 Asset / Vulnerability Mgmt     | CM-8 (Inventory); RA-5 (Vuln
  (Info deletion, masking,             |   Scanning); SI-2 (Patching);
  data leakage prevention,             |   MP (Media Protection)
  vulnerability management)            |
                                       |
8.9–8.13 Configuration & Change        | CM-1 through CM-14 (Config Mgmt);
  (Configuration mgmt, info            |   CP-9, CP-10 (Backup/Recovery);
  deletion, data backup)               |   SA-10 (Developer Config Mgmt)
                                       |
8.14–8.16 Redundancy / Monitoring      | CP-7, CP-11 (Availability);
  (Redundancy, monitoring,             |   AU-2 through AU-13 (Audit);
  logging, clock sync)                 |   SI-4 (System Monitoring)
                                       |
8.17–8.22 Network Security             | SC-1 through SC-51 (System &
  (Network segmentation, web           |   Communications Protection);
  filtering, cryptography,             |   SC-7, SC-8, SC-28; IA controls
  network connections)                 |
                                       |
8.23–8.28 Application Security         | SA-11, SA-15 (Dev Security Testing);
  (Secure development, testing,        |   SI-3 (Malware); SC-18
  source code)                         |   (Mobile Code)
                                       |
8.29–8.32 Secure Dev / Change          | SA-3 (SDLC); CM-3, CM-4 (Change
  (DevSecOps, outsourced dev,          |   Control); SA-8, SA-10, SA-11
  separation environments, change      |
  management)                          |
                                       |
8.33–8.34 Test / Audit                 | CA-2 (Security Assessments);
  (Test information, protection        |   RA-5; AU (Audit); SA-11
  of audit tools)                      |
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SUMMARY OF ALIGNMENT: Your ISO 27001:2022 certification confirms that you have a
functioning Information Security Management System (ISMS) covering policy,
risk assessment, supplier management, incident response, physical security,
business continuity, and access control at an organizational level. SP 800-53 Rev 5
derives from a common heritage with ISO 27001 and the two frameworks share roughly
70–80% conceptual overlap at the domain level.

However, alignment at the domain level does not equate to compliance at the
control level. SP 800-53 is significantly more prescriptive, more granular, and
carries U.S. federal-law specificity that ISO 27001 does not.

================================================================================
SECTION 2 — FEDRAMP MODERATE GAPS FOR ISO 27001:2022-CERTIFIED ORGANIZATIONS
================================================================================

The gaps below represent areas where ISO 27001:2022 certification typically
provides little or no coverage for FedRAMP Moderate requirements. These are
the highest-priority remediation items.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP 1 — FIPS 140-2 / FIPS 140-3 VALIDATED CRYPTOGRAPHIC MODULES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Controls: SC-13 (Cryptographic Protection), IA-7 (Cryptographic Module Auth)
Baseline: Moderate (required)

ISO 27001:2022 Annex A 8.24 requires "use of cryptography" and says
algorithms must be "appropriate." It does not mandate FIPS 140-validated
modules.

FedRAMP requirement: ALL cryptographic modules used in the system must be
validated to FIPS 140-2 or FIPS 140-3 (the successor standard). This applies
to:
  - TLS/HTTPS implementations (must use FIPS-approved cipher suites)
  - Data-at-rest encryption (SC-28; disk encryption libraries must be validated)
  - VPN / remote access tunnels (SC-8(1))
  - SSH implementations
  - Any key management system

Action items:
  - Inventory all cryptographic implementations across the system
  - Verify each against the NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP)
    at csrc.nist.gov/projects/cryptographic-module-validation-program
  - Replace or reconfigure any non-validated modules (e.g., OpenSSL must be
    used in FIPS mode with a FIPS 140-2/3 validated build)
  - Document FIPS validation certificate numbers in the SSP under SC-13

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP 2 — PIV / PIV-INTEROPERABLE MULTI-FACTOR AUTHENTICATION (MFA)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Controls: IA-2(1), IA-2(2), IA-2(12), IA-8(1), IA-8(2), IA-8(4)
Baseline: Moderate (required)

ISO 27001:2022 Annex A 8.5 ("Secure Authentication") requires MFA for
privileged access but does not specify PIV (Personal Identity Verification)
cards as the required authenticator type.

FedRAMP/HSPD-12 requirement: Federal policy (OMB M-11-11, HSPD-12, OMB
M-19-17) mandates that all privileged users and all users accessing
federal systems use PIV credentials or PIV-Interoperable (PIV-I) credentials
for network authentication:

  - IA-2(1): MFA for privileged accounts (network access) — PIV required
  - IA-2(2): MFA for non-privileged accounts (network access) — PIV or
    PIV-I required for federal employee/contractor access
  - IA-2(12): PIV credentials accepted and verified
  - IA-8(1)/(2)/(4): Non-organizational users (public, contractors not
    issued PIV) must use NIST IAL/AAL-aligned credentials per SP 800-63B

Action items:
  - Implement PIV card authentication for all privileged users
  - For SaaS/cloud offerings, implement PIV-compatible identity federation
    via SAML or OIDC with a PIV-capable IdP (e.g., Azure AD Government,
    Okta FedRAMP, Ping Identity FedRAMP)
  - Document authenticator types, IAL/AAL levels, and PIV certificate
    validation procedures in SSP IA-2 narratives

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP 3 — CONTINUOUS MONITORING (CONMON) FREQUENCIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Controls: CA-7 (Continuous Monitoring), RA-5 (Vulnerability Monitoring),
          SI-2 (Flaw Remediation), CM-6 (Configuration Settings)
Baseline: Moderate (required)

ISO 27001:2022 requires vulnerability management and monitoring but does not
specify frequencies. FedRAMP ConMon requirements are prescriptive:

  Activity                          | FedRAMP Frequency
  ----------------------------------|-------------------
  OS vulnerability scans            | Monthly
  Web application vulnerability scans| Monthly
  Database vulnerability scans      | Weekly
  Annual 3PAO control assessment    | Annual
  Annual penetration test (3PAO)    | Annual
  POA&M updates                     | Monthly
  Inventory updates                 | Monthly
  Significant Change Requests       | Before major changes

Remediation deadlines (FedRAMP):
  - Critical vulnerabilities: 30 days
  - High vulnerabilities: 90 days
  - Moderate vulnerabilities: 180 days
  - Low vulnerabilities: 1 year (or risk acceptance)

Action items:
  - Implement automated vulnerability scanning at required FedRAMP frequencies
  - Stand up a monthly POA&M management process
  - Procure a 3PAO-accredited assessor for annual assessment and penetration test
  - Document ConMon strategy in CA-7 SSP narrative with specific frequencies
    and responsible parties
  - Establish a FedRAMP Significant Change Request process

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP 4 — US-CERT / CISA INCIDENT REPORTING
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Controls: IR-6 (Incident Reporting), IR-6(1), IR-7, IR-8
Baseline: Moderate (required)

ISO 27001:2022 Annex A 5.26 ("Response to information security incidents")
and 5.27 require incidents be managed and lessons learned, but reporting
timelines are organization-defined. ISO 27001 does not require reporting to
external government bodies.

FedRAMP incident reporting requirements (OMB M-17-25, US-CERT Federal
Incident Notification Guidelines):
  - All security incidents affecting federal data must be reported to US-CERT
    (now CISA) within 1 hour of discovery for critical/major incidents
  - Monthly operational reports to FedRAMP PMO and agency Authorizing Official
  - Incident handling procedures must reference the US-CERT reporting portal
    and use US-CERT severity categories (CAT 0 through CAT 6)

FedRAMP IR-6 ODV (Organization-Defined Value): Report within 1 hour to
FedRAMP PMO and agency customer(s); submit US-CERT notification within
1 hour for CAT 1/2 incidents.

Action items:
  - Update IR policy and procedures to include US-CERT/CISA reporting
    obligations and timelines
  - Train incident response team on FedRAMP-specific reporting procedures
  - Establish secure communications channel with agency customers for
    incident notification
  - Document IR-6 SSP narrative with specific reporting timelines and
    escalation procedures
  - Include IR-8 (Incident Response Plan) that references FedRAMP IR
    Playbook template

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP 5 — THIRD-PARTY ASSESSMENT ORGANIZATION (3PAO) REQUIREMENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Controls: CA-2 (Control Assessments), CA-8 (Penetration Testing)
Baseline: FedRAMP-specific requirement (not in standard SP 800-53B baseline)

ISO 27001:2022 requires internal audits (Clause 9.2) and management review
(Clause 9.3), and certification body audits. However, the ISO certification
body (CB) auditor is not equivalent to a FedRAMP 3PAO.

FedRAMP requirement: All assessments must be conducted by an accredited 3PAO:
  - 3PAOs must be accredited by either:
    a. American Association for Laboratory Accreditation (A2LA) under R311
    b. FedRAMP PMO directly (for select organizations)
  - The 3PAO conducts the initial assessment (producing the SAR) and all
    annual assessments thereafter
  - 3PAOs must be independent from the CSP — internal teams cannot serve
    as the 3PAO

Action items:
  - Identify and engage an accredited 3PAO early in the process
    (list available at marketplace.fedramp.gov)
  - Budget for initial 3PAO assessment (typically 6–18 weeks engagement)
    and recurring annual assessments
  - Provide 3PAO with a complete readiness package: SSP, policies,
    architecture diagrams, system inventory, prior audit evidence
  - Consider engaging 3PAO for a FedRAMP Readiness Assessment (FRA)
    before the full assessment to identify gaps

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP 6 — FEDRAMP ORGANIZATION-DEFINED VALUES (ODVs)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Controls: Multiple — AC-2(3), AC-7, AU-11, IA-5(1), SI-2, CA-7, and others
Baseline: FedRAMP-specific parameter values

ISO 27001:2022 leaves most operational parameters to the organization
(e.g., password complexity, log retention, account lockout thresholds).
FedRAMP prescribes specific ODV values that override organization discretion.

Key FedRAMP ODV requirements for Moderate systems:

  Control   | Parameter                        | FedRAMP Required Value
  ----------|----------------------------------|------------------------
  AC-2(3)   | Disable inactive accounts after  | 90 days
  AC-7      | Account lockout threshold        | 3 invalid attempts
  AC-7      | Lockout duration                 | 30 minutes (or admin unlock)
  AU-11     | Audit log retention              | 90 days online; 1 year archived
  IA-5(1)   | Minimum password length          | 15 characters
  IA-5(1)   | Password complexity              | Upper, lower, number, special
  IA-5(1)   | Password history                 | 24 passwords remembered
  IA-5(1)   | Maximum password age             | 60 days (privileged);
            |                                  | No max for PIV/MFA accounts
  RA-5      | Vulnerability scan frequency     | Monthly (OS/web); Weekly (DB)
  SI-2      | Patch critical vulns within      | 30 days
  SI-2      | Patch high vulns within          | 90 days
  CA-7      | ConMon frequency                 | See Section 3 above

Action items:
  - Conduct an ODV gap analysis comparing your current operational parameters
    against FedRAMP-required values
  - Update technical configurations (AD Group Policy, SIEM retention settings,
    vulnerability scanner schedules) to meet FedRAMP ODVs
  - Document all ODVs explicitly in the SSP; FedRAMP reviewers specifically
    check that ODV parameters are populated (not left as "[organization-defined]")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP 7 — FEDRAMP SSP TEMPLATE AND DOCUMENTATION FORMAT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Controls: PL-2 (System Security Plan), CA-2, CA-7
Baseline: FedRAMP-specific requirement

ISO 27001:2022 requires a Statement of Applicability (SoA) and documented
ISMS policies, but does not specify SSP format.

FedRAMP requirement: CSPs must use the official FedRAMP SSP template
(available at fedramp.gov/documents-templates). The template is ~350+ pages
when fully completed and includes:

  - System description (architecture, boundaries, data flows)
  - FIPS 199 categorization determination
  - All 325+ Moderate baseline controls with implementation narratives
  - Responsible roles for each control
  - Implementation status (Implemented / Partially Implemented / Planned /
    Not Applicable)
  - Control inheritance designations (system-specific vs. inherited from CSP,
    IaaS/PaaS provider, or common control provider)
  - Customer responsibilities (controls customers must implement)

Additional FedRAMP package documents required:
  - System Security Plan (SSP) — FedRAMP template
  - SSP Attachments: Control Implementation Summary (CIS), Customer
    Responsibility Matrix (CRM), FIPS 199 categorization, system inventory,
    network diagrams, data flow diagrams, ports/protocols/services list
  - Security Assessment Plan (SAP) — developed by 3PAO
  - Security Assessment Report (SAR) — produced by 3PAO
  - Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) — maintained by CSP
  - FedRAMP Readiness Assessment Report (RAR) — optional but recommended

Action items:
  - Download the current FedRAMP SSP template from fedramp.gov
  - Map your existing ISO 27001 documentation (SoA, ISMS policies,
    risk assessment) to SSP sections — most will supplement rather than
    replace SSP narratives
  - Assign ISSO / compliance team to write control narratives for all
    325+ Moderate controls
  - Develop architecture diagrams and data flow diagrams at FedRAMP
    required detail level
  - Engage a FedRAMP consultant or use FedRAMP Marketplace CSP resources
    for SSP template guidance

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP SUMMARY TABLE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Gap Area                | ISO 27001 Coverage  | FedRAMP Moderate Requirement
------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------------
FIPS 140 Cryptography   | "Appropriate algs"  | FIPS 140-2/3 validated modules
                        | (8.24)              | for ALL crypto (SC-13, IA-7)
PIV/PIV-I MFA           | MFA required (8.5)  | PIV cards required; SP 800-63B
                        |                     | IAL/AAL alignment (IA-2(1)(2)(12))
ConMon Frequencies      | Unspecified         | Monthly scans, weekly DB scans,
                        |                     | annual 3PAO assessment (CA-7)
US-CERT Incident        | Internal reporting  | 1-hour US-CERT reporting for
Reporting               | (5.26)              | major incidents (IR-6)
3PAO Requirement        | CB audits           | Accredited 3PAO for all
                        |                     | assessments (CA-2, CA-8)
FedRAMP ODVs            | Org-defined         | Specific numeric values mandated
                        |                     | (AC-2, AC-7, AU-11, IA-5, SI-2)
SSP / Package Format    | SoA + policies      | FedRAMP SSP template; full
                        |                     | authorization package (PL-2)

================================================================================
SECTION 3 — RMF PROCESS FOR FEDRAMP (SP 800-37 Rev 2 ADAPTED)
================================================================================

FedRAMP is operationalized through the NIST Risk Management Framework
(SP 800-37 Rev 2), adapted with FedRAMP-specific requirements. There are two
authorization paths:

  AGENCY ATO PATH: CSP works directly with a specific federal agency customer.
    The agency Authorizing Official (AO) grants an Authority to Operate (ATO).
    Fastest for single-agency relationships.

  JAB P-ATO PATH: CSP works with the Joint Authorization Board (JAB) —
    comprised of DoD CIO, DHS CIO, and GSA CIO. Results in a Provisional ATO
    (P-ATO) accepted by all federal agencies. Highest rigor; longest timeline.
    JAB prioritizes CSPs using the FedRAMP Connect process.

Both paths follow the same seven RMF steps:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 1 — PREPARE
SP 800-37 Rev 2, Chapter 2; FedRAMP: fedramp.gov/prepare/
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Purpose: Establish the organizational and system context; assign roles;
         understand the authorization path.

Key tasks for FedRAMP Moderate:
  1. Assign FedRAMP roles:
     - Authorizing Official (AO): agency CISO or designated senior official
       (for Agency ATO) or JAB representatives (for P-ATO)
     - System Owner (SO): CSP executive accountable for the system
     - Information System Security Officer (ISSO): manages day-to-day
       security operations and SSP
     - FedRAMP Point of Contact (POC): CSP contact for FedRAMP PMO
  2. Understand FedRAMP boundary: define what is "in scope" for authorization
     (the Authorization Boundary — all components processing, transmitting,
     or storing federal data)
  3. Select authorization path (Agency ATO vs. JAB P-ATO)
  4. Conduct initial gap assessment against FedRAMP Moderate baseline
  5. Develop remediation roadmap and project plan
  6. Register system in FedRAMP Marketplace (fedramp.gov/marketplace)
  7. Engage 3PAO early

Key outputs: Assigned roles; selected authorization path; gap remediation plan;
             FedRAMP Marketplace registration.

Timeline estimate: 1–3 months

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 2 — CATEGORIZE
FIPS 199; SP 800-60; FedRAMP: FIPS 199 template
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Purpose: Determine the system's security impact level (Low, Moderate, or High)
         based on the federal data it processes, stores, and transmits.

Key tasks for FedRAMP Moderate:
  1. Identify all federal information types processed using SP 800-60 Vol II
  2. Assess Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability impact for each type
  3. Apply the high-water mark: overall system category = highest C/I/A value
  4. For FedRAMP Moderate: system must be categorized as FIPS 199 Moderate
     (at least one of C/I/A = Moderate; none = High)
  5. Complete and sign the FIPS 199 Security Categorization document
     (AO and SO signatures required)
  6. Incorporate categorization into SSP Section 2

Note: If categorization yields a High impact level for any objective, the
system requires the FedRAMP High baseline (~421 controls), not Moderate.

Key outputs: Signed FIPS 199 Security Categorization document; system
             description in SSP Sections 1–2.

Timeline estimate: 2–4 weeks

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 3 — SELECT
SP 800-53B (Moderate baseline); FedRAMP baseline documentation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Purpose: Select, tailor, and document the controls for the system.

Key tasks for FedRAMP Moderate:
  1. Start with SP 800-53B Moderate baseline (~325 controls/enhancements)
  2. Apply FedRAMP overlay: FedRAMP-specific ODV values override the standard
     baseline parameters (see Gap 6 above)
  3. Identify inherited controls:
     - Controls implemented by your underlying IaaS/PaaS provider
       (e.g., AWS GovCloud, Azure Government, Google Cloud) that are inherited
     - Document in the Control Implementation Summary (CIS) and SSP
     - Your IaaS/PaaS provider must have a FedRAMP authorization for
       you to inherit their controls
  4. Identify customer-responsible controls: controls the agency customer
     must implement (document in Customer Responsibility Matrix — CRM)
  5. Identify system-specific controls: controls you implement yourself
  6. Assign ODV parameter values for all controls with organization-defined
     parameters (use FedRAMP-required values where specified)
  7. Document tailoring decisions in SSP
  8. Develop Continuous Monitoring Strategy (CA-7 narrative)

Key outputs: Complete control selection documented in SSP; ConMon strategy;
             CIS; CRM.

Timeline estimate: 2–4 months (dependent on SSP writing resources)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 4 — IMPLEMENT
SP 800-53 Rev 5 (implementation guidance); FedRAMP SSP template
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Purpose: Implement all selected controls and document implementation in the SSP.

Key tasks for FedRAMP Moderate:
  1. Implement all system-specific controls (technical, operational,
     and management controls)
  2. Write SSP control narratives for all ~325 controls:
     - For each control: what is implemented, how it is implemented,
       who is responsible, what evidence exists
     - Address all control parameters (ODV values must be populated)
     - Distinguish base control from enhancements
  3. Complete SSP attachments:
     - System architecture diagrams (with authorization boundary marked)
     - Data flow diagrams (showing federal data flows)
     - Network diagrams (logical and physical)
     - Hardware/software inventory (CM-8)
     - Ports, protocols, services list
     - User types and privileges list
  4. Conduct internal pre-assessment / readiness review
  5. Complete POC for FedRAMP Readiness Assessment (optional but strongly
     recommended for first-time authorizations)

Key outputs: Completed SSP with all narratives; all SSP attachments;
             implemented controls with evidence artifacts.

Timeline estimate: 3–9 months (typically the longest phase for first ATO)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 5 — ASSESS
SP 800-53A Rev 5; FedRAMP SAP/SAR templates; 3PAO-conducted
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Purpose: Determine if controls are implemented correctly, operating as intended,
         and producing the desired outcome.

Key tasks for FedRAMP Moderate:
  1. 3PAO develops Security Assessment Plan (SAP) using SP 800-53A procedures
     and FedRAMP SAP template
  2. CSP reviews and approves SAP; agency AO concurs (for Agency ATO path)
  3. 3PAO conducts assessment using three methods:
     - EXAMINE: Review SSP, policies, procedures, configuration files,
       architecture diagrams, audit logs, training records
     - INTERVIEW: Discuss implementation with ISSO, system administrators,
       operators, developers, end users
     - TEST: Run vulnerability scans, penetration test, configuration checks,
       firewall rule reviews, backup restoration tests, access control tests
  4. 3PAO produces Security Assessment Report (SAR):
     - Executive summary with authorization recommendation
     - Control-by-control findings (Satisfied vs. Other Than Satisfied)
     - Risk ratings for OTS findings
     - Penetration test results
  5. CSP responds to SAR findings:
     - Remediate findings where possible before authorization
     - Open POA&M items for findings not yet remediated
  6. CSP develops initial POA&M from SAR OTS findings

FedRAMP-specific assessment requirements:
  - Annual penetration test using FedRAMP penetration test guidance
  - Automated vulnerability scanning results submitted to FedRAMP PMO
  - FedRAMP SAR template must be used
  - 3PAO independence must be maintained (no conflict of interest)

Key outputs: SAP; SAR; initial POA&M; 3PAO authorization recommendation.

Timeline estimate: 2–4 months (3PAO assessment engagement)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 6 — AUTHORIZE
SP 800-37 Rev 2, Chapter 2.7; FedRAMP authorization process
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Purpose: Senior official (Authorizing Official) formally accepts residual risk
         and grants or denies Authority to Operate.

Authorization package (submitted to AO or JAB):
  1. System Security Plan (SSP) — with all attachments
  2. Security Assessment Report (SAR) — produced by 3PAO
  3. Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) — with remediation timelines
  4. FedRAMP Authorization Package cover letter / executive summary

Agency ATO path:
  - CSP submits complete authorization package to agency AO
  - Agency reviews package (often with FedRAMP PMO support)
  - Agency AO makes authorization decision:
    * Authorization to Operate (ATO) — risk is acceptable; valid for 1 year
      (with ConMon) or up to 3 years
    * Denial of Authorization to Operate (DATO) — risk not acceptable;
      system must not process federal data until remediated
  - ATO letter issued; system listed as "FedRAMP Authorized" in Marketplace

JAB P-ATO path:
  - FedRAMP PMO conducts package review (kick-off through final package)
  - JAB technical reviewers assess the package
  - JAB issues Provisional ATO (P-ATO) if risk is acceptable
  - P-ATO can be leveraged by all federal agencies (reduces per-agency review)

Key FedRAMP authorization metrics (Moderate systems):
  - No unmitigated Critical or High risks at time of authorization
  - Open High POA&M items must have realistic remediation plans
  - AO accepts residual risk for all open POA&M items

Key outputs: ATO letter (Agency path) or P-ATO letter (JAB path);
             FedRAMP Marketplace listing updated to "Authorized."

Timeline estimate: 1–4 months (Agency ATO); 6–18 months (JAB P-ATO)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 7 — MONITOR
SP 800-37 Rev 2, Chapter 2.8; SP 800-137; FedRAMP ConMon Guide
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Purpose: Maintain ongoing situational awareness about the security posture;
         detect and respond to changes; sustain the ATO.

FedRAMP ConMon ongoing requirements:
  1. Monthly deliverables to FedRAMP PMO and agency customers:
     - Vulnerability scan results (OS, web app — monthly; DB — weekly)
     - Updated POA&M (with remediation status)
     - Updated system inventory
     - Updated CIS (Control Implementation Summary)
  2. Annual deliverables:
     - Annual assessment by 3PAO (all controls or a selected subset per
       FedRAMP ConMon Strategy guide)
     - Annual penetration test by 3PAO
     - Updated SSP (reflecting any significant changes)
     - Annual security review
  3. Significant Change Requests (SCRs):
     - Required before making significant changes to the authorization
       boundary, architecture, or security posture
     - CSP submits SCR to FedRAMP PMO; may trigger re-assessment
     - Examples: new cloud region, new external service, major architecture
       change, new data type
  4. Incident reporting:
     - US-CERT / CISA notification within 1 hour for major incidents
     - Agency customer notification per SLA / contract terms
     - Monthly operational reports to FedRAMP PMO
  5. Ongoing POA&M management:
     - Monthly updates required
     - Critical items: remediate within 30 days
     - High items: remediate within 90 days
     - Risk acceptances require ISSO, SO, and AO approval
  6. FedRAMP Marketplace status maintenance:
     - System remains "Authorized" only with current ConMon deliverables
     - FedRAMP PMO may revoke authorization for ConMon non-compliance

Continuous Authorization (preferred): Rather than periodic full re-authorization
every 3 years, FedRAMP encourages continuous authorization — the AO remains
continuously informed via ConMon deliverables and makes ongoing risk acceptance
decisions. This avoids the "big bang" re-authorization cycle.

Key outputs: Monthly ConMon deliverables; updated POA&M; annual 3PAO assessment;
             incident reports; SCRs as needed; sustained ATO status.

================================================================================
SECTION 4 — RECOMMENDED FEDRAMP MODERATE READINESS ROADMAP
================================================================================

Based on your ISO 27001:2022 certification, we recommend the following phased
approach:

Phase 1 — Prepare and Gap Analysis (Months 1–2):
  - Complete FedRAMP Marketplace registration
  - Engage 3PAO for FedRAMP Readiness Assessment (FRA)
  - Conduct FIPS 140 cryptographic module inventory
  - Assess PIV/MFA gaps with your IdP vendor
  - Download FedRAMP SSP template and begin scoping

Phase 2 — Remediate Critical Gaps (Months 3–8):
  - Implement FIPS 140-validated cryptographic modules throughout
  - Implement PIV/PIV-I authentication via federated IdP
  - Configure all ODVs to FedRAMP-required values
  - Stand up monthly vulnerability scanning program
  - Update IR policy with US-CERT reporting procedures
  - Begin writing SSP control narratives (can leverage ISO SoA as input)
  - Complete architecture diagrams and data flow diagrams

Phase 3 — SSP Completion and Pre-Assessment (Months 6–10):
  - Complete all 325+ SSP control narratives
  - Complete all SSP attachments
  - Conduct internal readiness review
  - Engage 3PAO for formal readiness assessment (optional but recommended)
  - Remediate readiness assessment findings

Phase 4 — 3PAO Assessment (Months 10–14):
  - 3PAO develops SAP and conducts full assessment
  - Respond to SAR findings; remediate where possible
  - Open POA&M for unresolved findings

Phase 5 — Authorization (Months 14–18):
  - Submit authorization package to agency AO or JAB
  - Respond to agency / FedRAMP PMO review questions
  - Receive ATO / P-ATO

Phase 6 — Sustain ConMon (Month 18+):
  - Begin monthly ConMon deliverables
  - Annual 3PAO assessment and pen test
  - Maintain POA&M; remediate findings on schedule

KEY REFERENCES
================================================================================

  NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5    — Security and Privacy Controls (control catalog)
  NIST SP 800-53B         — Control Baselines (Moderate baseline definition)
  NIST SP 800-53A Rev 5   — Assessment Procedures
  NIST SP 800-37 Rev 2    — Risk Management Framework
  NIST SP 800-60 Vol I/II — Information Types and Impact Levels
  NIST SP 800-63B         — Digital Identity (authenticator assurance)
  NIST SP 800-137         — Continuous Monitoring
  FIPS 140-3              — Cryptographic Module Validation Standard
  FIPS 199                — Standards for Security Categorization
  FIPS 200                — Minimum Security Requirements
  FedRAMP.gov             — SSP template, baseline documentation, 3PAO list,
                            ConMon guide, incident communications guide
  CMVP (csrc.nist.gov)    — Cryptographic Module Validation Program
  FedRAMP Marketplace     — marketplace.fedramp.gov (authorized CSPs, 3PAOs)

END OF RESPONSE
================================================================================
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4/5 assertions passed (80%)
✅Describes the ISO 27001 to SP 800-53 relationship including approximate control overlap
✅Identifies FIPS 140-2/3 cryptography as a gap (not covered by ISO 27001)
✅Identifies 3PAO requirement and US-CERT incident reporting as FedRAMP-specific gaps
❌Describes all 7 RMF steps (Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor) in context of FedRAMP
✅Distinguishes between Agency ATO and JAB P-ATO authorization paths
EXPERT RESPONSE: FedRAMP Moderate Authorization — SP 800-53, ISO 27001:2022 Mapping, Gap Analysis, and RMF Process

==============================================================================
1. RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN NIST SP 800-53 MODERATE AND ISO 27001:2022 ANNEX A
==============================================================================

Both NIST SP 800-53 (Rev. 5) at the Moderate baseline and ISO 27001:2022 Annex A are comprehensive security control frameworks, but they differ substantially in origin, philosophy, scope, and prescriptiveness.

STRUCTURAL OVERVIEW:

- SP 800-53 Rev. 5 Moderate baseline contains approximately 323 controls and control enhancements across 20 control families (AC, AT, AU, CA, CM, CP, IA, IR, MA, MP, PE, PL, PM, PS, PT, RA, SA, SC, SI, SR).
- ISO 27001:2022 Annex A contains 93 controls organized into 4 themes: Organizational Controls (37), People Controls (8), Physical Controls (14), and Technological Controls (34).

PHILOSOPHICAL DIFFERENCES:

SP 800-53 is prescriptive, parameter-driven, and process-oriented. It specifies not just what to do but frequently how to do it, with defined frequencies, technical standards, and federal-specific requirements (e.g., FIPS 140-3 validated cryptography, PIV/CAC for multi-factor authentication, FISMA reporting requirements). Controls include specific parameter values at the Moderate baseline — for example, AU-11 requires audit log retention for a defined period, and the Moderate baseline specifies 3 years for some categories.

ISO 27001:2022 Annex A is risk-driven and principle-based. Organizations select controls from Annex A based on a Statement of Applicability (SoA) derived from their risk assessment. There is significant flexibility in implementation. ISO 27001 does not mandate specific cryptographic standards, specific authentication mechanisms, or prescribe audit retention periods.

CONTROL FAMILY CORRESPONDENCE (HIGH LEVEL):

The following rough mappings exist between SP 800-53 families and ISO 27001:2022 Annex A themes:

Access Control (AC) <-> 5.15-5.18 (Identity/Access Management), 8.2-8.5 (Logical access controls), 8.18-8.20
Audit and Accountability (AU) <-> 8.15 (Logging), 8.16 (Monitoring activities), 5.33 (Protection of records)
Security Assessment and Authorization (CA) <-> 5.35 (Independent review), 5.36 (Compliance with policies), 8.8 (Management of technical vulnerabilities)
Configuration Management (CM) <-> 8.9 (Configuration management), 8.32 (Change management)
Contingency Planning (CP) <-> 5.30 (ICT readiness for business continuity), 8.13 (Information backup), 8.14 (Redundancy)
Identification and Authentication (IA) <-> 5.16 (Identity management), 5.17 (Authentication information), 8.5 (Secure authentication)
Incident Response (IR) <-> 5.24-5.28 (Incident management), 6.8 (Information security event reporting)
Maintenance (MA) <-> 8.6 (Capacity management) — partial; SP 800-53 MA is more specific to federal asset maintenance
Media Protection (MP) <-> 7.10 (Storage media), 8.10 (Information deletion)
Physical and Environmental Protection (PE) <-> 7.1-7.14 (Physical controls)
Planning (PL) <-> 5.1 (Policies), 5.37 (Documented operating procedures)
Program Management (PM) <-> 5.1-5.8 (Organizational controls, GRC program)
Personnel Security (PS) <-> 6.1-6.5 (People controls: screening, terms, awareness, disciplinary process, offboarding)
PII Processing and Transparency (PT) <-> 5.34 (Privacy and protection of PII) — partial
Risk Assessment (RA) <-> 5.7 (Threat intelligence), 8.8 (Vulnerability management), 6.1.2 (Information security risk assessment in the main standard)
System and Services Acquisition (SA) <-> 5.19-5.22 (Supplier relationships), 8.25-8.31 (Secure development)
System and Communications Protection (SC) <-> 8.20-8.26 (Network security, segregation, filtering, cryptography)
System and Information Integrity (SI) <-> 8.7 (Malware protection), 8.8 (Vulnerability management), 8.16 (Monitoring)
Supply Chain Risk Management (SR) <-> 5.19-5.22 (Supplier security), 5.20 (Addressing security in supplier agreements)

IMPORTANT DISTINCTION: ISO 27001:2022 Annex A maps reasonably well to SP 800-53 at a conceptual level, but the SP 800-53 Moderate baseline contains many controls with no meaningful ISO 27001 equivalent, particularly those related to federal compliance mandates (FISMA, FedRAMP-specific requirements, federal authentication standards, SCIF requirements, etc.).


==============================================================================
2. LIKELY GAPS MOVING FROM ISO 27001:2022 TO SP 800-53 MODERATE (FedRAMP)
==============================================================================

Organizations with ISO 27001:2022 certification will typically find they have a strong foundation but significant gaps in the following areas:

GAP CATEGORY 1: FEDERAL-SPECIFIC TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

- Cryptography (SC-13, SC-28): FedRAMP mandates FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules for all cryptographic operations. ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires "use of cryptography" but does not mandate FIPS validation. If your cloud service uses non-FIPS-validated libraries (e.g., standard OpenSSL builds, unvalidated TLS implementations), you must remediate this.

- Multi-Factor Authentication (IA-2, IA-2(1), IA-2(2)): FedRAMP Moderate requires MFA for all privileged accounts and remote access. More specifically, for federal information systems, PIV/CAC credentials are strongly preferred. ISO 27001 A.8.5 covers secure authentication but does not mandate specific mechanisms. You likely satisfy the general MFA requirement but may lack PIV/CAC capability.

- DNS Security (SC-20, SC-21, SC-22): FedRAMP requires DNSSEC implementation and validation. ISO 27001 does not address DNSSEC specifically.

- Email Security (SI-8): FedRAMP has specific requirements around anti-spam, anti-phishing (DMARC, DKIM, SPF) that go beyond ISO 27001's general malware protection controls.

GAP CATEGORY 2: AUTHORIZATION AND CONTINUOUS MONITORING

- Authority to Operate (CA-6): FedRAMP requires a formal ATO from an authorizing official (federal agency or JAB). There is no ISO 27001 equivalent — ISO 27001 certification is not an ATO. You must complete the full RMF authorization process.

- Continuous Monitoring (CA-7): FedRAMP's ConMon requirements are highly specific — monthly vulnerability scanning, annual penetration testing, ongoing POA&M management, monthly reporting to the sponsoring agency or FedRAMP PMO. ISO 27001 A.8.8 covers vulnerability management but does not mandate monthly scanning frequencies, specific reporting to government bodies, or the ConMon deliverable cadence.

- POA&M Management (CA-5): FedRAMP requires a formal Plan of Action and Milestones with tracked remediation timelines. While ISO 27001 involves corrective actions, the FedRAMP POA&M format, reporting cadence, and remediation deadline requirements (e.g., critical findings remediated within 30 days) are more prescriptive.

- Security Assessment (CA-2, CA-7, CA-8): FedRAMP requires assessments by a FedRAMP-authorized Third Party Assessment Organization (3PAO). Your ISO 27001 certification body is not a 3PAO and cannot perform FedRAMP assessments. Annual penetration testing (CA-8) to a defined scope is mandatory.

GAP CATEGORY 3: SUPPLY CHAIN AND EXTERNAL SERVICES

- Supply Chain Risk Management (SR family): SP 800-53 Rev. 5 introduced the SR control family with 12 controls covering supply chain risk plans, supply chain controls and processes, supplier reviews, and supply chain incident response. ISO 27001:2022 improved supplier security coverage in A.5.19-5.22, but SR controls go significantly further, including requirements for hardware and software acquisition controls, component authenticity verification (SR-11), and supply chain risk management plans (SR-2).

- External System Connections (CA-3): FedRAMP requires formal Interconnection Security Agreements (ISAs) or Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs) for all connections to external systems. This is more prescriptive than ISO 27001's general third-party access controls.

GAP CATEGORY 4: CONFIGURATION AND HARDENING

- Configuration Baselines (CM-6, CM-7): FedRAMP requires documented, implemented, and monitored configuration baselines aligned with USGCB (United States Government Configuration Baseline), DISA STIGs, or CIS Benchmarks. ISO 27001 A.8.9 covers configuration management but does not require alignment with federal configuration baselines.

- Least Functionality (CM-7): FedRAMP's interpretation of least functionality requires disabling all ports, protocols, and services not required for system operation. This level of rigor may exceed your current ISO 27001 implementation.

- Change Management (CM-3, CM-4): FedRAMP requires security impact analysis for all changes before implementation, with documented approval workflows. Your ISO 27001 change management may not capture the security impact analysis requirement explicitly.

GAP CATEGORY 5: INCIDENT RESPONSE AND REPORTING

- Incident Reporting Timelines (IR-6): FedRAMP requires reporting security incidents to US-CERT within 1 hour of discovery for major incidents, with specific reporting to the sponsoring agency. ISO 27001 A.6.8 covers event reporting but has no federal reporting obligation.

- Incident Response Testing (IR-3): FedRAMP requires annual IR tabletop exercises or simulations with documented results. While ISO 27001 encourages testing, the annual requirement and documentation expectations are more specific under FedRAMP.

GAP CATEGORY 6: AUDIT AND ACCOUNTABILITY

- Audit Log Content (AU-2, AU-3, AU-12): FedRAMP specifies required audit events and required audit record content in greater detail than ISO 27001 A.8.15. You may need to expand your audit event catalog and ensure audit records capture all required fields.

- Audit Log Protection (AU-9): FedRAMP requires cryptographic protection of audit logs and controls to prevent unauthorized modification. This may exceed current ISO 27001 implementations.

- Audit Reduction and Review (AU-6): FedRAMP requires regular review of audit logs with documented procedures. Automated audit review tools may be required at Moderate.

GAP CATEGORY 7: DOCUMENTATION AND EVIDENCE

- System Security Plan (PL-2): FedRAMP requires a comprehensive SSP documenting all 323+ Moderate controls, system boundaries, data flows, interconnections, and implementation details. This is a substantial documentation artifact with no ISO 27001 equivalent. Your ISMS documentation (policies, SOA, risk assessment) will inform the SSP but cannot substitute for it.

- Privacy Threshold Analysis / Privacy Impact Assessment (PT controls): FedRAMP requires privacy documentation including a PTA and potentially a full PIA if PII is processed. ISO 27001 A.5.34 covers PII protection but is less prescriptive.

- Data Flow Diagrams and Network Diagrams: FedRAMP requires detailed, up-to-date data flow diagrams (including data types, ports/protocols, encryption in transit markers) and network architecture diagrams as part of the SSP. These must be maintained as living documents.

GAP CATEGORY 8: PERSONNEL SECURITY

- Position Categorization and Screening (PS-1 through PS-9): FedRAMP requires position sensitivity designations and background checks aligned with OPM standards for personnel in sensitive roles. ISO 27001 A.6.1 covers screening but not to OPM standards. For cloud service providers, this typically applies to personnel with administrative access to the system.

GAP CATEGORY 9: AREAS WHERE ISO 27001:2022 LIKELY PROVIDES ADEQUATE COVERAGE

Your existing certification likely provides good coverage for:
- Physical security controls (PE family / ISO 27001 Annex A Section 7) — if your data centers are certified
- Policy and procedure documentation (PL, PM)
- User awareness and training (AT-2, AT-3 / ISO 27001 A.6.3)
- Business continuity and backup (CP-9, CP-10 / ISO 27001 A.8.13, A.5.30)
- General access control principles (AC / ISO 27001 A.8.2-8.5)
- Vulnerability management processes (RA-5, SI-2 / ISO 27001 A.8.8)
- Risk management program foundations (RA / ISO 27001 Clause 6.1)


==============================================================================
3. HOW THE RMF PROCESS WORKS FOR FedRAMP
==============================================================================

The Risk Management Framework (RMF), defined in NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2, provides the authorization process that FedRAMP uses. FedRAMP operationalizes RMF specifically for cloud service providers (CSPs) offering services to federal agencies.

FEDRAMP AUTHORIZATION PATHS:

There are three primary paths to FedRAMP authorization:
1. Agency Authorization: A specific federal agency sponsors the CSP, and the agency's Authorizing Official (AO) grants the ATO.
2. JAB Provisional ATO (P-ATO): The Joint Authorization Board (consisting of DOD, DHS, and GSA CISOs) grants a P-ATO, which other agencies can then leverage.
3. FedRAMP Authorization Act Path: More recent formalized agency authorization with PMO involvement.

For a first-time FedRAMP Moderate authorization, most CSPs pursue Agency Authorization unless their offering has very broad federal market potential warranting JAB pursuit.

RMF STEP 1 — CATEGORIZE (SP 800-60)

The system is categorized using FIPS 199 and SP 800-60 Volume II. Information types processed, stored, and transmitted by the system are identified and their confidentiality, integrity, and availability impact levels (Low, Moderate, High) are determined. The overall system impact level is the high-water mark across all three objectives.

For Moderate authorization: the system's overall impact level must be Moderate (at least one security objective is Moderate, none are High). FedRAMP provides categorization guidance in the SSP template.

Key deliverable: Completed FIPS 199 categorization documented in the SSP.

RMF STEP 2 — SELECT (SP 800-53)

The appropriate control baseline is selected based on categorization. For Moderate impact systems, the SP 800-53 Rev. 5 Moderate baseline is the starting point. FedRAMP has tailored this baseline in the FedRAMP Moderate baseline spreadsheet, which includes:
- Additional FedRAMP-specific parameter values
- Additional FedRAMP-required controls beyond the NIST baseline
- Guidance on control responsibilities (CSP-responsible, agency-responsible, shared)

The CSP identifies which controls are implemented, which are inherited from underlying infrastructure providers (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP FedRAMP-authorized IaaS/PaaS), and which are shared responsibility. This produces a Customer Responsibility Matrix (CRM).

Key deliverables: Selected control baseline, initial CRM, tailoring decisions documented in SSP.

RMF STEP 3 — IMPLEMENT

Controls are implemented across the system. For a cloud service, this involves:
- Implementing all CSP-responsible controls
- Inheriting controls from FedRAMP-authorized underlying providers (leveraging their existing P-ATOs)
- Documenting implementation details for every control in the SSP

The SSP is the primary output — a detailed document (often 300-500+ pages) describing the system boundary, all information types, data flows, interconnections, and implementation status and description for every applicable control.

Supporting artifacts include:
- System Security Plan (SSP)
- Customer Responsibility Matrix (CRM)
- Information System Contingency Plan (ISCP)
- Incident Response Plan (IRP)
- Configuration Management Plan (CMP)
- Control Implementation Summary (CIS)
- User Guide
- Privacy Threshold Analysis (PTA) / Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) if applicable

Key deliverable: Complete SSP package ready for assessment.

RMF STEP 4 — ASSESS (SP 800-53A)

A FedRAMP-authorized Third Party Assessment Organization (3PAO) conducts an independent assessment of control implementation. The 3PAO is selected by the CSP but must be on the FedRAMP authorized 3PAO list.

The 3PAO performs:
- Security Assessment Plan (SAP): Documents assessment scope, methodology, schedule, and test cases.
- Assessment Execution: Combination of interviews, document review, observation, and technical testing (including vulnerability scanning and penetration testing).
- Security Assessment Report (SAR): Documents findings, risk ratings (Critical, High, Moderate, Low, Informational), and recommendations. Each finding becomes either a Risk Acceptance or a POA&M item.

For Moderate systems, penetration testing must cover the entire authorization boundary and test for the OWASP Top 10, infrastructure vulnerabilities, and cloud-specific attack vectors.

The 3PAO assessment for a Moderate system typically takes 4-8 weeks for the assessment itself, with 2-4 weeks for the SAR.

Key deliverables: SAP, SAR, penetration test report.

RMF STEP 5 — AUTHORIZE

The authorization package is submitted to the Authorizing Official (agency AO for Agency Authorization, or JAB for P-ATO). The package includes:
- System Security Plan (SSP) and all supporting plans
- Security Assessment Report (SAR)
- Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M)
- Any additional agency-required documentation

The AO reviews the package, assesses residual risk, and makes an authorization decision:
- ATO (Authority to Operate): Granted, typically for 3 years
- ATO with Conditions: Granted with specific remediation requirements
- DATO (Denial of ATO): System may not operate

The FedRAMP PMO reviews the package before JAB authorization (for P-ATOs) or for Agency ATOs may review for completeness. The FedRAMP PMO does not make ATO decisions for Agency Authorizations — that authority rests with the agency AO.

POA&M: All open findings from the SAR with risk ratings of Low or above that are not accepted must be documented in the POA&M with remediation timelines. FedRAMP mandates: Critical findings remediated within 30 days, High within 30 days, Moderate within 90 days, Low within 180 days.

Key deliverable: ATO letter from the authorizing official.

RMF STEP 6 — MONITOR (Continuous Monitoring)

Post-authorization, FedRAMP's ConMon program requires ongoing activities:

Monthly:
- Vulnerability scanning of all operating system components, web applications, and databases
- Submission of updated POA&M to the sponsoring agency
- Submission of scan results to the sponsoring agency and FedRAMP PMO (for JAB P-ATOs)
- Inventory updates

Annually:
- Significant change assessments for any major changes to the system boundary, architecture, or controls
- Penetration testing (annual or as required by major changes)
- Review and update of all SSP documentation

Continuous:
- Incident reporting (within 1 hour for major incidents to US-CERT)
- Ongoing log monitoring and security event review
- Configuration baseline compliance monitoring
- Personnel security reviews

Significant Changes: Any change to the system boundary, data types, underlying infrastructure, or control implementations that may affect the security posture must be assessed. Significant changes may require a new 3PAO assessment of affected controls (called a Significant Change Request or SCR). Examples include: adding a new cloud region, migrating to a new database platform, changing authentication mechanisms, adding new data types.


==============================================================================
4. PRACTICAL RECOMMENDATIONS FOR YOUR FEDRAMP MODERATE JOURNEY
==============================================================================

Given your ISO 27001:2022 certification, here is a prioritized remediation and preparation approach:

PHASE 1 — FOUNDATION (Months 1-3):

1. Engage a FedRAMP-authorized 3PAO early to conduct a readiness assessment. This identifies your specific gaps before committing to a full assessment timeline.

2. Identify a federal agency sponsor if pursuing Agency Authorization. The agency must agree to sponsor and will have their own onboarding requirements.

3. Define your FedRAMP authorization boundary precisely. What is in scope? What underlying infrastructure will you inherit controls from? Ensure your IaaS/PaaS providers have existing FedRAMP authorizations.

4. Map your existing ISO 27001 controls to SP 800-53 Moderate controls using NIST's SP 800-53 to ISO 27001 crosswalk (NIST Interagency Report 8011 and SP 800-53B appendices provide mapping guidance). This reveals your actual coverage and prioritizes gaps.

5. Begin SSP development. The SSP template is available from FedRAMP.gov. Start populating system boundary, information types, and high-confidence controls immediately.

PHASE 2 — GAP REMEDIATION (Months 3-9):

1. Achieve FIPS 140-3 validated cryptography across all cryptographic operations. This often requires library upgrades and may affect system performance — plan accordingly.

2. Implement federal configuration baselines (USGCB/STIG/CIS) for all applicable components. This is often the most time-consuming technical remediation.

3. Establish ConMon tooling and processes: automated vulnerability scanning (e.g., Tenable Nessus, Qualys), SIEM for log aggregation and monitoring, POA&M tracking system.

4. Develop all required documentation artifacts: ISCP, IRP, CMP, PTA/PIA, Rules of Behavior, user guides, CIS/CRM.

5. Address supply chain risk management gaps — develop an SR program, conduct supplier reviews, implement SCRM plan.

6. Conduct internal tabletop exercises for incident response and contingency plan testing.

PHASE 3 — 3PAO ASSESSMENT (Months 9-15):

1. Conduct a formal readiness assessment with your 3PAO before the full assessment. This is optional but strongly recommended for first-time authorizations.

2. Remediate readiness assessment findings.

3. Execute formal 3PAO assessment (SAP execution, penetration testing, SAR development).

4. Develop POA&M for all SAR findings. Remediate Critical and High findings before submitting the authorization package.

5. Submit authorization package to agency AO.

TIMELINE EXPECTATION: First-time FedRAMP Moderate authorizations typically take 12-18 months from kickoff to ATO for well-prepared organizations. ISO 27001 certification shortens this by establishing foundational processes but does not eliminate the FedRAMP-specific work described above.

COST CONSIDERATION: 3PAO assessment costs for Moderate systems typically range from $250,000 to $500,000+ depending on system complexity. Internal preparation costs (personnel, tooling, documentation) can equal or exceed assessment costs.


==============================================================================
5. KEY RESOURCES
==============================================================================

- FedRAMP.gov: Authorization packages, SSP templates, 3PAO list, ConMon guidance
- NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Control catalog and Moderate baseline
- NIST SP 800-53B: Control baselines (Moderate baseline definition)
- NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2: RMF process
- NIST SP 800-53A Rev. 5: Assessment procedures
- NIST SP 800-60 Vol. II: Information type categorization
- FIPS 199: Security categorization standards
- FIPS 200: Minimum security requirements
- FedRAMP Moderate Baseline Spreadsheet (FedRAMP.gov): Parameter values, additional requirements
- NIST IR 8011: Automation of SP 800-53 controls (assessment automation guidance)
- FedRAMP Continuous Monitoring Strategy Guide (FedRAMP.gov)

==============================================================================

Response generated: 2026-05-07
Knowledge basis: General expert knowledge of NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5, FedRAMP Moderate requirements, ISO 27001:2022, and NIST RMF (SP 800-37 Rev. 2)
Prompt: We are a US SaaS company that sells an AI-powered CV screening and candidate ranking tool to European employers. It filters job applications and ranks candidates based on skills and predicted fit. We plan to launch in Germany and France in Q1 2026. Does this fall under the EU AI Act, and what are our obligations?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Correctly identifies system as high-risk under Annex III Area 4 (recruitment/candidate selection) via Art. 6(2)
✅Lists at least 4 key provider obligations from Arts. 9-17 (e.g., risk management, data governance, human oversight, conformity assessment)
✅States the 2 August 2026 compliance deadline for Annex III high-risk systems
✅Mentions CE marking (Art. 48) and EU AI database registration (Art. 49) requirements
✅Identifies the correct conformity assessment path: self-assessment (Annex VI) for Annex III Area 4 systems (Points 2-8)
