(detecting pathologies, anomalies, or conditions), infers from inputs (medical images and
patient data) how to generate outputs (diagnostic predictions or recommendations) that
affect real environments (clinical decisions and patient care). The threshold is met.

====================================================================================
STEP 3: PROHIBITED PRACTICES SCREEN (ART. 5 — IN FORCE FROM 2 FEBRUARY 2025)
====================================================================================

None of the eight prohibited practices under Article 5 apply to a standard diagnostic
imaging AI tool used for radiology support:

- No subliminal manipulation (Art. 5(1)(a))
- No exploitation of vulnerabilities (Art. 5(1)(b))
- No social scoring (Art. 5(1)(c))
- No predictive criminal profiling (Art. 5(1)(d))
- No untargeted biometric database scraping (Art. 5(1)(e))
- No prohibited workplace emotion inference (Art. 5(1)(f))
- No sensitive-attribute biometric categorisation (Art. 5(1)(g))
- No real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces for law enforcement (Art. 5(1)(h))

The system is not prohibited and may proceed to risk classification.

====================================================================================
STEP 4: RISK TIER DETERMINATION — HIGH-RISK VIA ART. 6(1), ANNEX I PATH
====================================================================================

This system is HIGH-RISK under BOTH available paths under Article 6.

PATH A — ART. 6(1): SAFETY COMPONENT OF AN ANNEX I PRODUCT (PRIMARY PATH)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Article 6(1) classifies an AI system as high-risk when two cumulative conditions are
satisfied:

Condition 1: The AI system is a safety component of a product (or is itself such a
product) covered by Annex I harmonisation legislation.

SATISFIED: Annex I, Section A, Item 11 of the EU AI Act explicitly lists "Medical
devices — Regulation 2017/745 (EU MDR)" as Annex I harmonisation legislation. Your
diagnostic imaging AI tool has been CE-marked under EU MDR as a Class IIa medical
device. Your vendor additionally describes it as a "safety component for your radiology
department." Either characterisation satisfies this limb: a CE-marked medical device
under the MDR is per se an Annex I product, and the vendor's own characterisation as a
safety component confirms the safety role.

Condition 2: The product must require third-party conformity assessment under the
relevant Annex I legislation.

SATISFIED: Under EU MDR Regulation 2017/745, Class IIa medical devices require
involvement of a notified body in the conformity assessment procedure. This is a
mandatory third-party assessment requirement — the self-certification route is not
available for Class IIa devices. The second condition is therefore met.

Conclusion on Path A: Both conditions under Article 6(1) are met. The AI diagnostic
imaging tool is definitively a HIGH-RISK AI system under the EU AI Act.

PATH B — ART. 6(2), ANNEX III: INDEPENDENT ALTERNATIVE BASIS
-------------------------------------------------------------
Even without the Annex I path, Annex III, Area 5(a) provides an independent basis for
high-risk classification: "AI systems intended to be used by or on behalf of competent
authorities or public authorities for the purpose of evaluating the eligibility of natural
persons for public assistance benefits and services, including healthcare." Where the
hospital is an NHS-equivalent or publicly funded hospital, this path may also apply.
However, the Annex I path under Article 6(1) is the primary and more certain
classification route here.

====================================================================================
STEP 5: DOES THE CE MARK UNDER EU MDR COVER YOU UNDER THE EU AI ACT?
====================================================================================

THE SHORT ANSWER: NO — THE MDR CE MARK DOES NOT SUBSTITUTE FOR AI ACT COMPLIANCE,
BUT IT IS INTEGRATED INTO (NOT SEPARATE FROM) THE AI ACT CONFORMITY PROCEDURE.

Here is the precise legal position:

1. INTEGRATION, NOT REPLACEMENT (ART. 43(3) AND RECITAL 88)
   For AI systems that are safety components of Annex I products, Article 43(3) of the
   AI Act provides that the conformity assessment of the AI system shall be carried out
   as part of the conformity assessment procedure applicable under the relevant Annex I
   legislation — in this case, the EU MDR procedure. The notified body that assessed
   the medical device under EU MDR therefore also forms part of the AI Act conformity
   pathway. There is no entirely separate AI Act notified body assessment for these
   systems.

   However — and this is the critical point — this integration means the MDR conformity
   assessment procedure must be conducted in a manner that ALSO covers compliance with
   the AI Act's Section 2 requirements (Arts. 9–15). The existing MDR CE certification
   alone, conducted before the AI Act applied, does not accomplish this. The notified
   body would need to have assessed compliance with the AI Act's specific requirements
   (risk management system under Art. 9, data governance under Art. 10, technical
   documentation under Art. 11, logging under Art. 12, transparency/instructions for use
   under Art. 13, human oversight under Art. 14, accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity under
   Art. 15) as part of or alongside the MDR assessment.

2. TIMING: AI ACT OBLIGATIONS FOR ANNEX I SAFETY COMPONENTS APPLY FROM 2 AUGUST 2027
   The AI Act's high-risk obligations for Annex I product safety components (including
   medical devices under EU MDR) do not apply until 2 August 2027 — one year later than
   the 2 August 2026 date for Annex III systems. This gives the vendor and your hospital
   additional time, but planning must begin now.

3. SEPARATE EU AI ACT DECLARATION OF CONFORMITY REQUIRED
   Article 47 requires a separate EU Declaration of Conformity for AI Act compliance.
   This is distinct from the EU Declaration of Conformity under the MDR. The AI Act DoC
   must specifically confirm compliance with all applicable AI Act requirements and must
   be maintained for 10 years from market placement. Your vendor must draw this up.

4. CE MARKING CARRIES DUAL MEANING BUT DUAL REQUIREMENTS
   After completing AI Act-compliant conformity assessment, the CE marking affixed under
   Article 48 of the AI Act signals conformity with both the MDR and the AI Act. The
   same physical CE mark is used, but the legal basis is dual: the vendor must have
   satisfied both regulatory frameworks. A CE mark obtained solely under the MDR before
   AI Act applicability is insufficient to signal AI Act conformity.

5. EU AI DATABASE REGISTRATION (ART. 49)
   The vendor, as provider, must register the AI system in the EU AI database before
   market placement (for Annex I systems, this registration obligation also applies).
   MDR registration (EUDAMED) is a separate database and does not substitute for EU AI
   database registration.

====================================================================================
STEP 6: PROVIDER OBLIGATIONS — WHAT YOUR VENDOR MUST DO
====================================================================================

As the hospital, you should verify that your vendor has met or has a compliant plan to
meet the following obligations before 2 August 2027 (the applicability date for Annex I
safety component systems):

ART. 9 — RISK MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
The vendor must establish and maintain a continuous, lifecycle-spanning risk management
system covering all known and reasonably foreseeable risks to patients, clinical staff,
and third parties. This must include five documented steps: risk identification, risk
estimation/evaluation, post-market monitoring integration, risk mitigation (design-first
hierarchy), and residual risk acceptability assessment. Testing against pre-defined
metrics before market placement is required.

ART. 10 — DATA AND DATA GOVERNANCE
Training, validation, and testing datasets must be representative of the patient
populations and imaging conditions the system will encounter in your radiology department.
Bias examination and gap identification are mandatory. If special-category health data
was used to detect/correct bias in training, the vendor must satisfy all six conditions
of the Article 10(5) exception.

ART. 11 — TECHNICAL DOCUMENTATION (ANNEX IV)
Comprehensive technical documentation per Annex IV must be created before market
placement and kept up to date throughout the system's lifecycle. This must include
the full description of the system, training methodologies, data used, validation
results, risk management documentation, and all changes made over the lifecycle.
Retention period: 10 years from market placement.

ART. 12 — AUTOMATIC LOGGING CAPABILITY
The system must be technically capable of automatically generating event logs throughout
operation. For a diagnostic imaging tool, this means logging should capture the
circumstances surrounding outputs (image inputs, model version, output confidence scores,
timestamps) to enable post-incident reconstruction and audit.

ART. 13 — TRANSPARENCY AND INSTRUCTIONS FOR USE
The vendor must provide your radiology department with clear instructions for use
covering: the system's intended purpose, performance metrics (accuracy, robustness),
known limitations, human oversight measures, input data specifications, and logging
mechanisms. These instructions are the basis on which your hospital's deployer
obligations rest.

ART. 14 — HUMAN OVERSIGHT DESIGN
The system must be designed to allow radiologists and clinical staff to:
- Understand the system's capabilities and limitations
- Recognise automation bias (over-reliance on AI output without independent clinical
  judgment)
- Correctly interpret AI outputs — including interpretability or explainability features
  showing which image features drove a finding
- Override or disregard AI outputs
- Intervene via a stop/interrupt mechanism

ART. 15 — ACCURACY, ROBUSTNESS, AND CYBERSECURITY
The vendor must declare and maintain appropriate accuracy levels throughout the system's
operational lifetime. Robustness against errors, image quality variations, and
foreseeable misuse is required. Cybersecurity protections must address data poisoning,
model poisoning, adversarial image attacks, and confidentiality attacks.

ART. 16 — FULL PROVIDER CHECKLIST
The vendor must complete all 12 provider obligations, including: compliance with Arts.
9–15; quality management system (Art. 17); technical documentation maintenance; EU
Declaration of Conformity (Art. 47); CE marking (Art. 48); and EU AI database
registration (Art. 49).

ART. 17 — QUALITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
The vendor must have a documented QMS covering 13 components, including regulatory
compliance strategy, design control, testing and validation, data management, risk
management, post-market monitoring, and serious incident reporting procedures. Existing
MDR QMS requirements (ISO 13485) can be adapted and integrated to avoid duplication,
but must be supplemented to cover AI Act-specific elements.

ART. 72 — POST-MARKET MONITORING SYSTEM
The vendor must establish a post-market monitoring system proportionate to the risks,
collecting and analysing performance data from deployed systems throughout operational
lifetime. Existing MDR post-market surveillance requirements can be integrated into
this system (Recital 88 and Art. 72 permit integration with sectoral post-market
monitoring obligations).

====================================================================================
STEP 7: DEPLOYER OBLIGATIONS — WHAT YOUR HOSPITAL MUST DO (ART. 26)
====================================================================================

As the deployer, your hospital bears the following independent obligations under Article
26, applicable from 2 August 2027 for Annex I systems (note: some general obligations
take effect on the broader 2 August 2026 date if the system is also classifiable under
Annex III):

1. INSTRUCTIONS COMPLIANCE (ART. 26(1))
   Use the AI diagnostic imaging tool only in accordance with the vendor's instructions
   for use (Art. 13). Do not use the system for purposes, patient populations, or imaging
   modalities outside those specified by the vendor.

2. STAFF COMPETENCE AND OVERSIGHT ASSIGNMENT (ART. 26(2) AND ART. 14(4))
   Assign human oversight of AI outputs to radiologists or clinical staff with the
   necessary competence, authority, and resources. Train oversight personnel on the
   system's capabilities and limitations, and specifically on automation bias risk —
   the tendency to over-rely on AI outputs without independent clinical assessment.

3. INPUT DATA CONTROL (ART. 26(3))
   Where your hospital controls the input data (e.g., image acquisition protocols,
   patient preparation, PACS integration), ensure input data is relevant and
   sufficiently representative. If your patient population differs materially from the
   training dataset described in the vendor's technical documentation, flag this to the
   vendor and assess whether additional validation is needed.

4. CONTINUOUS MONITORING (ART. 26(4))
   Monitor the operation of the AI system in your radiology department. If you identify
   risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights arising from actual use, you must
   notify the vendor/importer/distributor AND the relevant market surveillance authority.
   Where appropriate, suspend use pending investigation.

5. SERIOUS INCIDENT REPORTING (ART. 26(5) AND ART. 73)
   Immediately notify the vendor if the AI tool is involved in a serious incident —
   meaning an event that directly or indirectly leads or could lead to death, serious
   damage to health, or serious and irreversible harm. Additionally notify the market
   surveillance authority of the Member State where the incident occurred. For a hospital,
   this interacts with your existing MDR vigilance reporting obligations; Art. 72 and
   Recital 88 contemplate integration of these reporting channels.

6. LOG RETENTION (ART. 26(6))
   Retain automatically generated logs of the AI system's operation for a minimum of
   6 months. Where applicable Union or national law (including MDR post-market
   surveillance requirements) imposes longer retention periods, the longer period applies.

7. GDPR DPIA (ART. 26(9))
   Conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment under GDPR Article 35 before deploying
   the system if the processing of health data (which is special-category data under
   GDPR Article 9) presents a high risk to patient rights. For a diagnostic imaging AI
   processing identifiable patient health data, a DPIA is almost certainly required and
   should be completed before deployment.

8. FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ART. 27)
   If your hospital is a public authority or body, Art. 27 may require a Fundamental
   Rights Impact Assessment before deploying high-risk AI systems. This assessment covers:
   the processes in which the system will be used, the time period and frequency,
   categories of persons affected, specific risks to those persons, and planned human
   oversight measures. Even where Art. 27 does not apply as a legal mandate (e.g., for
   private hospitals), conducting a FRIA-equivalent assessment is strongly recommended
   given the vulnerability of the patient population.

====================================================================================
STEP 8: PRACTICAL COMPLIANCE ACTION PLAN
====================================================================================

| Requirement                        | Article      | Who        | Deadline        | Action Required                                                                                    |
|------------------------------------|--------------|------------|-----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Prohibited practices screen         | Art. 5       | Both       | Already applies | Confirm tool does not fall within any of the 8 prohibited categories (confirmed above: it does not)|
| Verify vendor AI Act readiness      | Arts. 9–17   | Hospital   | Before deploy   | Request confirmation from vendor that AI Act-compliant conformity assessment is planned/completed  |
| AI Act EU Declaration of Conformity | Art. 47      | Vendor     | 2 Aug 2027      | Vendor must draw up AI Act DoC separate from MDR DoC                                              |
| EU AI database registration         | Art. 49      | Vendor     | 2 Aug 2027      | Vendor must register system before market placement/service in AI Act database (not EUDAMED alone)|
| CE marking (dual basis)             | Art. 48      | Vendor     | 2 Aug 2027      | Vendor must affix CE marking reflecting AI Act conformity (integrated with MDR procedure)          |
| Staff training on AI oversight      | Arts. 14, 26 | Hospital   | Before deploy   | Train radiologists on system limits, automation bias, override capabilities                        |
| Instructions for use review         | Art. 13      | Hospital   | Before deploy   | Review vendor instructions; confirm use is within intended purpose and patient population           |
| Log retention procedures            | Art. 26(6)   | Hospital   | Before deploy   | Establish procedure to retain AI system logs for minimum 6 months                                  |
| Continuous monitoring protocol      | Art. 26(4)   | Hospital   | Ongoing         | Establish clinical governance process to monitor AI performance and flag anomalies                  |
| Incident reporting procedure        | Art. 73      | Hospital   | Before deploy   | Define clear escalation pathway: AI incident → vendor → market surveillance authority              |
| GDPR DPIA                          | Art. 26(9)   | Hospital   | Before deploy   | Complete DPIA for processing of patient health data through diagnostic AI system                   |
| FRIA (if public body)              | Art. 27      | Hospital   | Before deploy   | If public hospital, complete Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment                                   |
| Post-market monitoring integration  | Art. 72      | Both       | 2 Aug 2027      | Integrate AI Act post-market monitoring with existing MDR post-market surveillance programme        |
| QMS review                         | Art. 17      | Vendor     | 2 Aug 2027      | Vendor must supplement existing ISO 13485 QMS with AI Act-specific components                      |

====================================================================================
STEP 9: SPECIFIC ANSWER ON CE MARKING — WHAT IT COVERS AND WHAT IT DOES NOT
====================================================================================

Your existing CE mark under EU MDR (Regulation 2017/745) demonstrates that:
- The diagnostic imaging AI tool has undergone notified body review as a Class IIa
  medical device
- The device meets EU MDR safety and performance requirements for medical devices
- The device's clinical performance and safety have been assessed under MDR Annex IX
  or XI procedures

Your existing CE mark does NOT demonstrate that:
- The AI system complies with AI Act Article 9 (risk management system for AI risks)
- The AI system complies with AI Act Article 10 (AI-specific data governance)
- The AI system was assessed against AI Act Article 11/Annex IV (AI technical
  documentation requirements — which go beyond MDR technical file requirements)
- The AI system has automatic logging capability compliant with Art. 12
- The instructions for use meet Art. 13 requirements (which include AI-specific
  transparency requirements such as automation bias warnings and oversight instructions)
- The system is designed with Art. 14 human oversight mechanisms
- The system has been assessed for AI-specific cybersecurity risks under Art. 15
- The vendor has drawn up an AI Act EU Declaration of Conformity (Art. 47)
- The system is registered in the EU AI Act database (Art. 49)

The pathway forward under Art. 43(3) is that the MDR notified body assessment is
INTEGRATED into the AI Act conformity procedure — not replaced by it. Your vendor must
work with their notified body to extend the conformity assessment scope to cover the
AI Act's specific requirements, produce the AI Act Declaration of Conformity, and
register in the EU AI database. This is additional work on top of the MDR CE marking,
not a duplication of it.

====================================================================================
STEP 10: PENALTIES FOR NON-COMPLIANCE
====================================================================================

Failure to comply with AI Act obligations for providers and deployers can result in
fines under Article 99:

- Violations of provider/deployer obligations (Arts. 16, 26, etc.): up to €15,000,000
  or 3% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher.
- Incorrect or misleading information provided to authorities: up to €7,500,000 or 1%
  of total worldwide annual turnover.

Market surveillance authorities in each Member State have enforcement powers including
requiring corrective action, withdrawal from service, and recalls (Art. 74). The MDR's
existing market surveillance infrastructure will interface with AI Act enforcement for
medical device AI systems.

====================================================================================
CONCLUSION
====================================================================================

Your hospital's AI diagnostic imaging tool is unambiguously HIGH-RISK under the EU AI
Act via Article 6(1) — it is a safety component of a product (Class IIa medical device
under EU MDR, an Annex I law) that requires third-party conformity assessment. High-risk
obligations for Annex I safety components apply from 2 AUGUST 2027.

The CE mark under EU MDR is NOT sufficient to cover EU AI Act compliance. It is
integrated into the AI Act conformity pathway (Art. 43(3)) but does not replace the
additional AI Act-specific requirements: AI risk management (Art. 9), data governance
(Art. 10), AI technical documentation (Art. 11/Annex IV), automatic logging (Art. 12),
AI-specific transparency/instructions for use (Art. 13), human oversight design (Art. 14),
AI accuracy and cybersecurity assessment (Art. 15), AI Act EU Declaration of Conformity
(Art. 47), and EU AI database registration (Art. 49).

As the deployer, your hospital must: use the system per vendor instructions, assign
competent human oversight and train for automation bias, retain logs for at least 6
months, monitor operations and report serious incidents, conduct a GDPR DPIA, and
(if a public body) a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment.

Engage your vendor now to confirm their AI Act compliance roadmap, obtain contractual
commitments regarding their provider obligations, and begin building your internal
governance framework for AI oversight in your radiology department. The 2 August 2027
deadline allows time for preparation, but the governance infrastructure — training,
monitoring protocols, incident reporting pathways, and DPIA — should be in place at or
before initial deployment.
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4/5 assertions passed (80%)
❌Correctly identifies the system as high-risk via Annex I Path A (Art. 6(1)) because Regulation 2017/745 (Medical Devices) is listed in Annex I Section A
✅Explains that MDR CE marking does NOT automatically satisfy AI Act compliance requirements
✅Explains that AI Act requirements (Arts. 9-17) must be integrated into the existing MDR conformity assessment procedure
✅States the 2 August 2027 deadline specifically for Annex I safety component systems
✅Identifies hospital deployer obligations under Art. 26 (instructions compliance, staff competence, monitoring, log retention)
EU AI ACT COMPLIANCE ANALYSIS: AI DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING TOOL IN A HOSPITAL SETTING
CE-Marked Class IIa Medical Device (EU MDR 2017/745)

================================================================================
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
================================================================================

Yes, you likely need to take additional steps beyond your existing CE marking under the EU MDR. The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689, in force from August 2024, with most provisions applying from August 2026) establishes a parallel, complementary regulatory layer that interacts with — but does not fully replace — your existing medical device obligations. Whether the CE marking already covers you depends critically on which conformity assessment route was used and whether it was specifically updated to address the AI Act's requirements.

================================================================================
1. CLASSIFICATION OF YOUR AI SYSTEM UNDER THE EU AI ACT
================================================================================

1.1 High-Risk AI System Classification

Your AI diagnostic imaging tool almost certainly qualifies as a HIGH-RISK AI SYSTEM under the EU AI Act. Annex III of the Regulation lists categories of AI systems presumed to be high-risk. Point 5(a) of Annex III explicitly includes:

  "AI systems intended to be used as safety components of products, or as products themselves, covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I..."

EU MDR (Regulation 2017/745) is listed in Annex I of the EU AI Act. Since your tool:
  (a) Is covered by EU MDR as a Class IIa medical device, AND
  (b) The vendor designates it as a "safety component" of your radiology department's operations,

...it falls squarely within the high-risk category under Annex III, Point 5.

1.2 What "Safety Component" Means

The EU AI Act defines a safety component as a component of a product or system that fulfils a safety function for that product or system, or the failure or malfunctioning of which endangers the health and safety of persons. In diagnostic imaging AI, failures (false negatives missing cancer, false positives triggering unnecessary procedures) directly affect patient safety. The vendor's characterization appears legally sound and works against any argument that this system is lower-risk.

1.3 No Exemption for Already CE-Marked Devices

There is no blanket exemption in the EU AI Act for AI systems that are already CE-marked under EU MDR. The two frameworks operate in parallel.

================================================================================
2. THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN EU MDR CE MARKING AND THE EU AI ACT
================================================================================

2.1 The "Fast-Track" Pathway Under Article 8 and Annex I

The EU AI Act creates a mechanism for AI systems that are already subject to EU harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I (which includes EU MDR). Under Article 8(1), high-risk AI systems that are already covered by those sector-specific laws may meet some EU AI Act requirements through compliance with those sector-specific laws — BUT ONLY IF:

  - The existing sector legislation covers the same objectives and requirements as the corresponding EU AI Act obligations; AND
  - The conformity assessment was conducted by a Notified Body and already addresses AI-specific risks.

This is NOT a full exemption. It is a potential streamlining mechanism, often called the "single conformity assessment" pathway.

2.2 What EU MDR Does and Does Not Cover

EU MDR (2017/745) already requires, for Class IIa devices:
  - Risk management systems (ISO 14971)
  - Clinical evaluation and post-market surveillance
  - Quality management systems (typically ISO 13485)
  - Technical documentation
  - Notified Body involvement in conformity assessment

However, EU MDR does not fully address all EU AI Act requirements. Specific AI Act obligations NOT typically covered by EU MDR alone include:
  - Transparency and explainability requirements for AI-specific decision logic
  - Human oversight mechanisms specific to AI systems (as opposed to general device supervision)
  - Data governance requirements specific to training, validation, and test datasets
  - Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity requirements framed in AI-specific terms
  - AI system logging and auditability of AI decisions (immutable logs of AI outputs)
  - Conformity with technical specifications for AI accuracy and bias mitigation
  - Post-market monitoring specifically targeting AI model drift and retraining events

2.3 The "Presumption of Conformity" Does Not Apply Automatically

Your existing CE mark does not automatically create a presumption of conformity with the EU AI Act's requirements. The AI Act's presumption of conformity mechanisms relate to adherence to harmonized standards developed under the AI Act (e.g., by CEN/CENELEC) or to common specifications adopted by the European Commission — most of which were still under development as of mid-2025.

================================================================================
3. OBLIGATIONS UNDER THE EU AI ACT THAT APPLY TO YOU AS A DEPLOYER
================================================================================

The EU AI Act distinguishes between "providers" (those who develop and place the AI system on the market) and "deployers" (those who use the AI system in a professional context). As a hospital, you are a DEPLOYER. However, deployers of high-risk AI systems have significant, non-trivial obligations.

3.1 Deployer Obligations Under Article 26

As a deployer you must:

  a) USE THE SYSTEM AS INTENDED: Only use the AI system in accordance with the provider's instructions of use. Any use outside the intended purpose may transfer provider obligations to you.

  b) IMPLEMENT HUMAN OVERSIGHT: Designate individuals with the necessary competence, authority, and resources to exercise meaningful human oversight over the AI system. In a radiology context, this means ensuring qualified radiologists can understand, question, and if necessary override AI outputs. Human oversight must be real and not merely nominal.

  c) MONITOR FOR ANOMALIES: Conduct ongoing monitoring to detect unexpected performance or outputs inconsistent with the system's intended purpose. You must have processes in place to detect when the system may be operating outside its validated parameters (e.g., patient population drift, equipment changes affecting image quality).

  d) SUSPEND USE WHEN RISKS ARISE: If you identify a risk to health, safety, or fundamental rights not addressed by the provider, you must inform the provider and, if necessary, suspend use. You must have a defined protocol for this.

  e) MAINTAIN LOGS: To the extent technically possible, ensure the keeping of automatically generated logs. You must retain logs for at least six months (or as otherwise required by EU or national law, which may be longer in healthcare contexts).

  f) INFORM AFFECTED PERSONS: Natural persons (patients) subject to decisions from the AI system have the right to be informed that they are subject to an AI system. You must have a mechanism for disclosing AI involvement to patients and, upon request, providing a meaningful explanation of the AI's role in their care pathway.

  g) CONDUCT A FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS IMPACT ASSESSMENT (FRIA): Under Article 27, deployers that are bodies governed by public law OR operators of critical infrastructure (hospitals may qualify as both) must conduct a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment before deploying the high-risk AI system. This assessment must:
     - Describe the processes in which the AI system will be used
     - Identify the categories of natural persons affected
     - Assess specific risks to fundamental rights (non-discrimination, dignity, privacy)
     - Identify measures to address those risks
     - Be documented and registered

  h) REGISTER IN THE EU DATABASE: Under Article 49 and the EU AI Act's database provisions, deployers that are public bodies must register their use of high-risk AI systems in the EU-wide database (managed by the European AI Office). Private hospitals may have analogous obligations depending on how "public body" is interpreted in your jurisdiction.

3.2 Operator Obligations That May Shift to You

If you materially modify the AI system (change its purpose, retrain it, significantly alter its use environment beyond what the provider envisioned), you may assume provider obligations — including full conformity assessment obligations. Carefully document the scope of your deployment to avoid inadvertently becoming a "provider" under the AI Act.

================================================================================
4. OBLIGATIONS THAT REMAIN WITH THE VENDOR (PROVIDER)
================================================================================

While your vendor bears primary responsibility for the AI system's conformity as a high-risk AI system, you should verify that they have fulfilled their obligations, because deploying a non-compliant system exposes you to enforcement risk.

Confirm with your vendor that they have:

  a) Registered the AI system in the EU database for high-risk AI systems (Article 49)
  b) Drawn up EU Declaration of Conformity specifically under the AI Act (not just under MDR)
  c) Affixed the CE marking in compliance with AI Act requirements (noting that for Annex I products, the AI Act CE marking is folded into the existing CE mark but must specifically cover AI Act compliance)
  d) Established a post-market monitoring system for the AI model specifically, tracking model performance, drift, and incidents under AI Act Article 72
  e) Provided you with instructions of use that specifically address AI Act requirements: intended purpose, performance levels, known limitations, foreseeable misuse scenarios, human oversight measures, cybersecurity requirements
  f) Established an EU-based representative if they are a non-EU provider
  g) Implemented a quality management system covering the AI system's lifecycle

================================================================================
5. TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS YOU SHOULD VERIFY
================================================================================

5.1 Accuracy, Robustness, and Cybersecurity (Article 15)

The AI system must be designed and developed to achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity throughout its lifecycle. You should verify with your vendor:
  - The validated performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) on representative European patient populations
  - How the system performs on subpopulations (age, sex, ethnicity, equipment type) — bias and fairness testing
  - Adversarial robustness provisions
  - Cybersecurity measures protecting the AI model from manipulation

5.2 Data Governance

The training, validation, and test datasets must have met requirements for relevance, representativeness, and freedom from errors. Request evidence from your vendor that:
  - Training data included representative patient populations for your clinical use cases
  - Data governance practices met EU AI Act Article 10 standards
  - Any known data limitations are disclosed in the instructions of use

5.3 Logging and Auditability

Ensure your IT infrastructure can capture and retain the logs that the AI system automatically generates. These logs must be capable of tracing AI outputs back to specific inputs (patient images) and must be kept for the legally required period. In healthcare, this likely means integration with your RIS/PACS and conformance with your clinical records retention policies.

================================================================================
6. TRANSITIONAL PROVISIONS AND TIMELINE
================================================================================

The EU AI Act entered into force on 1 August 2024. For high-risk AI systems covered by Annex III (which includes your system), the compliance deadline is 2 August 2026. However:

  - Prohibited AI practices: Applied from 2 February 2025
  - GPAI model obligations: Applied from 2 August 2025
  - High-risk Annex III systems (your category): 2 August 2026
  - High-risk Annex I systems (products under harmonisation law): 2 August 2027

IMPORTANT DISTINCTION: Your system likely falls under BOTH Annex I (as an EU MDR product) and Annex III (as an AI safety component). Where a system is covered by both, the more specific classification should be applied, but regulatory guidance from the European AI Office should be consulted. The 2027 deadline for Annex I products may apply, but you should not assume the later deadline applies without verifying your system's precise classification.

For systems already on the market before the AI Act compliance dates, transitional provisions in Article 111 may provide additional time, subject to conditions (no significant changes to the system after the relevant dates).

================================================================================
7. INTERACTION WITH OTHER EU LAW
================================================================================

7.1 GDPR

AI diagnostic imaging involves special category health data under GDPR. Your AI Act compliance must be coordinated with your GDPR obligations, particularly:
  - Legal basis for processing health data in AI systems
  - Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) under GDPR Article 35 (likely mandatory for high-risk AI processing health data at scale)
  - Rights of data subjects including the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions (Article 22 GDPR) — important where AI influences clinical decisions
  - Data retention and deletion obligations

7.2 EU MDR Post-Market Surveillance

Your EU MDR post-market surveillance plan should be updated to specifically monitor AI-specific failure modes: model drift, dataset shift, performance degradation on new equipment, and unexpected error patterns. This aligns with but goes beyond the AI Act's post-market monitoring requirements.

7.3 NIS2 Directive

Hospitals are designated as "essential entities" under NIS2. Your AI system introduces cybersecurity considerations that must be addressed in your NIS2 compliance framework, particularly regarding supply chain security (your vendor as a critical supplier) and incident reporting for AI system failures.

================================================================================
8. PRACTICAL STEPS YOU SHOULD TAKE NOW
================================================================================

IMMEDIATE ACTIONS (Within 3-6 Months):

1. Obtain documentation from your vendor confirming their AI Act compliance roadmap and whether they have or will obtain EU Declaration of Conformity under the AI Act in addition to EU MDR CE marking.

2. Review the vendor's instructions of use specifically for AI Act-required content (human oversight measures, performance limits, known risks).

3. Designate a responsible person internally (could be your medical AI governance lead, Chief Medical Information Officer, or equivalent) with oversight responsibility for AI Act compliance.

4. Conduct or commission a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA) under Article 27 if you are a public hospital or operate as critical infrastructure.

5. Map your existing EU MDR technical file and quality management system to the EU AI Act's high-risk AI system requirements to identify gaps.

SHORT-TERM ACTIONS (6-12 Months):

6. Establish or update your human oversight protocols for AI-assisted radiology: define the radiologist's supervisory role, decision authority, and documentation requirements when AI outputs are used.

7. Implement log retention policies for AI system outputs, integrated with your clinical records management.

8. Develop a patient information and transparency mechanism (consent documentation, patient-facing notices) disclosing AI involvement in diagnostics.

9. Update your IT vendor management framework to treat your AI vendor as a high-risk supplier under both AI Act and NIS2.

10. Register your use of the AI system in the EU database (if applicable for deployers in your jurisdiction).

MEDIUM-TERM ACTIONS (12-24 Months):

11. Implement ongoing performance monitoring: establish thresholds for acceptable AI performance, protocols for suspending use if performance degrades, and a clear escalation path to notify the vendor.

12. Coordinate your AI Act compliance with your GDPR DPIA, NIS2 security obligations, and EU MDR post-market surveillance plan into a unified AI governance framework.

13. Train radiology staff on AI Act obligations: their role in human oversight, incident reporting, and documentation requirements.

14. Establish contractual protections with your vendor: require notification of model updates, retraining events, performance changes, and regulatory status changes; require cooperation in your incident reporting obligations.

================================================================================
9. DOES THE CE MARKING ALREADY COVER YOU?
================================================================================

DIRECT ANSWER: No, not fully.

Your existing CE marking under EU MDR:
  - DOES demonstrate conformity with medical device safety and performance requirements
  - DOES involve Notified Body assessment for a Class IIa device
  - DOES address risk management, clinical evaluation, and quality management
  - MAY overlap with some EU AI Act technical requirements where the MDR requirements are equivalent

Your existing CE marking DOES NOT:
  - Constitute a Declaration of Conformity under the EU AI Act
  - Address AI-specific transparency, explainability, and logging requirements
  - Cover your deployer obligations under the AI Act (FRIA, human oversight protocols, patient information)
  - Automatically register the system in the EU AI Act database
  - Satisfy the AI Act's specific accuracy, robustness, and bias testing requirements as framed in the AI Act

WHAT THE VENDOR SHOULD PROVIDE: The vendor should be updating their conformity assessment and technical documentation to cover EU AI Act requirements, and should issue a supplementary or updated Declaration of Conformity that specifically addresses AI Act compliance in addition to EU MDR conformity. If your vendor claims the existing CE mark is sufficient without any additional steps, that claim should be examined very carefully and legal/regulatory advice sought.

================================================================================
10. KEY RISKS OF NON-COMPLIANCE
================================================================================

- Market surveillance authorities (national competent authorities for AI in each Member State) can require corrective actions, withdrawal, or prohibition of use
- Administrative fines under the AI Act for providers: up to EUR 15 million or 3% of global annual turnover for violations of high-risk AI system obligations; up to EUR 30 million or 6% for prohibited AI practice violations
- Fines for deployers are determined by Member States and may be significant
- Patient safety incidents involving non-compliant AI systems create liability exposure under both the AI Act and EU MDR
- Reputational risk in a regulated healthcare environment
- Potential conflict with your hospital's accreditation requirements if AI governance is found inadequate

================================================================================
CONCLUSION
================================================================================

Your AI diagnostic imaging tool is almost certainly a high-risk AI system under the EU AI Act, and the existing CE marking under EU MDR does not alone satisfy the EU AI Act's requirements. You face a dual regulatory framework: continue meeting EU MDR obligations while also building out the additional governance, oversight, documentation, and transparency infrastructure required by the AI Act. The compliance deadline for your category of system is 2 August 2026 (or possibly 2027 for Annex I products — seek guidance on this point).

You should engage your vendor immediately to confirm their AI Act compliance roadmap, begin your internal gap assessment, and conduct a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment. Given the clinical stakes and regulatory complexity, engaging a regulatory affairs specialist with specific EU AI Act and EU MDR expertise is strongly recommended.

================================================================================
DISCLAIMER
================================================================================

This analysis is based on general knowledge of the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) and EU MDR (Regulation 2017/745) as of early 2026. It does not constitute legal advice. Regulatory guidance, harmonized standards, and implementing acts under the EU AI Act continue to evolve. You should consult qualified legal counsel and regulatory affairs professionals for advice specific to your situation and jurisdiction.
Prompt: We are building a customer-facing chatbot for our e-commerce website. It can answer product questions, process returns, and occasionally recommend products based on browsing history. It will be powered by a third-party GPAI model via API. What are our EU AI Act obligations as the deployer, and do we need to disclose it is an AI?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Correctly classifies the chatbot as limited risk (not high-risk) under Art. 50 — not falling under Annex III
✅Correctly states Art. 50(1) disclosure obligation: deployers must inform users they interact with an AI system
✅Notes that an e-commerce chatbot is not 'obviously AI' to a reasonably well-informed person and therefore disclosure is required
✅Distinguishes deployer obligations (Art. 26, Art. 50) from provider/GPAI provider obligations (Art. 53)
✅States the 2 August 2026 application date for Art. 50 limited-risk transparency requirements
EU AI Act Compliance Analysis: E-Commerce Customer-Facing Chatbot
Regulation (EU) 2024/1689

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 1 — SCOPE AND ROLE IDENTIFICATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

You are a DEPLOYER under Art. 3(4): you are using an AI system under your own authority in a professional context (operating an e-commerce business and placing the chatbot in front of customers). You are not developing or placing an AI system on the market under your own name — the underlying GPAI model is developed and placed on the market by your third-party API provider, who holds the role of PROVIDER under Art. 3(3).

This deployer/provider split is critical: the EU AI Act assigns distinct and largely non-overlapping obligation sets to each role. You bear deployer obligations; your third-party GPAI model vendor bears provider and GPAI obligations.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 2 — AI SYSTEM AND GPAI CLASSIFICATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Your chatbot is an AI system within the meaning of Art. 3(1): it is a machine-based system that infers from inputs (user queries, browsing history) how to generate outputs (product answers, return processing decisions, product recommendations) that affect real environments (customer purchasing behaviour and satisfaction).

The underlying third-party model accessed via API is almost certainly a GPAI model under Art. 3(63) — it is trained on large amounts of data using self-supervision at scale, displays significant generality, and performs a wide range of distinct tasks. The chatbot you build on top of it is a GPAI system under Art. 3(64).

This GPAI classification has compliance implications for your vendor (not for you), which are addressed in Step 7 below.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 3 — PROHIBITED PRACTICES SCREEN (Art. 5 — in force from 2 February 2025)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Running through all eight prohibited categories:

Art. 5(1)(a) — Subliminal/manipulative techniques: A standard e-commerce chatbot answering product questions and processing returns does not deploy subliminal techniques. However, the product recommendation function warrants scrutiny. Recommendations based on browsing history that are honest (showing products genuinely relevant to the user's interests) are not manipulative. Recommendations designed to exploit psychological weaknesses, create false urgency, or use hidden persuasion techniques could cross into this prohibition. Ensure your prompting and recommendation logic does not direct the model to use deceptive or manipulative tactics.

Art. 5(1)(b) — Exploitation of vulnerabilities: Not applicable for a standard product chatbot, provided you do not target users based on vulnerability characteristics (age, disability, socioeconomic circumstances).

Art. 5(1)(c) — Social scoring: Not applicable. Your chatbot does not evaluate or classify persons based on social behaviour over time to make consequential decisions in unrelated social contexts.

Art. 5(1)(d) — Predictive criminal risk assessment: Not applicable.

Art. 5(1)(e) — Untargeted facial recognition database creation: Not applicable.

Art. 5(1)(f) — Emotion inference in workplace or educational institutions: Not applicable to a customer-facing e-commerce chatbot.

Art. 5(1)(g) — Biometric categorization for sensitive attributes: Not applicable, provided your chatbot does not process biometric data.

Art. 5(1)(h) — Real-time remote biometric identification: Not applicable.

CONCLUSION: No prohibited practices are engaged, provided you avoid manipulative recommendation techniques. The system may lawfully be deployed in the EU.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 4 — RISK TIER DETERMINATION (Art. 6)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

High-Risk Path A (Art. 6(1) — Annex I safety component): Your chatbot is not a safety component of any product covered by harmonised sectoral legislation (machinery, medical devices, civil aviation, etc.). Path A does not apply.

High-Risk Path B (Art. 6(2) — Annex III listed use case): Review of all eight Annex III areas:

- Area 1 (Biometrics): No biometric identification, categorization, or emotion recognition. Not engaged.
- Area 2 (Critical infrastructure): Not a safety component of road traffic, water, energy, or digital infrastructure management. Not engaged.
- Area 3 (Education): Not applicable to an e-commerce context.
- Area 4 (Employment/workers): Not used for recruitment, promotion, termination, or performance evaluation of workers. Not engaged.
- Area 5 (Essential services — creditworthiness, insurance, benefits): This is the most important area to examine for your chatbot. The chatbot answers questions, processes returns, and makes product recommendations. It does not evaluate creditworthiness (Area 5(b)), assess insurance risk (Area 5(c)), determine eligibility for public assistance (Area 5(a)), or dispatch emergency services (Area 5(d)). Not engaged for standard functions.
- Area 6 (Law enforcement): Not applicable.
- Area 7 (Migration/border control): Not applicable.
- Area 8 (Justice and democratic processes): Not applicable.

IMPORTANT CAVEAT — Art. 6(3) profiling override: If your chatbot's recommendation engine performs "profiling" within the meaning of Art. 3(52) — automated processing of personal data to evaluate natural persons' preferences, behaviour, or economic situation — this warrants legal attention. However, profiling alone does not make a system high-risk under Art. 6(3); it is only an override when the system is already within an Annex III category. Since your chatbot does not fall within any Annex III area, the profiling override does not elevate it to high-risk.

RISK TIER CONCLUSION: Your e-commerce chatbot is LIMITED RISK under Art. 50.

It is NOT high-risk. The primary legal obligation is transparency under Art. 50(1) (chatbot disclosure), with potential additional GDPR obligations given the processing of browsing history and personal data.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 5 — YOUR OBLIGATIONS AS DEPLOYER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Because your chatbot is limited-risk (not high-risk), the full Art. 26 deployer obligation regime (which applies only to high-risk systems) does not apply. Your obligations are as follows:

A. ART. 50(1) — MANDATORY AI DISCLOSURE (applies from 2 August 2026)

This is your primary obligation and it is unambiguous.

Art. 50(1) requires that deployers of AI systems intended to interact with natural persons — chatbots — ensure that users are informed they are interacting with an AI system. This disclosure must be made:
- At the latest at the time of first interaction
- In a clear and distinguishable manner
- In a format meeting accessibility standards under Directive 2019/882

The only exception is when it is "obvious to a reasonably well-informed user given the context and circumstances." A customer-facing chatbot on an e-commerce website does NOT meet this standard — not all customers will assume they are speaking with an AI, and the law does not permit that assumption to substitute for actual disclosure. You must disclose.

Practical implementation:
- Display a clear message at the start of every chat session, such as: "You are chatting with an AI assistant."
- Do not give the chatbot a name that implies it is a human (e.g., "Hi, I'm Sarah from customer support").
- The disclosure does not need to reveal which AI model powers the chatbot, only that the user is interacting with an AI system.

B. GDPR — DATA PROTECTION OBLIGATIONS (concurrent, applies now)

Your chatbot processes personal data (browsing history, return requests, purchase history). GDPR applies in full, independently of the AI Act. Key obligations:
- Art. 13/14 GDPR: Inform users in your privacy notice that their data is processed by an AI system for personalisation purposes, what data is used, the legal basis, and their rights.
- Art. 6 GDPR: Establish a valid legal basis for processing browsing history for recommendations. Consent (Art. 6(1)(a)) or legitimate interests (Art. 6(1)(f)) are the most likely bases; legitimate interests requires a balancing test.
- Art. 22 GDPR: If return decisions are made fully automatically without meaningful human review and produce legal or similarly significant effects on customers, Art. 22 may apply, requiring either human review, a right to contest, or explicit consent.
- Art. 35 GDPR: Consider whether a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is required, particularly for large-scale profiling of individuals based on browsing history.

C. VOLUNTARY BEST PRACTICES (no mandatory obligation but strongly recommended)

Even though high-risk obligations (Arts. 9–17, 26) do not apply, the following practices are commercially prudent and may be required by your contractual relationship with your GPAI API vendor:
- Maintain usage policies consistent with the provider's terms of service
- Implement input/output monitoring to detect misuse or harmful outputs
- Establish an escalation path for complex queries or complaints that routes to a human agent
- Document your risk assessment concluding the system is limited-risk (this is good practice under Art. 6(3) documentation norms and provides a defence if ever challenged)


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 6 — CONFORMITY ASSESSMENT AND CE MARKING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Conformity assessment (Art. 43), CE marking (Art. 48), and EU AI database registration (Art. 49) are PROVIDER obligations for high-risk systems. Since your chatbot is limited-risk and you are the deployer (not the provider of the underlying model), NONE of these obligations fall on you.

Your third-party GPAI model vendor (the provider) handles these obligations for their model. You should verify in your contract that your vendor confirms their model is compliant with applicable AI Act requirements as they come into force.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 7 — GPAI OBLIGATIONS (your vendor's obligations, not yours — but relevant contractually)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The GPAI obligations under Arts. 51–55 fall on the model PROVIDER (your API vendor), not on you as deployer. However, these obligations matter to you because:

Art. 53 — Universal GPAI obligations (your vendor must comply from 2 August 2025):
- Produce technical documentation (Annex XI)
- Make available to downstream system providers (you) information about capabilities and limitations enabling your own compliance — this is the Annex XII downstream information obligation
- Maintain a copyright compliance policy
- Publish a training data summary

You should, as a downstream system provider building on the GPAI model via API, request and obtain the Annex XII information from your vendor. This information helps you understand the limitations of the model you are deploying, which is relevant to accurate user communications and responsible use.

Art. 51/55 — Systemic risk GPAI: If your vendor's model was trained on compute exceeding 10²⁵ FLOPs (which major frontier models may meet), it may be subject to additional Art. 55 obligations (model evaluation, adversarial testing, serious incident reporting to the AI Office, cybersecurity). Again, these fall on the vendor. However, you should ask whether your vendor has designated systemic-risk status, as this affects the robustness of their compliance programme and your own supply-chain risk.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 8 — POST-MARKET MONITORING AND INCIDENT REPORTING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Formal post-market monitoring (Art. 72) and serious incident reporting to market surveillance authorities (Art. 73) are mandatory only for HIGH-RISK AI system providers. Since your chatbot is limited-risk, these are not mandatory obligations.

However, as a responsible deployer, you should:
- Monitor chatbot outputs regularly for harmful, inaccurate, or misleading responses
- Maintain a feedback mechanism for customers to flag problematic interactions
- If a serious incident does occur (e.g., automated return decisions causing customer harm), your contractual agreement with your vendor should establish notification pathways


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ANSWER TO THE DISCLOSURE QUESTION: YES — YOU MUST DISCLOSE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Art. 50(1) is unambiguous: deployers of chatbots (AI systems intended to interact with natural persons) must ensure users are informed they are interacting with an AI system, unless it is obvious from context. A customer chatbot on an e-commerce site does not meet the "obvious" exception — disclosure is mandatory.

The disclosure requirement applies from 2 August 2026.

You do NOT need to:
- Name the specific model or vendor powering the chatbot
- Provide technical details about how the AI works
- Obtain consent for the AI interaction itself (though GDPR consent or legitimate interests applies to the data processing involved)

You DO need to:
- Clearly state at the start of each chat that the user is speaking with an AI
- Make this disclosure before the user has provided substantive input
- Meet accessibility standards in the disclosure format


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

COMPLIANCE SUMMARY TABLE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Requirement              | Article        | Applies To You? | Applies From    | Action Required
-------------------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------------------------
AI disclosure to users   | Art. 50(1)     | YES             | 2 Aug 2026      | Add clear "You are chatting with an AI" message at session start
Prohibited practices     | Art. 5         | YES             | 2 Feb 2025      | Verify recommendation logic avoids manipulation
GDPR compliance          | GDPR           | YES             | Now             | Privacy notice, legal basis, DPIA assessment
Risk management system   | Art. 9         | NO (provider)   | —               | Vendor obligation; request confirmation via contract
Technical documentation  | Art. 11        | NO (provider)   | —               | Vendor obligation
Conformity assessment    | Art. 43        | NO (provider)   | —               | Vendor obligation
CE marking               | Art. 48        | NO (provider)   | —               | Vendor obligation
EU AI database reg.      | Art. 49        | NO (provider)   | —               | Vendor obligation
Annex XII info request   | Art. 53        | Indirect        | 2 Aug 2025      | Request capabilities/limitations info from GPAI vendor
Post-market monitoring   | Art. 72        | NO (mandatory)  | —               | Recommended voluntarily
Serious incident report  | Art. 73        | NO (mandatory)  | —               | Establish internal escalation and vendor notification process
Human oversight          | Art. 14        | NO (mandatory)  | —               | Recommended: human escalation path for complex cases


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PENALTIES FOR NON-COMPLIANCE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Violations of Art. 50 (including failure to disclose AI interaction) are deployer/provider violations falling under Art. 99's second tier: up to €15,000,000 or 3% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. For SMEs and startups, the lower of the fixed amount or percentage applies.

Violations of Art. 5 prohibited practices: up to €35,000,000 or 7% of global annual turnover.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KEY IMMEDIATE ACTIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

NOW (pre-August 2026):
1. Audit your recommendation logic for any manipulative design patterns that could trigger Art. 5(1)(a) — this prohibition applies from 2 February 2025.
2. Complete GDPR compliance: update privacy notice to disclose AI-powered personalisation, establish legal basis for browsing history processing, assess DPIA need.
3. Review your GPAI vendor contract: ensure it requires them to provide Annex XII downstream information by 2 August 2025 and commits them to ongoing AI Act compliance.

BY 2 AUGUST 2026:
4. Implement Art. 50(1) AI disclosure: display a clear notification at the start of every chat session confirming the user is interacting with an AI system.
5. Conduct and document a risk classification assessment confirming your chatbot is limited-risk (not high-risk), retaining it in case of regulatory enquiry.

ONGOING:
6. Monitor chatbot outputs for quality, accuracy, and potential harms.
7. Maintain an accessible human escalation pathway for return disputes and complex queries.
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Correctly classifies the chatbot as limited risk (not high-risk) under Art. 50 — not falling under Annex III
✅Correctly states Art. 50(1) disclosure obligation: deployers must inform users they interact with an AI system
✅Notes that an e-commerce chatbot is not 'obviously AI' to a reasonably well-informed person and therefore disclosure is required
✅Distinguishes deployer obligations (Art. 26, Art. 50) from provider/GPAI provider obligations (Art. 53)
✅States the 2 August 2026 application date for Art. 50 limited-risk transparency requirements
EU AI ACT COMPLIANCE OBLIGATIONS FOR AN E-COMMERCE CHATBOT DEPLOYER
=====================================================================

SCENARIO SUMMARY
----------------
- System: Customer-facing chatbot on an e-commerce website
- Functions: Answering product questions, processing returns, recommending products based on browsing history
- Underlying technology: Third-party General Purpose AI (GPAI) model accessed via API
- Role in the AI value chain: You are the DEPLOYER (not the provider/developer of the underlying AI model)

---

PART 1: UNDERSTANDING YOUR ROLE UNDER THE EU AI ACT
----------------------------------------------------

The EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, which entered into force on 1 August 2024, with most provisions applying from 2 August 2026) distinguishes between several actors in the AI value chain:

- PROVIDER: The entity that develops or places an AI system on the market (in your case, the GPAI model vendor, e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.)
- DEPLOYER: An entity that uses an AI system under its own authority for a specific purpose (THIS IS YOU — you are integrating the GPAI model into your e-commerce chatbot and deploying it to customers)
- IMPORTER / DISTRIBUTOR: Not directly relevant here

As the DEPLOYER, your obligations differ from those of the PROVIDER. However, you bear significant responsibilities, particularly around risk management, transparency, and human oversight.

---

PART 2: RISK CLASSIFICATION OF YOUR CHATBOT
--------------------------------------------

The EU AI Act establishes a tiered risk framework. Correctly classifying your system is the first step.

2.1 Is it a Prohibited AI Practice?
The chatbot as described does NOT fall under any of the prohibited practices listed in Article 5 (e.g., subliminal manipulation, social scoring, real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces). You are safe on this front.

2.2 Is it High-Risk AI?
High-risk AI systems are listed in Annex III of the Act. The categories most relevant to e-commerce include:
- Biometric categorisation systems
- AI used in employment and workers management
- AI used for access to essential private services and public services / benefits

A standard e-commerce chatbot handling product queries, returns, and product recommendations does NOT ordinarily fall into the Annex III high-risk categories. The product recommendation engine based on browsing history is a profiling activity but does not constitute a "high-risk" AI system under the Act's current Annex III definitions, as it is not making consequential decisions about access to essential services, creditworthiness, or similar impactful areas.

IMPORTANT CAVEAT: If your chatbot were extended to make credit decisions (e.g., buy-now-pay-later eligibility) or employment screening, the risk classification would change. Stick to the described scope and you are outside the high-risk tier.

CONCLUSION ON RISK TIER: Your chatbot most likely falls into the LIMITED RISK or MINIMAL RISK category.

---

PART 3: TRANSPARENCY OBLIGATIONS (LIMITED RISK — ARTICLES 50 AND 52)
---------------------------------------------------------------------

Even though your chatbot is not high-risk, it is subject to TRANSPARENCY OBLIGATIONS under Article 50 of the EU AI Act. This is the most immediately actionable set of obligations for you.

3.1 Mandatory AI Disclosure — Article 50(1): THE ANSWER IS YES, YOU MUST DISCLOSE

Article 50(1) states: "Providers shall ensure that AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons are designed and developed in such a way that the natural persons concerned are informed that they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the circumstances or the context of use."

As a DEPLOYER, Article 50(1) also imposes an obligation on you (since you are deploying this system to interact with customers). The disclosure obligation applies to chatbots by design.

Specifically:
- You MUST inform users they are talking to an AI, not a human
- This disclosure must be made BEFORE or AT THE START of the interaction, not buried in terms and conditions
- The disclosure must be clear and intelligible
- Exception: If it is "obvious from the circumstances" — however, regulators and guidance suggest this exception is narrow; you should not assume obviousness and should always disclose proactively

3.2 What the Disclosure Must Say
The Act does not mandate a precise form of words, but the disclosure must:
- Be in plain language
- Be prominent (not hidden in a footer or FAQ)
- Clearly indicate the user is interacting with an AI system
- Ideally, name it as a chatbot or AI assistant

Example compliant disclosure: "Hi! I'm [Brand Name]'s AI assistant. How can I help you today?" or a persistent label such as "AI-Powered Assistant" visible throughout the conversation.

3.3 Prohibition on Impersonating Humans — Article 50(3)
Article 50(3) prohibits AI systems from impersonating real persons or creating the false impression of being human. Your chatbot must not:
- Claim to be a human customer service agent
- Use a human name without clearly indicating it is AI (e.g., "Hi, I'm Sarah" without disclosing Sarah is an AI)
- Deny being an AI when directly asked by a user

This is one of the strongest obligations in the Act for chatbot operators.

3.4 Emotion Recognition and Biometric Categorisation
If your chatbot uses any emotion recognition (e.g., sentiment analysis to flag frustrated customers) or biometric categorisation, additional transparency requirements apply under Article 50(3)-(4). These likely do not apply to the described scenario, but review your technical stack carefully.

---

PART 4: OBLIGATIONS SPECIFICALLY FOR DEPLOYERS — ARTICLE 26
------------------------------------------------------------

Article 26 of the EU AI Act sets out obligations for deployers. Even for a limited-risk or minimal-risk system, the following considerations apply:

4.1 Use in Accordance with Instructions
You must use the AI system in accordance with the provider's instructions of use (the GPAI model provider's terms of service and usage policies). If the provider has specified permissible use cases, you must not operate outside those use cases.

4.2 Human Oversight
You must implement appropriate measures to enable human oversight of the AI system's outputs, particularly:
- Monitoring for harmful, incorrect, or misleading outputs
- Establishing processes for customers to escalate to a human agent
- Reviewing chatbot interactions for quality and accuracy

4.3 Data Governance and Logging
You should maintain logs of interactions to the extent necessary to monitor system behaviour and investigate complaints. This also intersects with GDPR obligations (see Part 7 below).

4.4 Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA)
Article 27 requires deployers of HIGH-RISK AI systems to conduct a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA) before deployment. Since your chatbot is not high-risk, a formal FRIA is not legally required. However, it is best practice to consider potential impacts on consumer rights, privacy, and non-discrimination before launch.

---

PART 5: GPAI MODEL — YOUR RELATIONSHIP WITH THE PROVIDER
----------------------------------------------------------

Because you are using a third-party GPAI model via API, you need to understand the obligations that flow upstream.

5.1 GPAI Provider Obligations (Not Yours Directly)
Under Articles 53-55, GPAI providers (the model developers) must:
- Maintain technical documentation
- Have a copyright compliance policy
- Publish a sufficiently detailed summary of training data (for transparency to the public)
- If the model is "systemic risk" GPAI (very large models, >10^25 FLOPs training compute): additional obligations including adversarial testing, incident reporting, cybersecurity measures

These obligations fall on the GPAI provider (e.g., your API vendor), not on you as the deployer of an application built on top of that model.

5.2 What You Should Do As Deployer Using a GPAI Model
- Review the GPAI provider's documentation and ensure their model is compliant with the EU AI Act (they should be able to provide assurances or documentation to this effect)
- Review the provider's Terms of Service — ensure your use case is permitted
- Ensure you have a contract with the provider that clearly defines their responsibilities under the EU AI Act vs. your responsibilities
- Obtain and review any model cards, transparency summaries, or capability disclosures the GPAI provider publishes

5.3 Liability Allocation
The EU AI Act creates a division of liability. If a GPAI provider fails to meet their obligations (e.g., inadequate documentation), liability may rest with them. However, if you deploy the system in a manner that exceeds the provider's specified instructions or in a prohibited context, liability shifts to you.

---

PART 6: PRODUCT RECOMMENDATIONS BASED ON BROWSING HISTORY — PROFILING CONSIDERATIONS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The product recommendation feature involves processing browsing history data to personalise recommendations. This is a form of automated profiling. Key considerations:

6.1 Under EU AI Act
Automated profiling for product recommendations on an e-commerce site does not by itself trigger high-risk classification, as it does not make decisions with significant legal or similarly significant effects on individuals (compare to credit scoring or insurance pricing). However:
- You must be transparent about this automated personalisation (also required under GDPR — see below)
- If the recommendation engine were to affect access to pricing, discounts, or services in a discriminatory way, the risk profile could change

6.2 Non-Discrimination
AI-driven recommendations must not discriminate against users on the basis of protected characteristics (race, gender, religion, etc.) under applicable EU law. While the EU AI Act's non-discrimination provisions are most stringent for high-risk systems, you should ensure your recommendation model is not producing discriminatory outputs.

---

PART 7: INTERSECTION WITH GDPR
--------------------------------

The EU AI Act does not replace GDPR — both apply simultaneously. For your e-commerce chatbot, GDPR implications include:

7.1 Legal Basis for Processing
- Browsing history used for recommendations: Typically requires consent (GDPR Article 6(1)(a)) or legitimate interests assessment (Article 6(1)(f)) — but consent is strongly preferred for this type of profiling
- Conversation logs: May be processed under contract performance or legitimate interests, depending on purpose

7.2 Right to Explanation / Right Not to Be Subject to Automated Decisions
GDPR Article 22 applies where decisions are made "solely by automated means" and produce "legal or similarly significant effects." Product recommendations on an e-commerce site typically do not reach this threshold, but if the chatbot were used to make decisions about user accounts, credit, or access, Article 22 rights would be triggered.

7.3 Privacy Notice Updates
Your privacy notice must disclose:
- That an AI chatbot is used
- The nature of the personal data processed by the chatbot (conversation content, browsing history)
- Any third-party GPAI provider that processes personal data (as a data processor)
- Retention periods for conversation logs

7.4 Data Processing Agreement (DPA)
You must have a GDPR-compliant Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with your GPAI API provider, since they will process personal data contained in user queries on your behalf (Article 28 GDPR).

---

PART 8: TIMELINE AND APPLICABILITY
------------------------------------

Key EU AI Act timeline dates:
- 1 August 2024: Act entered into force
- 2 February 2025: Prohibited AI practices (Article 5) became applicable
- 2 August 2025: Obligations for GPAI providers (Articles 53-55) became applicable
- 2 August 2026: Most remaining obligations, including deployer obligations and transparency obligations (Article 50), become fully applicable
- 2 August 2027: High-risk AI system obligations for certain legacy systems

For a new deployment in 2026 or beyond, you need to be compliant with Articles 26 and 50 from day one of your launch.

If you are already operating such a chatbot, you should be working toward compliance now, particularly on the transparency/disclosure requirements which are straightforward to implement.

---

PART 9: PRACTICAL COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST FOR YOUR CHATBOT
--------------------------------------------------------

MUST-DO (Legal Requirements):

[ ] 1. AI DISCLOSURE: Display a clear disclosure at the start of every conversation that the user is interacting with an AI system. Example: "You're chatting with our AI assistant."

[ ] 2. NO HUMAN IMPERSONATION: Ensure the chatbot does not claim or imply it is a human. If given a name (e.g., "Aria"), clearly label it as an AI assistant.

[ ] 3. HONEST RESPONSE TO "ARE YOU AN AI?": Program the chatbot to always acknowledge it is an AI when directly asked. Never deny being an AI.

[ ] 4. USE WITHIN PROVIDER TERMS: Review and comply with the GPAI API provider's acceptable use policy. Ensure your use case (returns processing, recommendations) is permitted.

[ ] 5. HUMAN ESCALATION PATH: Provide users with a clear and accessible way to speak with a human agent. This supports the human oversight requirement.

[ ] 6. DATA PROCESSING AGREEMENT: Execute a GDPR-compliant DPA with your GPAI API provider.

[ ] 7. PRIVACY NOTICE UPDATE: Update your website's privacy notice to disclose the use of AI, the data processed by the chatbot, and the GPAI provider as a processor.

[ ] 8. CONSENT FOR BROWSING HISTORY PROFILING: Ensure you have valid GDPR legal basis (preferably consent) for using browsing history to power recommendations.

SHOULD-DO (Best Practice):

[ ] 9. INTERACTION LOGGING AND MONITORING: Log chatbot interactions for quality assurance and safety monitoring, with appropriate retention limits.

[ ] 10. INTERNAL DOCUMENTATION: Document your AI system, its purpose, the underlying model, and your compliance measures (helpful if ever audited by a national authority).

[ ] 11. BIAS AND FAIRNESS REVIEW: Periodically review recommendation outputs for discriminatory patterns.

[ ] 12. INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAN: Establish a process to handle complaints or incidents involving the chatbot, including escalation to human agents and reporting mechanisms.

[ ] 13. CONTRACTUAL PROTECTIONS: Ensure your contract with the GPAI provider includes EU AI Act compliance warranties and allocates liability appropriately.

[ ] 14. STAFF TRAINING: Train customer service and technical staff on AI Act obligations, how the chatbot works, and how to handle escalations.

---

PART 10: ENFORCEMENT AND PENALTIES
------------------------------------

The EU AI Act is enforced by national market surveillance authorities (each EU Member State designates one). For deployers:
- Violations of transparency obligations (Article 50) can result in fines of up to EUR 15,000,000 or 3% of global annual turnover (whichever is higher)
- Violations of prohibited practices (Article 5) carry fines of up to EUR 35,000,000 or 7% of global annual turnover
- Supplying incorrect or misleading information to authorities can result in fines of up to EUR 7,500,000 or 1% of global annual turnover

Given the relatively low compliance cost for a limited-risk chatbot (primarily a disclosure notice and process controls), the risk/benefit analysis strongly favours early compliance action.

---

SUMMARY ANSWER TO THE CORE QUESTIONS
--------------------------------------

Q: What are your EU AI Act obligations as the deployer?

A: As a deployer of a limited-risk AI chatbot, your primary obligations are:
1. Transparency/disclosure to users (Article 50)
2. Using the AI within the provider's instructions (Article 26)
3. Implementing human oversight mechanisms (Article 26)
4. Reviewing GPAI provider compliance documentation
