You do NOT have the extensive obligations of a high-risk AI system deployer (formal conformity assessments, CE marking, registration in EU database, etc.) unless your chatbot's scope expands into high-risk use cases.

Q: Do you need to disclose it is an AI?

A: YES — UNAMBIGUOUSLY. Article 50(1) of the EU AI Act requires that users interacting with an AI chatbot be clearly informed they are interacting with an AI, unless it is obvious from the context. This obligation applies to your chatbot. It is one of the clearest and most straightforward obligations in the Act. You must implement this disclosure before or at the beginning of each interaction, and you must never allow the chatbot to deny being an AI when asked.

---

DISCLAIMER: This analysis reflects the EU AI Act as enacted (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) and the state of regulatory guidance as of mid-2026. The EU AI Act is supplemented by delegated acts, implementing acts, and guidance from the AI Office that continue to evolve. Always consult qualified legal counsel for advice specific to your organisation's circumstances.
Prompt: We are a SaaS vendor preparing to respond to a federal agency RFP that requires a VPAT. Our product is a web-based project management tool. Walk us through how to complete an Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) using the VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition template, what conformance levels mean, and what testing we need to do first.
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Explains the four conformance levels: Supports, Partially Supports, Does Not Support, Not Applicable
✅References the VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition (not an older version) and mentions the ITI template
✅Explains the three main VPAT tables: Level A success criteria, Level AA success criteria, and Functional Performance Criteria
✅Specifies at least two assistive technology + browser combinations required for testing (e.g., JAWS + Chrome, NVDA + Firefox, VoiceOver + Safari)
✅Mentions automated testing tools (e.g., axe, WAVE, Lighthouse) as a first step, noting they catch only a portion of issues
Completing an Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) Using the VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition

As a SaaS vendor responding to a federal RFP that requires a VPAT, you are entering a formal procurement process governed by Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act (29 U.S.C. § 794d), as implemented by the Revised Section 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194, effective January 18, 2018). Below is a complete walkthrough of the ACR process for your web-based project management tool.

---

STEP 1: UNDERSTAND THE TEMPLATE

Use the VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition template published by ITI (Information Technology Industry Council), available at www.itic.org. Do not use older VPAT 1.x templates — federal agencies now require the 2.x series because it maps directly to the 2018 Revised Section 508 Standards, which incorporate WCAG 2.0 Level A and AA (via provision E205).

The VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition ACR has the following major sections:

  1. Product Information — Product name, version, report date, vendor contact, and a brief description of the product.
  2. Evaluation Methods Used — Describe what tools, assistive technologies, browsers, and manual methods you used.
  3. Table 1: Success Criteria, Level A — One row per WCAG 2.0 Level A success criterion.
  4. Table 2: Success Criteria, Level AA — One row per WCAG 2.0 Level AA success criterion.
  5. Table 3: Functional Performance Criteria — How your product supports users with various disabilities independent of specific technical criteria.
  6. Chapter 5 (Software) and Chapter 6 (Support Documentation and Services) — Applicable if your product includes native software components or has support documentation requirements.

For a pure web-based SaaS product, Tables 1, 2, and 3 are the core deliverables. Chapters 5 and 6 apply if you have downloadable clients or published help documentation.

---

STEP 2: UNDERSTAND THE THREE MAIN TABLES

Table 1 — WCAG 2.0 Level A Success Criteria
This table covers the baseline accessibility requirements. Key criteria for a project management tool include:
  - 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A): All icons, avatars, chart images, and status indicators must have meaningful alt text.
  - 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A): Tables, lists, headings, and form labels must use proper semantic markup.
  - 2.1.1 Keyboard (A): Every feature — task creation, drag-and-drop, modals, menus — must be fully operable by keyboard alone.
  - 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (A): Users must be able to navigate away from every component using keyboard alone.
  - 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A): All interactive UI components (buttons, dropdowns, date pickers, custom widgets) must expose their name, role, and state to assistive technologies via ARIA or native HTML semantics.

Table 2 — WCAG 2.0 Level AA Success Criteria
These are the additional requirements that Section 508 mandates for federal procurement. Key criteria include:
  - 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum (AA): Text must have at least 4.5:1 contrast ratio (3:1 for large text). Check all text colors, disabled state labels, and placeholder text.
  - 1.4.4 Resize Text (AA): The application must remain functional and readable when text is scaled to 200% in the browser.
  - 2.4.5 Multiple Ways (AA): Users must be able to find content via multiple means (navigation, search, sitemap).
  - 2.4.6 Headings and Labels (AA): All headings and form labels must be descriptive.
  - 2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA): The keyboard focus indicator must be visible at all times.
  - 3.3.3 Error Suggestion (AA): When a form error occurs (e.g., invalid date, missing required field), the error message must suggest how to correct it.

Table 3 — Functional Performance Criteria (Chapter 3)
This table evaluates whether users with various disability types can independently use your product, even if they rely on methods not covered by the WCAG criteria alone. The criteria are:
  - 302.1 Without Vision — Can a blind user access all features using a screen reader?
  - 302.2 With Limited Vision — Can a low-vision user access features using magnification and high contrast?
  - 302.3 Without Perception of Color — Is color never the sole indicator of status or meaning?
  - 302.4 Without Hearing — Is all audio content available as text (captions, transcripts)?
  - 302.5 With Limited Hearing — Is audio quality sufficient for users with partial hearing?
  - 302.6 Without Speech — Does the product function without voice input?
  - 302.7 With Limited Manipulation — Can users with limited dexterity operate all features (no fine-motor-required gestures)?
  - 302.8 With Limited Reach and Strength — All controls operable without requiring sustained physical effort.
  - 302.9 With Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — Content uses plain language and consistent navigation.

For each row in all three tables, you must supply a Conformance Level and Remarks.

---

STEP 3: UNDERSTAND THE FOUR CONFORMANCE LEVELS

Each row in Tables 1, 2, and 3 requires one of exactly four conformance level values:

  1. Supports
     The product fully meets the success criterion across all relevant functionality. There are no known failures. Example: "All interactive controls expose their name and role via native HTML semantics and ARIA; verified with JAWS 2024 + Chrome and NVDA + Firefox."

  2. Partially Supports
     The product meets the criterion in some instances but not all. You must specify in the Remarks column exactly where it fails. Example: "Most form fields have associated labels via <label> elements. However, the custom date-picker widget in the task creation modal does not expose its current value to screen readers (SC 4.1.2 failure)."

  3. Does Not Support
     The product does not meet the criterion. You must describe the failure and, ideally, your remediation timeline. Example: "Drag-and-drop task reordering has no keyboard-accessible equivalent (SC 2.1.1 failure). Remediation planned for Q3 2026 release."

  4. Not Applicable
     The criterion does not apply to the product. You must justify this. Example: "SC 1.2.1 Audio-only and Video-only: The product contains no pre-recorded audio-only or video-only content. Not Applicable."

Important: Do not use "Not Applicable" as an escape hatch. Auditors will challenge unjustified Not Applicable entries. If your product has any video tutorials, embedded media, or dynamic content, those criteria apply.

---

STEP 4: CONDUCT THE REQUIRED TESTING

You cannot complete an accurate ACR without real testing. Self-attestation based on code inspection alone is not sufficient and will be challenged during agency review. Complete the following testing phases before filling in the VPAT tables.

Phase 1 — Automated Scanning (Start Here)
Run automated accessibility scanners as a first pass. Recommended tools:
  - axe DevTools (browser extension or axe-core API) — the industry standard; integrates with CI/CD pipelines
  - WAVE (WebAIM) — browser extension with visual overlay of errors
  - Google Lighthouse — built into Chrome DevTools; run the Accessibility audit

Important caveat: Automated tools catch approximately 30-40% of WCAG failures. They reliably detect missing alt text, duplicate IDs, missing form labels, and obvious contrast failures. They cannot detect logical reading order failures, meaningful focus order problems, or whether a screen reader actually announces custom widgets correctly. Automated results are a floor, not a ceiling.

Phase 2 — Keyboard-Only Navigation
Test every workflow in your project management tool using only Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, Escape, and Arrow keys. Verify:
  - All task creation, editing, and deletion flows are keyboard operable.
  - Modal dialogs trap focus correctly (focus stays inside the modal) and release focus properly on close.
  - Dropdown menus and custom selects are operable by keyboard.
  - Drag-and-drop functionality has a keyboard-accessible alternative.
  - There are no keyboard traps (SC 2.1.2).
  - Focus order is logical (SC 2.4.3).
  - Focus indicator is always visible (SC 2.4.7).

Phase 3 — Screen Reader + Browser Combinations
Test with the following assistive technology and browser combinations. Each combination reflects real-world usage patterns among federal government users:

  - JAWS (latest version) + Google Chrome — JAWS is the most widely used screen reader in federal environments. Chrome is the dominant browser.
  - NVDA (latest version) + Mozilla Firefox — NVDA is the primary free/open-source screen reader; Firefox has strong ARIA support.
  - NVDA (latest version) + Google Chrome — Cross-test NVDA with Chrome to catch browser-specific rendering differences.
  - VoiceOver + Safari on macOS — Required for Mac users and for testing ARIA live regions on WebKit.
  - VoiceOver + Safari on iOS — Test mobile responsiveness for government employees using iPads or iPhones.
  - TalkBack + Chrome on Android — Android is common on federal mobile devices.

For each combination, walk through the primary user workflows: logging in, creating a project, creating a task, assigning a user, setting a due date, and viewing a dashboard. Document what the screen reader announces at each step.

Phase 4 — Color Contrast Verification
Use the Colour Contrast Analyser (TPGi) or the browser DevTools contrast checker to verify:
  - All body text: minimum 4.5:1 ratio (SC 1.4.3)
  - Large text (18pt/14pt bold and above): minimum 3:1 ratio
  - Focus indicators: verify visible against adjacent colors
  - Status badges and tags (e.g., "High Priority" in red): ensure contrast meets threshold

Phase 5 — Zoom and Reflow Testing
Set browser zoom to 200% and verify no content is lost or obscured. At 400% zoom (WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.10, Reflow — not required by 508 but good practice), content should reflow to a single column without horizontal scrolling.

Phase 6 — Document Results
For each failure found, record: the WCAG success criterion number and name, the specific element or workflow, the assistive technology/browser combination that revealed it, a screenshot or video clip, and the severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low).

---

STEP 5: FILL IN THE VPAT TABLES

With test results in hand:

1. Complete the Product Information section: include product name, version number, report date, company name, and a description of what the product does.

2. Complete the Evaluation Methods section: list every tool and AT combination used (e.g., "axe DevTools 4.x, NVDA 2024.1 + Firefox 125, JAWS 2024 + Chrome 124, VoiceOver + Safari 17 on macOS Sonoma, keyboard-only navigation testing, manual code inspection").

3. For each row in Table 1 and Table 2: assign the appropriate conformance level and write a Remarks entry. The Remarks must be specific — cite the affected component, the failure mode, and your remediation status. Vague remarks like "Generally supports this criterion" will not satisfy agency reviewers.

4. For each row in Table 3: describe how your product supports the functional category. Reference your screen reader test results and keyboard test results.

5. Complete Chapters 5 and 6 if applicable.

---

STEP 6: WRITE THE REMARKS CORRECTLY

Federal agency evaluators read the Remarks column carefully. Best practices:

- For "Supports": briefly confirm and cite the mechanism (e.g., "All images use <img> with meaningful alt text or alt='' for decorative images. Verified with axe-core and NVDA + Firefox.").
- For "Partially Supports": name the specific component that fails and the failure mode. Do not leave this vague.
- For "Does Not Support": state the failure clearly and include your remediation commitment if you have one.
- For "Not Applicable": provide a one-sentence justification tied to your product's actual functionality.

---

PROCUREMENT OBLIGATIONS SUMMARY

Under FAR clause 52.239-2 (when incorporated) and the agency's 508 program requirements, you may be required to:
  - Submit the completed ACR with your RFP response or within 30 days of contract award.
  - Provide updated ACRs with each major product release.
  - Remediate Critical issues (keyboard traps, complete screen reader failures) within 30 days of identification; High issues within 60 days; Medium within 90 days.
  - Provide an alternative means of access if claiming undue burden on any criterion.

If your product has known gaps, do not hide them. Agencies expect gaps in commercial products. A "Partially Supports" entry with a clear remediation timeline is far preferable — legally and practically — to a false "Supports" claim that unravels during agency accessibility testing.

---

REGULATORY CITATION SUMMARY

- Section 508 authority: 29 U.S.C. § 794d; Revised Standards: 36 CFR Part 1194 (effective January 18, 2018)
- Web/software/document requirement: E205 (incorporates WCAG 2.0 Level A and AA)
- Functional Performance Criteria: Chapter 3 (302.1–302.9)
- VPAT template source: ITI (Information Technology Industry Council), VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition
- FAR procurement clause: 52.239-2 (AccessIbilty of ICT)
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Explains the four conformance levels: Supports, Partially Supports, Does Not Support, Not Applicable
✅References the VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition (not an older version) and mentions the ITI template
✅Explains the three main VPAT tables: Level A success criteria, Level AA success criteria, and Functional Performance Criteria
✅Specifies at least two assistive technology + browser combinations required for testing (e.g., JAWS + Chrome, NVDA + Firefox, VoiceOver + Safari)
✅Mentions automated testing tools (e.g., axe, WAVE, Lighthouse) as a first step, noting they catch only a portion of issues
Completing an Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) Using the VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition Template

Overview

When responding to a federal agency RFP that requires a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT), you must produce an Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR). The ACR is your formal, documented statement of how well your product meets accessibility standards. Federal agencies use it to evaluate whether your SaaS product complies with Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act, which incorporates WCAG 2.0 Level A and AA success criteria by reference (via the 2017 ICT Final Rule).

The VPAT 2.x templates are published and maintained by the Information Technology Industry Council (ITI). For a web-based product, the correct template is the VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition. As of the current versions (VPAT 2.4 and later revisions), this template is available directly from the ITI website (www.itic.org). Do not use older VPAT 1.x templates; federal procurement reviewers will expect the 2.x format.


Step 1: Testing Before You Fill Out the VPAT

You cannot accurately complete a VPAT without prior accessibility testing. Completing a VPAT based on assumptions or developer intent — rather than actual test results — is a serious error that can expose your company to legal risk and damage your agency relationship. Testing should precede document authoring.

1a. Automated Testing (First Step — Necessary but Not Sufficient)

Start with automated scanning tools. These are fast, repeatable, and catch a well-defined subset of issues. Common tools include:

- axe (Deque Systems) — available as a browser extension and CI/CD integration
- WAVE (WebAIM) — browser extension and API
- Lighthouse (Google) — built into Chrome DevTools, also available via CLI
- IBM Equal Access Checker
- Siteimprove Accessibility Checker

Important caveat: Automated tools can reliably detect roughly 30–40% of WCAG failures (some estimates range from 20–57% depending on the tool and ruleset). They cannot assess cognitive usability, logical reading order in complex layouts, the meaningfulness of alt text, or whether interactive components are operable by keyboard in practice. Automated testing is a starting point, not a finishing point.

1b. Manual Keyboard Testing

Navigate your entire application using only a keyboard (Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, Arrow keys, Escape). Verify that:
- All interactive elements receive visible focus
- Focus order is logical
- No keyboard traps exist
- Modals and custom widgets (date pickers, drag-and-drop, rich text editors) are fully keyboard-operable

1c. Assistive Technology Testing

You must test with real assistive technology (AT) paired with specific browsers, because AT/browser combinations behave differently. Required combinations for a thorough VPAT covering a web product include at minimum:

- JAWS (Job Access With Speech) + Chrome on Windows — the most common enterprise screen reader combination; JAWS is widely used in federal government
- NVDA (NonVisual Desktop Access) + Firefox on Windows — open-source screen reader, widely used; pairing with Firefox tests a distinct rendering path
- VoiceOver + Safari on macOS — required for Apple ecosystem coverage
- VoiceOver + Safari on iOS (iPhone/iPad) — if your product has a mobile-responsive interface
- TalkBack + Chrome on Android — for mobile coverage
- Dragon NaturallySpeaking (Dragon Professional) + Chrome — for speech input testing (relevant to WCAG 2.1 success criteria and Section 508 functional performance criteria)

At minimum, cover JAWS + Chrome, NVDA + Firefox, and VoiceOver + Safari to address the most common federal user scenarios.

1d. Color Contrast and Visual Testing

Use tools like the Colour Contrast Analyser or browser dev tools to verify all text meets the 4.5:1 ratio (normal text) and 3:1 ratio (large text) required by WCAG 1.4.3.

1e. User Testing with People with Disabilities

Where possible, include participants with disabilities in usability testing. This is not strictly required for the VPAT but strengthens the credibility of your conformance claims.


Step 2: Understanding the VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition Structure

The ITI VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition template contains three primary tables you must complete:

Table 1: Success Criteria, Level A

This table lists all WCAG 2.x Level A success criteria (the minimum baseline requirements). For a web application, this includes criteria such as:
- 1.1.1 Non-text Content
- 1.3.1 Info and Relationships
- 2.1.1 Keyboard
- 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value
...and all other Level A criteria.

For each criterion, you provide:
- A conformance level (see Step 3)
- Remarks and explanations describing how your product meets or does not meet the criterion, and what specific issues exist

Table 2: Success Criteria, Level AA

This table lists all WCAG 2.x Level AA success criteria, which are required by Section 508 (the 2017 ICT Final Rule incorporates WCAG 2.0 Levels A and AA). Examples include:
- 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
- 1.4.4 Resize Text
- 2.4.7 Focus Visible
- 3.3.3 Error Suggestion

The same conformance level and remarks structure applies.

Note: The VPAT 2.x template also includes an optional Level AAA table. Federal agencies do not require Level AAA conformance, but you may include it voluntarily.

Table 3: Functional Performance Criteria (FPC)

This table is specific to Section 508 and does not map directly to individual WCAG criteria. Instead, it addresses the functional outcomes that the product must support, corresponding to 36 CFR 1194.31. The criteria include:

- Without Vision — usable without sight (screen reader access)
- With Limited Vision — usable with low vision (magnification, contrast)
- Without Perception of Color — usable when color is not available
- Without Hearing — usable without audio
- With Limited Hearing — usable with limited hearing
- Without Speech — operable without spoken input
- With Limited Manipulation — operable with limited hand/arm dexterity
- With Limited Reach and Strength — operable with limited physical reach
- With Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — usable by people with cognitive disabilities
- With Photosensitivity — does not cause seizures

For each FPC, you again provide a conformance level and narrative explanation.

The template also includes a header section where you document: product name and version, report date, contact information, evaluation methods used, and applicable standards (WCAG 2.x, Revised Section 508).


Step 3: Understanding the Four Conformance Levels

Every row in every VPAT table must be assigned one of four conformance levels. These are standardized terms — do not use alternatives like "Mostly Supports" or "N/A with asterisk."

1. Supports
The product fully meets the success criterion without exception. All functionality that is within scope of the criterion works in an accessible manner. Use this level only when you have confirmed through testing that there are no failures.

2. Partially Supports
Some functionality meets the success criterion, but some does not. This is the most commonly used level for products that have some accessibility issues but are not completely inaccessible for a given criterion. Your remarks must explain what works, what does not, and ideally describe the specific defect (e.g., "The modal dialog closes on Escape in Chrome but does not return focus to the triggering element in NVDA + Firefox").

3. Does Not Support
The product does not meet the success criterion. The functionality exists but is not accessible in the way the criterion requires. Your remarks should explain the nature of the failure.

4. Not Applicable
The success criterion does not apply to your product because the relevant feature type is not present. For example, if your project management tool has no audio or video content, criteria like 1.2.1 (Audio-only and Video-only) would be Not Applicable. Document clearly why the criterion does not apply.

Important note on honesty: Federal agencies and their 508 coordinators are increasingly sophisticated in reviewing VPATs. Overclaiming "Supports" where issues exist will damage your credibility during procurement. It is better to acknowledge "Partially Supports" with a clear remediation roadmap than to claim full conformance inaccurately.


Step 4: Writing the Remarks and Explanations Column

For every row where conformance is not "Supports" or "Not Applicable," the Remarks and Explanations column must provide enough detail for the agency to understand:
- What specific functionality is affected
- What the failure mode is
- What user impact results (e.g., screen reader users cannot access the Gantt chart export function)
- What your remediation plan and timeline is (optional but strengthens the submission)

Vague remarks like "Some issues exist" are insufficient. Specificity demonstrates that you have actually tested the product.


Step 5: Scoping Your VPAT

For a web-based project management SaaS tool, you need to define what is in scope before you begin. Typical scope considerations:

- Core application features: task creation, project dashboards, user management, reporting
- Authentication flows: login, MFA, password reset
- Third-party embedded components: if you use a third-party Gantt chart widget, rich text editor, or file picker, you are responsible for its accessibility in your product
- Help documentation and support portal (if web-based): also subject to Section 508
- Mobile web experience: if your app is responsive or has a mobile site
- Out of scope: things genuinely not part of your product offering (document clearly)


Step 6: Keeping the ACR Current

An ACR is a point-in-time document. Federal agencies expect it to reflect the version of the product being procured. When you release new versions with significant UI changes, update the ACR. Some agencies will ask for an ACR dated within the past 12–18 months. Maintain version control on your ACR alongside your product releases.


Summary Checklist for VPAT Completion

1. Select the correct template: VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition from ITI (itic.org)
2. Define scope (what features and user flows are covered)
3. Run automated scans (axe, WAVE, Lighthouse) — document tool versions and scan dates
4. Conduct manual keyboard testing across all workflows
5. Test with AT/browser combinations: at minimum JAWS + Chrome, NVDA + Firefox, VoiceOver + Safari
6. Complete Table 1 (Level A criteria) with conformance levels and remarks
7. Complete Table 2 (Level AA criteria) with conformance levels and remarks
8. Complete Table 3 (Functional Performance Criteria) with conformance levels and remarks
9. Fill in the header: product name, version, report date, evaluation methodology, contact
10. Review for honesty and specificity before submission
11. Plan a remediation roadmap for any "Partially Supports" or "Does Not Support" findings
12. Update the ACR with each significant product release
Prompt: Our federal agency's internal web application fails Section 508 because users report they cannot navigate it using only a keyboard — focus disappears and some dropdown menus can't be opened without a mouse. What specific WCAG 2.0 success criteria are violated, and give us a concrete remediation plan?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Identifies SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A) as the primary violated criterion
✅Identifies SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA) for the disappearing focus issue
✅Identifies SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap as applicable if focus is trapped or lost
✅Provides specific remediation guidance — e.g., use native HTML controls, implement ARIA keyboard patterns, never use CSS outline:none without replacement
✅Recommends a specific testing approach: keyboard-only tab-through test covering all interactive elements
Section 508 / WCAG 2.0 Keyboard Accessibility Analysis and Remediation Plan
Federal Internal Web Application — Keyboard Navigation Failures

===========================================================================
SECTION 1: VIOLATED WCAG 2.0 SUCCESS CRITERIA
===========================================================================

The issues described — focus disappearing during navigation and dropdown menus
that require a mouse — map to three specific WCAG 2.0 success criteria
incorporated into the Revised Section 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194, E205):

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A) — PRIMARY VIOLATION
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Requirement: All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard
interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes, except
where the underlying function requires input that depends on the path of the
user's movement and not just the endpoints.

Violation: Dropdown menus that cannot be opened without a mouse are a direct
failure of SC 2.1.1. Every interactive function — including opening, navigating,
and selecting options from dropdown menus — must be fully operable via keyboard
(Tab, Enter, Space, Arrow keys). If any mouse-only interaction exists, the
application Does Not Support this criterion.

Applicable Section 508 provision: E205.4 (WCAG 2.0 Level A and AA)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA) — SECONDARY VIOLATION
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Requirement: Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where
the keyboard focus indicator is visible.

Violation: Users reporting that "focus disappears" describes a direct failure of
SC 2.4.7. When a user tabs through the application and cannot see which element
currently has focus, they are operating blind. Common causes include:
  - CSS rule: outline: none or outline: 0 applied to :focus selectors without
    a replacement focus indicator
  - Focus being moved programmatically to a non-visible element
  - Focus being set on a container element with no visible styling

Applicable Section 508 provision: E205.4 (WCAG 2.0 Level AA)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (Level A) — POTENTIALLY VIOLATED
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Requirement: If keyboard focus can be moved to a component of the page using a
keyboard interface, then focus can be moved away from that component using only
a keyboard interface, and, if it requires more than unmodified arrow or tab keys,
the user is advised of the method for moving focus away.

Violation: The report of focus "disappearing" may indicate one of two conditions:
  (a) Focus is genuinely lost — moved to a non-focusable element or off-screen
      element — rather than trapped, which is a SC 2.4.7 failure; OR
  (b) Focus enters a component (e.g., a modal, custom widget, or dropdown) and
      cannot be moved out without a mouse, which is a SC 2.1.2 keyboard trap.

Both conditions should be tested. If a user can Tab into a dropdown or modal but
cannot Escape or Shift-Tab out, SC 2.1.2 is violated.

Applicable Section 508 provision: E205.4 (WCAG 2.0 Level A)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
ADDITIONAL CRITERION TO VERIFY
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
SC 2.4.3 Focus Order (Level A): If the programmatic focus order does not match
the visual/logical order of the interface (e.g., focus jumps unexpectedly after
interacting with a dropdown), SC 2.4.3 may also be violated.

SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (Level A): Custom dropdown widgets built from
non-semantic HTML elements (e.g., <div> or <span> used as menus) will have no
keyboard role by default and may fail SC 4.1.2 in addition to SC 2.1.1.

===========================================================================
SECTION 2: REMEDIATION PLAN
===========================================================================

The following phased plan addresses each violation with specific technical fixes,
responsible owners, effort estimates, and recommended timelines aligned with
federal remediation SLA guidance (Critical issues: 30 days; High: 60 days).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Phase 1 — Critical (Target: 30 days)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Issue 1: Dropdown menus not operable by keyboard
Criterion: SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A) — Critical
Fix:
  Option A (Preferred): Replace custom dropdown widgets with native HTML
  <select> elements. Native controls are keyboard-accessible by default, require
  no additional scripting, and are supported by all assistive technologies.

  Option B (If custom UI required): Implement the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide
  (APG) Listbox or Disclosure Navigation patterns:
    - The trigger button must be a <button> element (or have role="button") so
      it receives focus and responds to Enter/Space.
    - Arrow keys (Up/Down) must move focus between menu items.
    - Escape must close the menu and return focus to the trigger button.
    - Home/End keys should optionally jump to first/last item.
    - Each menu item must have role="menuitem" (or "option" for listboxes) and
      tabindex="-1" so Arrow keys control focus within the widget.
  Reference: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/

  NEVER use event listeners bound only to mousedown, mouseenter, or click
  without equivalent keyboard event handlers (keydown for Enter, Space, Arrows).

Owner: Front-end development team
Effort: Medium (2–5 days per widget type)
Timeline: 30 days

Issue 2: Keyboard trap in dropdown or modal components
Criterion: SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (Level A) — Critical
Fix:
  - Ensure Escape key closes any open dropdown, modal, or custom widget and
    returns focus to the element that triggered it.
  - Implement focus containment in modals (Tab and Shift-Tab should cycle only
    within the modal while it is open), and focus must be released when the
    modal closes.
  - Audit all third-party component libraries for keyboard trap issues; if a
    vendor component is the source, escalate to the vendor with reference to
    SC 2.1.2 and require a patch or replace the component.

Owner: Front-end development team
Effort: Low–Medium (1–3 days)
Timeline: 30 days

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Phase 2 — High (Target: 60 days)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Issue 3: Disappearing focus indicator
Criterion: SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA) — High
Fix:
  Step 1 — Audit CSS: Search the entire stylesheet for the following patterns
  and remove or replace every instance:
    *:focus { outline: none; }
    *:focus { outline: 0; }
    a:focus { outline: none; }
    button:focus { outline: none; }
  Use of outline: none without a replacement focus style is the single most
  common cause of this failure and is never acceptable.

  Step 2 — Implement a visible focus style: Apply a high-contrast focus
  indicator to all interactive elements. Recommended approach:
    :focus-visible {
      outline: 3px solid #005FCC;   /* high-contrast blue */
      outline-offset: 2px;
    }
  Alternatively, use a box-shadow focus ring. The indicator must be visible
  against both light and dark backgrounds used in the application.

  Step 3 — Verify programmatic focus: Search for JavaScript calls to
  element.focus() and confirm the target element is visible in the viewport
  and has a visible focus style. If focus is set on a <div> or <span>, ensure
  the element has tabindex="0" and an appropriate focus indicator.

  Step 4 — Do not suppress :focus styles for mouse users only without also
  providing them for keyboard users. The :focus-visible pseudo-class can be
  used to show focus rings only during keyboard navigation without affecting
  mouse interactions, but the keyboard indicator must always be present.

Owner: Front-end development team / UX/design team
Effort: Low (1–2 days for CSS audit and fix; additional time for design review)
Timeline: 60 days

Issue 4: Non-semantic custom widgets lacking keyboard roles
Criterion: SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (Level A) — High
Fix:
  - Audit all custom interactive components (dropdowns, tabs, accordions,
    tooltips, date pickers) built from <div>, <span>, or <a> elements.
  - Add appropriate ARIA roles, states, and properties per the APG patterns.
  - Ensure every interactive element has an accessible name (aria-label,
    aria-labelledby, or visible text label).
  - Add tabindex="0" to any custom focusable element that is not a native
    interactive element.

Owner: Front-end development team
Effort: Medium (varies by number and complexity of components)
Timeline: 60 days

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Phase 3 — Ongoing / Systemic (Target: 90 days and continuous)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Issue 5: Prevent recurrence — developer standards
Fix:
  - Establish a coding standard that mandates use of native semantic HTML
    elements for interactive controls wherever possible.
  - Add automated accessibility testing to the CI/CD pipeline using axe-core
    (axe-core npm package or axe DevTools) to catch regressions on every build.
    Note: automated tools catch ~30–40% of issues; manual testing is still
    required.
  - Require keyboard-only smoke testing as part of QA sign-off for every sprint
    that touches UI components.

Owner: Engineering lead, QA team, DevOps
Effort: Medium
Timeline: 90 days

===========================================================================
SECTION 3: TESTING APPROACH
===========================================================================

Before and after remediation, perform the following structured keyboard-only
test. Do NOT use a mouse at any point during this test.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Keyboard-Only Tab-Through Test Procedure
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Tools needed: Any modern browser (Chrome or Firefox recommended); no mouse or
trackpad input during testing.

Step 1 — Establish baseline
  Load the application in the browser and note the starting focus position
  (or lack thereof). The browser tab itself may have initial focus; press Tab
  once to enter the page.

Step 2 — Tab through all interactive elements
  Press Tab repeatedly and verify:
  [ ] A visible focus indicator appears on each focusable element.
  [ ] Focus moves in a logical, top-to-bottom / left-to-right order matching
      the visual layout (SC 2.4.3 Focus Order).
  [ ] Focus never becomes invisible or moves to an element that cannot be seen
      or identified (SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible).
  [ ] Focus does not become trapped in any component — you can always Tab
      forward or Shift-Tab backward to exit (SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap).

Step 3 — Operate all interactive controls by keyboard
  For each interactive element reached by Tab, test:
  [ ] Links: press Enter to activate.
  [ ] Buttons: press Enter or Space to activate.
  [ ] Dropdown menus: press Enter or Space to open; Arrow keys to navigate
      options; Enter to select; Escape to close without selecting.
  [ ] Checkboxes: press Space to toggle.
  [ ] Radio buttons: use Arrow keys within the group to change selection.
  [ ] Text fields: type to enter text.
  [ ] Modals/dialogs: verify Tab cycles within the modal; Escape closes it
      and returns focus to the trigger (SC 2.1.2, SC 2.4.3).
  [ ] Date pickers, sliders, tabs, accordions: follow APG keyboard patterns.

Step 4 — Document every failure
  For each failure, record:
  - Element type and location (page, section, element ID or description)
  - WCAG 2.0 success criterion violated
  - Screenshot or video capture showing the failure
  - Steps to reproduce

Step 5 — Supplement with automated scanning
  Run axe DevTools or WAVE on each page after manual testing to capture
  additional programmatic failures (missing labels, invalid ARIA, etc.).

Step 6 — Screen reader verification (after keyboard fixes are deployed)
  Confirm fixes with NVDA + Chrome and JAWS + Chrome to ensure ARIA roles
  and focus management work correctly with assistive technologies.

===========================================================================
SECTION 4: VPAT / ACR IMPACT
===========================================================================

The application's current Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR/VPAT) must
be updated to reflect:

  SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A):       Does Not Support → (target) Supports
  SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (A):     Partially Supports or Does Not Support
                                       (confirm via testing) → Supports
  SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA): Does Not Support → Supports

Until remediation is complete, the agency must document these failures in the
ACR and, per E205, provide an alternative means of access for users who cannot
operate the application with a mouse (e.g., an accessible alternative workflow,
a phone-based service, or an accessible offline process). Retain the written
determination and alternative access documentation for audit purposes.

===========================================================================
REFERENCES
===========================================================================

- 36 CFR Part 1194 — Revised Section 508 Standards (2018)
- WCAG 2.0, Guideline 2.1 (Keyboard Accessible): https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG20/#keyboard-operation
- WCAG 2.0, SC 2.1.1: https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG20/#keyboard-operation-keyboard-operable
- WCAG 2.0, SC 2.1.2: https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG20/#keyboard-operation-trapping
- WCAG 2.0, SC 2.4.7: https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG20/#navigation-mechanisms-focus-visible
- ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG): https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/
- ARIA APG Menu Button Pattern: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/menu-button/
- Section508.gov Testing Guide: https://www.section508.gov/test/
- axe-core: https://github.com/dequelabs/axe-core
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Identifies SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A) as the primary violated criterion
✅Identifies SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA) for the disappearing focus issue
✅Identifies SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap as applicable if focus is trapped or lost
✅Provides specific remediation guidance — e.g., use native HTML controls, implement ARIA keyboard patterns, never use CSS outline:none without replacement
✅Recommends a specific testing approach: keyboard-only tab-through test covering all interactive elements
Section 508 Keyboard Accessibility: Violated WCAG 2.0 Success Criteria and Remediation Plan
==============================================================================================

OVERVIEW
--------
Section 508 (revised 2018) incorporates WCAG 2.0 Level A and Level AA success criteria by reference.
The reported issues — disappearing focus and mouse-only dropdown menus — map to several specific
violations that must be addressed to achieve compliance.


VIOLATED WCAG 2.0 SUCCESS CRITERIA
------------------------------------

1. SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A) — PRIMARY VIOLATION
   "All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard interface without requiring
   specific timings for individual keystrokes."

   Applicability: Dropdown menus that cannot be opened or operated without a mouse directly violate
   this criterion. Every interactive element — menus, buttons, links, form controls, modal dialogs —
   must be fully operable via keyboard alone (Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, arrow keys as appropriate).

2. SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA)
   "Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator
   is visible."

   Applicability: When users report that "focus disappears," the application is hiding the browser's
   default focus ring, most commonly via CSS rules such as `outline: none` or `outline: 0` applied
   globally or to focused elements without providing an alternative visible indicator. This is a
   direct violation of 2.4.7.

3. SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (Level A)
   "If keyboard focus can be moved to a component of the page using a keyboard interface, then focus
   can be moved away from that component using only a keyboard interface, and if it requires more
   than unmodified arrow or tab keys to move focus away, the user is informed of the method."

   Applicability: When focus "disappears" it may indicate that focus is being sent to an element
   that cannot be reached again (a trap or dead-end). Dropdown menus implemented with custom
   JavaScript that intercept key events can inadvertently trap focus. This criterion is violated
   when a user cannot Tab or Shift+Tab away from an interactive component, or when focus is
   programmatically moved to a non-interactive element where it becomes stranded.

4. SC 2.4.3 Focus Order (Level A) — Secondary concern
   "If a Web page can be navigated sequentially and the navigation sequences affect meaning or
   operation, focusable components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning and operation."

   Applicability: Custom dropdown implementations often inject DOM elements dynamically, which can
   cause focus to jump to unexpected locations in the page, violating logical reading/navigation order.


CONCRETE REMEDIATION PLAN
--------------------------

PHASE 1 — AUDIT (Week 1-2)

   1. Keyboard-Only Tab-Through Test
      - Disconnect or disable the mouse entirely.
      - Starting from the top of each page, use the Tab key to cycle through all interactive
        elements and Shift+Tab to reverse.
      - Document every element that:
          a. Cannot receive focus at all.
          b. Receives focus but shows no visible indicator.
          c. Traps focus (cannot Tab away).
          d. Cannot be activated with Enter or Space.
      - Test all dropdown menus specifically: Tab to the trigger, press Enter/Space to open,
        use arrow keys to navigate options, press Escape to close.
      - Use browser DevTools to inspect computed CSS on focused elements, looking for
        `outline: none`, `outline: 0`, or `opacity: 0` on `:focus` pseudo-class rules.

   2. Automated Scan
      - Run axe-core, WAVE, or IBM Equal Access Checker against each page to flag missing
        focusable elements and absent focus styles. Note: automated tools catch ~30-40% of
        keyboard issues; manual testing is essential.

PHASE 2 — FIX FOCUS VISIBILITY (Week 2-3)

   1. Remove or override global `outline: none` rules.
      - Search codebase for: `outline: none`, `outline: 0`, `:focus { outline`.
      - Replace with a visible, high-contrast focus indicator, for example:
          :focus {
            outline: 3px solid #005FCC;
            outline-offset: 2px;
          }
      - If a custom focus style is desired for aesthetics, use `:focus-visible` to show the
        indicator for keyboard users while suppressing it for mouse clicks (with a fallback
        `:focus` rule for browsers that do not support `:focus-visible`).

   2. Ensure focus indicators meet contrast requirements (WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast,
      Level AA — 3:1 ratio minimum against adjacent colors).

PHASE 3 — FIX DROPDOWN KEYBOARD OPERABILITY (Week 3-5)

   Option A — Use Native HTML Controls (Preferred for simplicity)
      - Replace custom dropdown widgets with native `<select>`, `<button>`, or `<details>`/`<summary>`
        elements. Native HTML controls are keyboard-accessible by default and require no additional
        ARIA or JavaScript.

   Option B — Implement ARIA Keyboard Patterns (Required for custom widgets)
      - Follow the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG) patterns for the specific widget type:
          * Navigation menus: use the "Disclosure Navigation Menu" or "Menu Button" pattern.
          * Dropdown/listbox: implement the "Combobox" or "Listbox" pattern.
      - Required keyboard behaviors for a menu button dropdown:
          * Tab: moves focus to the menu button trigger.
          * Enter or Space: opens the menu; focus moves to first menu item.
          * Arrow Down/Up: moves focus among menu items.
          * Escape: closes the menu; returns focus to the trigger button.
          * Tab (while menu is open): closes the menu; moves focus to next element in page.
      - Apply correct ARIA roles and states:
          * Trigger: `role="button"` (or use `<button>`), `aria-haspopup="true"`, `aria-expanded="false/true"`.
          * Menu container: `role="menu"`.
          * Menu items: `role="menuitem"`.
      - Manage focus programmatically with JavaScript (`element.focus()`) when menu opens and closes.

   Option C — Third-Party Accessible Component Libraries
      - Adopt a library with built-in accessibility support (e.g., Headless UI, Radix UI, Adobe
        React Aria, or Deque's cauldron) to replace custom implementations. These libraries
        implement ARIA patterns and keyboard handling correctly.

PHASE 4 — FIX KEYBOARD TRAPS (Week 3-4)

   1. Audit all modal dialogs, slide-out panels, and menus for focus containment logic.
   2. For modal dialogs: focus SHOULD be contained within the dialog while it is open (this is
      intentional and correct per the ARIA modal pattern). Ensure Tab cycles within the dialog
      and Escape closes it, returning focus to the trigger element.
   3. For non-modal components (dropdowns, tooltips): focus must NOT be trapped. Pressing Tab
      or Escape must always allow the user to leave the component.
   4. Use a focus-trap utility (e.g., focus-trap-js) for modals only; ensure it is not
      accidentally applied to non-modal elements.

PHASE 5 — REGRESSION TESTING AND VALIDATION (Week 5-6)

   1. Re-run the full keyboard-only tab-through test after fixes.
   2. Test with actual assistive technology:
      - NVDA + Chrome (Windows)
      - JAWS + Chrome or Edge (Windows)
      - VoiceOver + Safari (macOS/iOS)
   3. Conduct user testing with at least one keyboard-only user if possible.
   4. Run automated scans again to confirm no regressions.
   5. Document findings and remediation in a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT)
      to track conformance.

PHASE 6 — PROCESS IMPROVEMENTS (Ongoing)

   1. Add keyboard accessibility checks to Definition of Done for all UI stories.
   2. Integrate axe-core or similar into CI/CD pipeline to catch regressions automatically.
   3. Establish a developer training module on ARIA patterns and keyboard navigation.
   4. Designate an accessibility SME or engage a third-party accessibility consultant for
      periodic audits.


SUMMARY TABLE OF VIOLATIONS AND FIXES
--------------------------------------

| Issue Reported               | WCAG 2.0 SC Violated         | Level | Primary Fix                                      |
|------------------------------|------------------------------|-------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Dropdowns require mouse      | 2.1.1 Keyboard               | A     | Native HTML or ARIA keyboard patterns            |
| Focus disappears             | 2.4.7 Focus Visible          | AA    | Remove outline:none; add visible :focus styles   |
| Focus stranded/lost          | 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap       | A     | Fix JS event handlers; use focus management      |
| Focus jumps unexpectedly     | 2.4.3 Focus Order            | A     | Correct DOM order; manage focus programmatically |


REFERENCES
----------
- WCAG 2.0: https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG20/
- ARIA Authoring Practices Guide: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/
- Section 508 Standards: https://www.access-board.gov/ict/
- Revised 508 Standards Mapping to WCAG 2.0: https://www.access-board.gov/ict/wcag2ict.html
Prompt: We have 200 existing PDF forms on our agency website that are not accessible to screen reader users. We need to remediate them for Section 508 compliance. What are the specific requirements for PDF accessibility and what is the remediation process?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅States PDFs must be Tagged PDFs with a correct tag hierarchy (Document, headings, paragraphs, tables, lists)
✅Mentions form fields must have accessible names (Tooltip/name field in Acrobat Pro form editor)
✅States all images in PDFs must have Alt text set in the tag properties
✅Mentions document language must be set in Document Properties
✅References a verification/testing step using Acrobat Pro's Full Accessibility Check or equivalent
PDF Accessibility Remediation for Section 508 Compliance: Requirements and Process
====================================================================================

Your 200 PDF forms fall under Section 508 provision E205, which requires electronic documents to conform to WCAG 2.0 Level A and AA success criteria. For PDFs specifically, the governing standards are SC 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships), SC 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value), SC 3.1.1 (Language of Page), SC 1.1.1 (Non-text Content), and SC 3.3.2 (Labels or Instructions), cross-referenced against PDF/UA (ISO 14289).

---

SPECIFIC PDF ACCESSIBILITY REQUIREMENTS
----------------------------------------

1. Tagged PDF with Correct Tag Hierarchy (SC 1.3.1, 4.1.2)

Every PDF must be a "Tagged PDF." Tags are hidden metadata that describe the document structure to screen readers. Without tags, a screen reader user experiences the PDF as a stream of unordered text or complete silence. The tag tree must follow a correct hierarchy:

  - Document (root tag)
    - H1, H2 ... H6 (headings in logical order)
    - P (paragraphs)
    - Table > TR > TH / TD (data tables with header associations)
    - L > LI > Lbl + LBody (lists)
    - Form (form containers)
    - Figure (images)
    - Link (hyperlinks)
    - Artifact (decorative content excluded from reading order)

Scanned image-based PDFs (PDF images of paper forms) cannot be made accessible by tagging alone — they require OCR to produce actual text, followed by full remediation.

2. Accessible Form Field Names (SC 4.1.2, 3.3.2)

Every interactive form field (text boxes, checkboxes, radio buttons, dropdowns, signature fields) must have an accessible name that is programmatically exposed to screen readers. In Adobe Acrobat Pro, this is set in the form field properties under the "General" tab — the "Tooltip" field is what JAWS, NVDA, and VoiceOver announce when a user moves focus to that field. A field labeled visually with nearby text but lacking a Tooltip value will be announced only by its internal field name (often a meaningless string like "Text1") or announced as "blank."

Steps in Acrobat Pro:
  - Open the Forms editor (Tools > Prepare Form)
  - Right-click each field > Properties > General tab
  - Enter a descriptive Tooltip (e.g., "First Name," "Date of Birth (MM/DD/YYYY)," "Applicant Signature")
  - For radio button groups, each button should have the Tooltip set to the group question (e.g., "Are you a U.S. citizen? Yes" / "Are you a U.S. citizen? No")

3. Alt Text for All Images (SC 1.1.1)

Every image in the PDF that conveys information must have Alt text set in the tag properties. Decorative images (dividers, background graphics) must be marked as Artifacts so screen readers skip them.

In Acrobat Pro:
  - Open the Tags panel
  - Locate each <Figure> tag
  - Right-click > Properties > Tag tab > enter Alt Text
  - For decorative images: right-click the tag > Change Tag to Artifact

Charts, graphs, logos with informational content, and signature line graphics all require meaningful Alt text descriptions.

4. Document Language Set in Document Properties (SC 3.1.1)

The default human language of the document must be programmatically declared so that screen readers apply the correct speech synthesis engine and pronunciation rules. A form with no language set will be read with the screen reader's default language, which may produce unintelligible output for non-English speakers or cause the wrong language engine to be applied even to English text.

In Acrobat Pro:
  - File > Properties > Advanced tab
  - Under "Reading Options," set the Language field to the appropriate IETF language tag (e.g., "en-US" for American English, "es" for Spanish)

5. Correct Reading Order (SC 1.3.2)

The order in which a screen reader reads content must match the logical visual order. Multi-column layouts, sidebars, and complex form layouts often produce incorrect reading order in tagged PDFs. Use Acrobat Pro's Reading Order tool (Tools > Accessibility > Reading Order) to inspect and correct the sequence. The Order panel shows the numerical reading sequence and allows drag-and-drop reordering.

6. Meaningful Hyperlink Text (SC 2.4.4)

All hyperlinks must have descriptive link text. A link tagged as "click here" or a raw URL ("https://www.agency.gov/very-long-path") fails SC 2.4.4. Update link display text in the tag properties or via the Link tool in Acrobat.

7. Table Header Associations (SC 1.3.1)

Data tables must use TH (table header) tags with a Scope attribute (Row or Column) or explicit ID/Headers associations. Layout tables used purely for visual positioning should be marked as Artifacts or have their table role removed.

8. Document Title Set (SC 2.4.2)

The document title — not the filename — must be set in Document Properties and configured to display as the window title. This allows screen reader users to identify the document.

In Acrobat Pro: File > Properties > Description tab > enter a meaningful Title, then in the Initial View tab set "Show" to "Document Title."

---

REMEDIATION PROCESS FOR 200 PDF FORMS
---------------------------------------

Given the volume, a phased triage-and-batch approach is recommended.

Phase 1 — Triage and Prioritization (Weeks 1–2)

Categorize all 200 forms by type:
  - Category A: Scanned image-only PDFs (require OCR + full rebuild — highest effort)
  - Category B: Digitally created PDFs with no tags (require tagging + form field remediation)
  - Category C: Tagged PDFs with incomplete or incorrect tags (require audit and repair)

Prioritize by public impact: forms used in high-volume public-facing transactions (applications, benefits, enrollment) should be addressed before internal administrative forms.

Run an automated pre-screening using Acrobat Pro's Full Accessibility Check (Tools > Accessibility > Full Check) on each PDF. Export results to identify the most common failure categories across your inventory. This surfaces patterns (e.g., all forms missing language, all forms have untagged images) that allow batch processing.

Phase 2 — Remediation (Weeks 3–12, scaled to staff/vendor capacity)

For each form:
  1. If scanned image-only: run OCR (Tools > Scan & OCR > Recognize Text), then proceed to tagging.
  2. Apply or correct the tag tree to establish correct Document > heading > paragraph > table > list hierarchy.
  3. Set reading order using the Reading Order tool and Order panel.
  4. Add Tooltip/accessible name to every form field via the Prepare Form editor.
  5. Add Alt text to all informational Figure tags; mark decorative images as Artifacts.
  6. Set document language in File > Properties > Advanced > Reading Options.
  7. Set document title in File > Properties > Description; configure Initial View to display title.
  8. Verify table header (TH) Scope attributes for all data tables.
  9. Update any non-descriptive hyperlink text.

For large batches of structurally similar forms, consider using the Acrobat Action Wizard to automate repetitive steps (adding language, title settings) across multiple files simultaneously.

Phase 3 — Verification and Testing (Concurrent with Phase 2)

After each form is remediated, run the Full Accessibility Check in Acrobat Pro (Tools > Accessibility > Full Check). This check tests against the PDF/UA standard and flags issues including: missing tags, missing Alt text, missing form field names, missing document language, incorrect reading order, and table structure problems. Review each flagged item and resolve before sign-off.

Supplement automated checking with manual screen reader testing using NVDA + Chrome (for PDF opened in browser) or JAWS + Adobe Acrobat Reader. Tab through all form fields and confirm each is announced with its correct Tooltip name. Navigate headings using the H key in NVDA/JAWS to verify heading hierarchy is correct.

Use the Acrobat Pro Tags panel and Order panel for visual inspection of tag structure and reading sequence.

Phase 4 — Quality Assurance and Publication (Ongoing)

Establish a sign-off checklist for each form before it is re-published:
  [ ] Tagged PDF confirmed (File > Properties > Description > "Tagged PDF: Yes")
  [ ] Full Accessibility Check passes with zero errors
  [ ] All form fields have Tooltip values confirmed via screen reader test
  [ ] Document language set (en-US or applicable language code)
  [ ] Document title set and displayed as window title
  [ ] Reading order verified manually
  [ ] At least one screen reader spot-check completed (NVDA or JAWS)

---

APPLICABLE WCAG 2.0 SUCCESS CRITERIA SUMMARY
----------------------------------------------

  SC 1.1.1 (Level A)    — Alt text for all informational images
  SC 1.3.1 (Level A)    — Tagged structure with correct hierarchy; table headers
  SC 1.3.2 (Level A)    — Logical reading order
  SC 2.4.2 (Level A)    — Document title set
  SC 2.4.4 (Level A)    — Descriptive hyperlink text
  SC 3.1.1 (Level A)    — Document language declared
  SC 3.3.2 (Level A)    — Labels or instructions for form fields (Tooltip/accessible name)
  SC 4.1.2 (Level A)    — Form fields expose name, role, value to assistive technology

All of the above are Level A requirements, meaning they are non-negotiable baseline requirements — no undue burden exception applies to Level A criteria unless the agency head makes a formal written determination and provides an alternative means of access.

---

TOOLING SUMMARY
----------------

  Adobe Acrobat Pro        — Primary remediation tool (tag editor, Reading Order tool, Prepare Form, Full Accessibility Check, Action Wizard for batch)
  NVDA (free) + Chrome     — Screen reader verification
  JAWS + Acrobat Reader    — Additional screen reader verification
  PAC 2024 (PDF Accessibility Checker) — Free standalone tool that validates PDF/UA conformance; useful as a second automated check alongside Acrobat's built-in checker

If your agency lacks Acrobat Pro licenses at scale, consider CommonLook PDF or Equidox as enterprise-grade PDF remediation platforms that integrate with Section 508 workflows and support batch processing with audit trails.

---

If you would like a phased remediation plan table with effort estimates by category, a procurement template for outsourcing this work to a 508 remediation vendor, or a form-by-form audit issue tracker template, I can provide those as well.
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅States PDFs must be Tagged PDFs with a correct tag hierarchy (Document, headings, paragraphs, tables, lists)
✅Mentions form fields must have accessible names (Tooltip/name field in Acrobat Pro form editor)
✅States all images in PDFs must have Alt text set in the tag properties
✅Mentions document language must be set in Document Properties
✅References a verification/testing step using Acrobat Pro's Full Accessibility Check or equivalent
Section 508 PDF Accessibility: Requirements and Remediation Process
===================================================================

Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act requires federal agencies to ensure their electronic and information technology, including PDF documents and forms, is accessible to people with disabilities. For PDFs, the relevant technical standard is WCAG 2.0 Level AA (incorporated by reference in the Revised 508 Standards) and PDF/UA (ISO 14289).

---

SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS FOR PDF ACCESSIBILITY
--------------------------------------------

1. Tagged PDFs with Correct Tag Hierarchy
   PDFs must be "Tagged PDFs" — meaning they contain a hidden tag structure that screen readers use to interpret content order and meaning. The tag tree must follow a logical hierarchy:
   - <Document> as the root tag
   - Headings: <H1>, <H2>, <H3>, etc. used in proper nesting order
   - Paragraphs: <P> tags for body text
   - Tables: <Table>, <TR>, <TH>, <TD> tags with proper header associations
   - Lists: <L>, <LI>, <Lbl>, <LBody> tags
   - Figures/Images: <Figure> tags with Alt text
   - Form fields: <Form> tags
   Tags must reflect the logical reading order of the document.

2. Accessible Form Fields
   All interactive form fields must have accessible names so screen readers can announce the purpose of each field. In Adobe Acrobat Pro's form editor, this is set via the Tooltip field in each form field's properties dialog. The Tooltip becomes the accessible name read by assistive technology. Every text field, checkbox, radio button, dropdown, and button must have a descriptive, meaningful Tooltip/name.

3. Alternative Text for Images
   All meaningful images (figures, charts, logos, icons) must have alternative text (Alt text) set in the tag properties. In Acrobat Pro, this is done by opening the Tags panel, right-clicking the <Figure> tag, selecting "Properties," and entering descriptive Alt text. Decorative images should be marked as artifacts so screen readers skip them.

4. Document Language
   The primary language of the PDF must be specified in Document Properties. In Acrobat Pro: File > Properties > Advanced tab > Reading Options > Language. Setting the correct language (e.g., "EN-US") allows screen readers to use the correct pronunciation and speech engine.

5. Logical Reading Order
   The order of tags in the tag tree must match the intended reading order of the document. Use the Order panel or Tags panel in Acrobat Pro to verify and correct reading order.

6. Color and Contrast
   Text must meet minimum contrast ratios (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text) per WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.3. Information must not be conveyed by color alone.

7. Bookmarks
   PDFs with 21 or more pages must include bookmarks (an outline/navigation structure) to allow users to navigate without reading every page.

8. Document Title
   The document title must be set in Document Properties (File > Properties > Description tab > Title) and configured to display the title (rather than filename) in the title bar (Initial View tab > Show: Document Title).

9. No Inaccessible Security Settings
   Document security settings must not block screen reader access. Assistive technology access must be permitted.

10. Tables: Headers and Scope
    Data tables must use proper header cells (<TH>) with Scope attributes (Column, Row, Both) to associate headers with data cells.

---

REMEDIATION PROCESS FOR 200 PDF FORMS
---------------------------------------

Phase 1: Inventory and Prioritization
- Catalog all 200 forms with metadata (form name, size, complexity, usage frequency).
- Prioritize based on public usage volume — highest-traffic forms first.
- Identify whether source files (Word, InDesign, etc.) are available, as remediating from the source is faster than remediating the PDF directly.

Phase 2: Tooling Setup
Required tools:
- Adobe Acrobat Pro DC (or current version) — primary remediation tool
- PAC 3 or PAC 2024 (PDF Accessibility Checker) — free tool for PDF/UA validation
- NVDA (free) or JAWS screen reader — for manual testing
- Microsoft Word or InDesign — if regenerating from source

Phase 3: Automated Pre-Scan
- Run Adobe Acrobat Pro's Full Accessibility Check (Tools > Accessibility > Full Check) on each PDF to identify categories of failures.
- This check evaluates tagged structure, alt text, form field names, reading order, language, contrast, and more.
- Use results to estimate remediation effort per document and group similar issues for batch fixing.

Phase 4: Remediation (Per Document)
For each PDF form, perform the following steps:

Step 1 — Add/Repair Tags:
  - If the PDF has no tags: Use Acrobat's "Add Tags to Document" auto-tagger (Tools > Accessibility > Add Tags to Document), then manually correct the resulting tag tree.
  - Open the Tags panel to review the tag hierarchy.
  - Correct heading levels, ensure paragraphs use <P>, tables use full table markup.

Step 2 — Fix Reading Order:
  - Use the Reading Order tool or Order panel to verify content flows logically.
  - Reorder tags as needed by dragging in the Tags panel.

Step 3 — Set Alt Text on Images:
  - In the Tags panel, locate each <Figure> tag.
  - Right-click > Properties > Alternate Text field — enter descriptive alt text.
  - Mark purely decorative images as Artifacts (right-click > Change Tag to Artifact).

Step 4 — Remediate Form Fields:
  - Open the Forms editor (Tools > Prepare Form).
  - Double-click each form field to open its properties.
  - In the General tab, fill in the Tooltip field with a clear, descriptive label that matches the visible label on the form.
  - Ensure tab order (Tab Order in the Pages panel) follows a logical sequence.
  - Verify required fields are marked as required.

Step 5 — Set Document Language:
  - File > Properties > Advanced > Reading Options > Language — set to the appropriate language code (e.g., EN-US).

Step 6 — Set Document Title and Metadata:
  - File > Properties > Description — set Title, Author, Subject.
  - File > Properties > Initial View — set "Show" to "Document Title."

Step 7 — Add Bookmarks (if 21+ pages):
  - Use the Bookmarks panel to create a navigable outline matching the heading structure.

Step 8 — Fix Table Headers:
  - In the Tags panel, right-click <TH> cells > Properties > set Scope attribute.
  - Use the Table Editor (in the Reading Order tool) to assign header relationships.

Phase 5: Verification and Testing
- Run the Full Accessibility Check in Adobe Acrobat Pro (Tools > Accessibility > Full Check) — this must pass with zero errors for all accessibility categories.
- Validate against PDF/UA standard using PAC 3 or PAC 2024 (free tool from PDF Association).
- Perform manual testing with a screen reader (NVDA + Firefox or JAWS + Internet Explorer/Edge):
  - Navigate headings, form fields, tables, and images.
  - Confirm all form fields are announced with correct labels.
  - Confirm images are announced with appropriate alt text.
  - Confirm reading order is logical.
- Conduct testing with a user with a disability if possible.

Phase 6: Quality Assurance and Documentation
- Document the remediation performed on each form.
- Maintain a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT) or Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) for each form if required.
- Establish a process so future forms are created accessibly from the start (accessible templates, author training).

Phase 7: Publication and Monitoring
- Replace inaccessible PDFs on the website with remediated versions.
- Set up periodic re-checks (e.g., annually) to ensure PDFs remain accessible as content is updated.
- Establish an accessibility feedback mechanism on the website so users can report barriers.

---

ESTIMATED EFFORT
----------------
- Simple text-heavy PDF (few images, no forms): 1-3 hours per document
- Complex form with many fields, tables, images: 4-8+ hours per document
- For 200 forms, consider engaging a Section 508 remediation vendor for volume efficiency.
- Some vendors offer batch remediation using automated tools supplemented by manual review.

---

KEY STANDARDS AND REFERENCES
------------------------------
- Revised Section 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194), effective January 18, 2018
- WCAG 2.0 Level AA (incorporated by reference in the Revised 508 Standards)
- PDF/UA (ISO 14289-1) — the international standard for accessible PDFs
- GSA's Section508.gov — provides guidance, training, and testing resources
- HHS Section 508 Program Office guidance on PDF accessibility
Prompt: Our agency is procuring a new case management system. What Section 508 requirements do we need to include in our RFP, how should we evaluate the vendor's VPAT responses, and what are the most common VPAT deficiencies we should watch for?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅References FAR clause 52.239-2 or FAR Subpart 39.2 as the procurement mandate
✅Specifies requiring a VPAT 2.x (WCAG Edition) from vendors — not an older template version
✅Lists at least 3 common VPAT deficiencies (e.g., outdated template, unsupported 'Supports' claims without evidence, overuse of 'Not Applicable', missing testing methodology)
✅Recommends including remediation SLAs in the contract (e.g., timeline for fixing critical vs. moderate issues post-award)
✅States that the VPAT must match the exact product version being procured
Section 508 Requirements for Case Management System Procurement

This guidance covers three areas: what to include in your RFP, how to evaluate VPAT responses, and what deficiencies to watch for.

---

PART 1: RFP REQUIREMENTS

Regulatory Mandate

Your RFP must include FAR Clause 52.239-2 (Access to Diagnostic and Therapeutic Equipment), which implements the Section 508 procurement requirements under FAR Subpart 39.2. This clause mandates that ICT delivered under the contract conform to the Revised Section 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194), effective January 18, 2018.

Core Provisions to Reference in the RFP

1. Applicable Technical Standards
   - E205 (Web / Software / Electronic Documents): WCAG 2.0 Level A and AA — this is the primary standard for a case management system's web-based or desktop software interface.
   - Chapter 3 (Functional Performance Criteria): Must support users without vision, without color perception, without hearing, without speech, with limited fine motor control, and with limited cognitive/language/learning abilities.
   - Chapter 5 (Software): Applies to native software components not covered by WCAG 2.0.
   - Chapter 6 (Support Documentation and Services): All user guides, help documentation, and support services must also be accessible.

2. Required Deliverables — VPAT / ACR
   Include the following language:
   "The vendor shall provide an Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) using the VPAT 2.x (WCAG Edition) template, published by the Information Technology Industry Council (ITI), within 30 days of contract award. The ACR must correspond to the exact product version being delivered under this contract. An ACR for a different product version will not be accepted."

3. Testing Methodology Requirements
   Require vendors to document the methodology used to produce their VPAT, including:
   - Automated tools used (e.g., axe-core, WAVE, Lighthouse)
   - Screen readers tested (e.g., JAWS + Chrome, NVDA + Firefox, VoiceOver + Safari)
   - Manual testing performed (keyboard-only navigation, color contrast verification, zoom to 200%)
   - Dates of testing and versions of assistive technologies used

4. Assistive Technology Compatibility
   Specify the assistive technologies the system must support consistent with your agency's environment, at minimum:
   - JAWS (current and prior major version) with Google Chrome
   - NVDA with Mozilla Firefox
   - Windows Magnifier and/or ZoomText
   - Dragon NaturallySpeaking (speech input)
   - Microsoft Narrator (where applicable)

5. Remediation SLAs (Post-Award)
   Include enforceable remediation timelines in the contract. Recommended SLAs by severity:
   - Critical (keyboard trap, complete screen reader blockage, login inaccessible): 30 calendar days
   - High (major workflows inaccessible, missing alt text on functional images, form fields unlabeled): 60 calendar days
   - Moderate (partial support failures, inconsistent navigation, contrast failures): 90 calendar days
   - Low (minor informational issues, low-impact WCAG AA gaps): 180 calendar days

   These SLAs should run from the date of written notice from the Contracting Officer's Representative (COR) or agency accessibility team.

6. Ongoing ACR Refresh
   Require the vendor to provide an updated ACR with each major product release or significant update during the contract period of performance.

7. Undue Burden Provisions
   If the vendor claims undue burden for any provision, require:
   - Written justification referencing the specific Section 508 provision at issue
   - Proposed alternative means of access for affected users
   - Agency COR review and acceptance before any exception is granted

---

PART 2: EVALUATING VENDOR VPAT RESPONSES

Step 1 — Verify Template Version
Confirm the vendor used the VPAT 2.x (WCAG Edition) template. Older templates (VPAT 1.x) do not map to the Revised 508 Standards and are not acceptable. The VPAT 2.x WCAG Edition includes tables for WCAG 2.x Level A and AA success criteria, Functional Performance Criteria, and Chapter 5/6 provisions. If the submitted ACR uses any other template format, reject it and request a conforming document.

Step 2 — Verify Product Version Match
Confirm that the product name and version documented in the ACR match exactly the version being delivered. A VPAT for version 4.1 is not valid evidence for version 5.0. Require the vendor to certify in writing that the ACR covers the procured version.

Step 3 — Evaluate Completeness
Every WCAG 2.0 Level A and Level AA success criterion must have an entry. For each criterion, look for:
- A conformance level: Supports, Partially Supports, Does Not Support, or Not Applicable
- Substantive remarks — not just a repeated conformance level
   - "Supports" entries must explain HOW the product meets the criterion
   - "Partially Supports" entries must identify WHICH scenarios succeed and which fail
   - "Does Not Support" entries must describe the nature of the failure
   - "Not Applicable" entries must justify why the criterion does not apply

Step 4 — Assess Testing Methodology
The ACR should include a methodology section. Red flags:
- No testing methodology disclosed
- Only automated scanning listed (automated tools catch only 30-40% of issues)
- No screen reader testing documented
- Testing performed on a different version than the one being procured

Step 5 — Score Weighted Criteria
Weight your evaluation toward criteria most critical for a case management system:
- 2.1.1 Keyboard (A): Full keyboard operability — critical for screen reader and motor-impaired users
- 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A): Programmatic names for all UI components — essential for JAWS/NVDA compatibility
- 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A): Form field labels, table structure, heading hierarchy
- 3.3.1 Error Identification (A) and 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions (A): Error handling in data entry workflows
- 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum (AA): Text contrast at 4.5:1 ratio
- 2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA): Visible keyboard focus indicator throughout

Step 6 — Request a Demonstration
For top-ranked vendors, require a live accessibility demonstration with an agency assistive technology user or certified accessibility tester. VPAT claims should be verifiable against the live product.

---

PART 3: COMMON VPAT DEFICIENCIES TO WATCH FOR

1. Outdated VPAT Template
The vendor submits a VPAT 1.x document or a proprietary format rather than the ITI VPAT 2.x (WCAG Edition). These templates do not cover all Revised 508 Standards criteria and cannot serve as valid ACRs under the current regulatory framework.

2. Unsupported "Supports" Claims Without Evidence
The vendor marks criteria as "Supports" with no explanatory remarks — or with boilerplate text like "Our product fully supports all WCAG criteria." Without specific, verifiable evidence of how conformance is achieved, these claims have no probative value. Each "Supports" entry must include substantive explanation.

3. Overuse of "Not Applicable"
Vendors frequently mark criteria as "Not Applicable" to avoid disclosing failures. For a case management system, very few WCAG 2.0 AA criteria are genuinely not applicable. Be especially skeptical of "Not Applicable" for: 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships), 2.1.1 (Keyboard), 3.3.1 (Error Identification), and 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value). Require justification for every "Not Applicable" entry.
