4. Missing or Vague Testing Methodology
The ACR omits the testing methodology section, lists only automated scanning, or fails to name specific assistive technologies and versions. Automated tools alone are insufficient. A credible VPAT must reference manual keyboard testing, named screen readers (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver), contrast analysis tools, and zoom testing.

5. Version Mismatch
The ACR covers a prior product version, a demo environment, or a "similar" product rather than the exact version being procured. Conformance data from a different version is not valid evidence of the procured version's conformance. This is one of the most common — and consequential — deficiencies in federal procurements.

6. Blanket "Partially Supports" Without Scope
The vendor marks a criterion as "Partially Supports" without describing which components or workflows partially conform and which do not. A conforming ACR must scope the partial support — for example: "SC 2.1.1: The main navigation and search functions support keyboard operation. The bulk-upload module does not currently support keyboard-only operation."

7. No Functional Performance Criteria Coverage
Chapter 3 (Functional Performance Criteria) is omitted entirely or marked "Not Applicable" without justification. These criteria ensure usability for users across all disability categories and must be addressed for any ICT that does not fully satisfy the technical standards.

8. No Chapter 6 (Support Documentation) Coverage
Vendors frequently omit coverage of user guides, help content, training materials, and support services. All documentation delivered under the contract must also meet accessibility requirements under Chapter 6 / E205.

---

SUMMARY CHECKLIST FOR PROCUREMENT OFFICERS

RFP Must Include:
[ ] FAR Clause 52.239-2 (FAR Subpart 39.2)
[ ] Reference to 36 CFR Part 1194 and applicable provisions (E205, Chapter 3, Chapter 5, Chapter 6)
[ ] Requirement for VPAT 2.x (WCAG Edition) matching the exact procured version
[ ] Documented testing methodology requirement
[ ] Specified assistive technology compatibility requirements
[ ] Remediation SLAs by severity (Critical: 30 days, High: 60 days, Moderate: 90 days)
[ ] Updated ACR requirement with each major release

VPAT Evaluation — Watch For:
[ ] Wrong template version (VPAT 1.x or proprietary format)
[ ] Unsupported "Supports" claims (no explanatory remarks)
[ ] Excessive "Not Applicable" without justification
[ ] Missing testing methodology or automated-only testing
[ ] Version mismatch between ACR and procured product
[ ] Vague "Partially Supports" without scope description
[ ] Missing Chapter 3 (Functional Performance) or Chapter 6 (Support Documentation) coverage
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅References FAR clause 52.239-2 or FAR Subpart 39.2 as the procurement mandate
✅Specifies requiring a VPAT 2.x (WCAG Edition) from vendors — not an older template version
✅Lists at least 3 common VPAT deficiencies (e.g., outdated template, unsupported Supports claims without evidence, overuse of Not Applicable, missing testing methodology)
✅Recommends including remediation SLAs in the contract (e.g., timeline for fixing critical vs. moderate issues post-award)
✅States that the VPAT must match the exact product version being procured
Section 508 Requirements for Case Management System Procurement: RFP Language, VPAT Evaluation, and Common Deficiencies
==========================================================================================================================

1. LEGAL BASIS AND PROCUREMENT MANDATE
---------------------------------------

All federal IT procurements must comply with Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, as amended (29 U.S.C. § 794d), and the Access Board's revised standards published at 36 CFR Part 1194 (effective January 18, 2018). On the acquisition side, the primary regulatory hook is FAR Subpart 39.2 — Information and Communication Technology, which implements Section 508 requirements in federal contracting. Specifically, FAR clause 52.239-2 (Access to Nonpublic Information) and the broader requirements of FAR 39.203 mandate that agencies must include Section 508 standards in solicitations and contracts for ICT supplies and services.

Your RFP should cite 36 CFR Part 1194 directly, reference the applicable Technical Standards (typically WCAG 2.1 Level AA for web-based case management software under Chapter 5 of the Revised 508 Standards), and incorporate FAR Subpart 39.2 as the procurement authority. The contracting officer should coordinate with the agency's Section 508 Program Manager to ensure the correct standards apply.

2. WHAT TO INCLUDE IN YOUR RFP
--------------------------------

A. Mandatory 508 Conformance Statement
Include a statement requiring vendors to demonstrate how their proposed solution meets or exceeds the applicable Section 508 Technical Standards, specifically:
  - Chapter 5 (Software) — for application functionality
  - Chapter 6 (Support Documentation and Services) — for user guides, help content, and training materials
  - WCAG 2.1 Level AA — for any web-based components (via Chapter 7 / E205)

B. VPAT Requirement
Require vendors to submit a completed Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT), version 2.x — specifically the WCAG Edition or INT (International) Edition, not older pre-2017 templates. The VPAT 2.x (published by the Information Technology Industry Council, ITI) maps to the Revised 508 Standards and WCAG 2.1. Older VPAT formats based on the original 1194.21/1194.22 standards are not acceptable for procurements under the Revised 508 Standards.

The VPAT must:
  - Reflect the exact product name, version number, and release date being proposed
  - Be completed no more than 12–18 months prior to submission (or updated to match the proposed version)
  - Include the name, title, and contact information of the evaluator
  - Describe the evaluation methodology used (e.g., automated testing tools, manual testing, assistive technology combinations)

C. Testing Methodology Disclosure
Require vendors to describe how they tested their product:
  - Which automated tools were used (e.g., axe, WAVE, Deque tools)
  - Which assistive technologies were tested (e.g., JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver, Dragon NaturallySpeaking)
  - Whether testing was conducted by an independent third party or internally
  - The scope of testing (all modules, specific workflows, representative sample)

D. Remediation SLAs
The RFP should specify, and the resulting contract must include, remediation service level agreements (SLAs) for accessibility defects discovered post-award:
  - Critical barriers (completely blocks access for users with disabilities): remediation within 30 days of written notice
  - Significant deficiencies (substantially impairs access): remediation within 60–90 days
  - Moderate issues (workarounds exist but accessibility is degraded): remediation within 180 days or within the next scheduled release cycle
  - SLAs should be tied to contract performance metrics and, where appropriate, financial penalties or cure notices

E. Ongoing Conformance
Include requirements for:
  - Updated VPATs with each major release or version update
  - Notification to the contracting officer within 30 days of any accessibility regression introduced in a new release
  - Access to the vendor's accessibility roadmap and bug tracking process

F. Market Research and Exceptions
Document that you conducted pre-solicitation accessibility market research. If no fully conformant solution exists, the agency may need to invoke an exception under 36 CFR 1194.3 (e.g., undue burden or fundamental alteration), which requires written documentation approved by the agency head.

3. HOW TO EVALUATE VENDOR VPAT RESPONSES
------------------------------------------

A. Verify Version and Template Currency
Check that the submitted VPAT uses version 2.x (the current ITI template). Look for the version notation in the document header. Reject or flag submissions using the legacy VPAT 1.x format, which does not map to the Revised 508 Standards.

B. Match VPAT to Proposed Product Version
Confirm that the VPAT explicitly identifies the product version that matches the version the vendor is offering. A VPAT for version 3.1 is not valid for a proposal offering version 4.0. This is a frequent and significant discrepancy.

C. Review Conformance Level Entries
For each criterion, vendors may claim: Supports, Partially Supports, Does Not Support, or Not Applicable. Evaluate each entry:
  - "Supports" claims must be accompanied by meaningful remarks explaining how the feature works accessibly. Bare "Supports" with no remarks is a red flag.
  - "Partially Supports" entries should describe specifically what works and what does not, and ideally identify a remediation timeline.
  - "Not Applicable" claims require justification. Overuse of "Not Applicable" — particularly for common criteria like keyboard access (2.1.1), focus order (2.4.3), or color contrast (1.4.3) — is a significant deficiency.
  - "Does Not Support" entries should be understood as known accessibility barriers.

D. Assess Testing Methodology Quality
A credible VPAT will describe:
  - Specific assistive technologies and browser combinations tested
  - Whether an independent accessibility auditor was involved
  - The date of testing relative to the proposed product version
VPATs with vague methodology sections (e.g., "tested internally") with no specifics warrant skepticism.

E. Score or Weight the VPAT in Evaluation Criteria
Consider including accessibility conformance as a rated technical factor in the source selection, not merely a pass/fail checkbox. Agencies with strong 508 programs may weight third-party-validated VPATs higher than self-attested ones.

F. Request a Demonstration
For critical workflows (e.g., case intake, search, document management), request a live accessibility demonstration using JAWS or NVDA as part of the technical evaluation. This can surface gaps not reflected in the VPAT.

4. MOST COMMON VPAT DEFICIENCIES TO WATCH FOR
------------------------------------------------

1. Outdated Template Version
   The vendor submits a VPAT using the pre-2017 format (VPAT 1.x), which references the original 1194.21 (desktop software) and 1194.22 (web) standards rather than the Revised 508 Standards. This template is not aligned with WCAG 2.1 Level AA and should be rejected.

2. Product Version Mismatch
   The VPAT covers a different version than what is being proposed. For example, the VPAT was written for version 2.5 but the vendor is offering version 3.0, which may have undergone significant UI changes. Always require the VPAT to match the exact proposed version.

3. Unsupported "Supports" Claims Without Evidence
   A vendor marks dozens of success criteria as "Supports" but provides no remarks, no description of implementation, and no testing evidence. This is the most common and most misleading deficiency. A credible VPAT explains how conformance is achieved for non-trivial criteria.

4. Overuse or Misuse of "Not Applicable"
   Vendors mark critical accessibility criteria as "Not Applicable" without legitimate justification. Common examples: marking keyboard accessibility (SC 2.1.1) as N/A for a web application, or marking color contrast (SC 1.4.3) as N/A. These criteria apply to virtually all software products.

5. Missing or Vague Testing Methodology
   The VPAT does not describe how the product was tested — no mention of assistive technologies, browsers, testing tools, or whether the evaluation was conducted by a qualified accessibility professional. Without this, the VPAT cannot be verified or trusted.

6. Stale VPAT — Not Updated for Recent Releases
   The product has had multiple releases since the VPAT was written, but the vendor resubmits an unchanged document. New features, redesigns, or UI framework changes can introduce new barriers not reflected in an old VPAT.

7. Incomplete Coverage of Product Modules
   The VPAT covers only the main dashboard or homepage, while ignoring complex workflows like case creation, document upload, search results, data entry forms, or reporting modules — which are often the least accessible parts of case management systems.

8. No Remarks for "Partially Supports" Entries
   Vendors acknowledge partial conformance but provide no detail about what works, what does not, and what the remediation plan is. This provides insufficient information for the agency to assess risk.

9. No Contact Information for the Evaluator
   The VPAT lacks the name, title, organization, and contact information of the person or firm that conducted the evaluation, making it impossible to follow up with questions.

5. SUMMARY CHECKLIST FOR YOUR RFP
-----------------------------------

[ ] Cite FAR Subpart 39.2 and incorporate FAR 39.203 requirements
[ ] Reference 36 CFR Part 1194 (Revised 508 Standards)
[ ] Require VPAT 2.x (WCAG Edition or INT Edition) — reject VPAT 1.x
[ ] Require VPAT to match the exact product version being procured
[ ] Require disclosure of testing methodology and assistive technologies used
[ ] Include remediation SLAs: critical (30 days), significant (60-90 days), moderate (180 days)
[ ] Require updated VPATs with each major version release post-award
[ ] Consider weighted evaluation factor for accessibility in source selection
[ ] Reserve right to conduct independent accessibility testing as part of evaluation
[ ] Document market research on accessible alternatives per FAR 39.203(c)

---
Prepared: 2026-05-16
Scope: Federal Agency Procurement Advisory — Section 508 / ICT Accessibility
Prompt: Our small federal agency has a 15-year-old legacy grants management system that would cost approximately $3.5M to fully remediate for Section 508 compliance, which exceeds our entire IT budget. Can we claim an undue burden exception? What is the legal basis, the process we must follow, and what alternative access must we provide?
With Skill
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Correctly states the legal basis: E202.5 of the Revised Section 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194) and/or 29 U.S.C. § 794d
✅States that undue burden means 'significant difficulty or expense' and lists factors to consider (agency resources, size, cost of compliance)
✅Requires a written determination signed by the agency head (or delegated official) documenting the undue burden finding
✅States that an alternative means of access must still be provided to people with disabilities — not a full exemption from accessibility obligations
✅Notes that the exception must be re-evaluated if the ICT is altered or replaced
SECTION 508 UNDUE BURDEN EXCEPTION: LEGAL BASIS, PROCESS, AND ALTERNATIVE ACCESS REQUIREMENTS
================================================================================================

Your situation — a legacy grants management system requiring $3.5M in remediation against a constrained IT budget — is precisely the scenario the undue burden exception was designed to address. Here is a complete analysis.

---

1. LEGAL BASIS
--------------

The undue burden exception is grounded in two authorities:

  a) 29 U.S.C. § 794d — Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, as amended, which establishes the accessibility mandate for federal ICT and expressly authorizes agencies to claim an exception when compliance would impose an undue burden.

  b) E202.5 of the Revised Section 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194) — the operative regulatory provision. E202.5 states that agencies are not required to conform to the Standards where doing so would impose an "undue burden" on the agency, defined as a significant difficulty or expense.

Additionally, the legacy ICT exception under E202.7 of 36 CFR Part 1194 may independently apply to ICT that was acquired or deployed before January 18, 2018 (the effective date of the Revised Standards) and has not been altered. However, the undue burden exception provides the stronger, more thoroughly documented path for systems that are still actively in use and maintained.

---

2. DEFINITION AND FACTORS: WHAT "UNDUE BURDEN" MEANS
------------------------------------------------------

Under E202.5, "undue burden" means significant difficulty or expense. This is not an absolute dollar threshold — it is a proportionality analysis. The Revised Standards require agencies to consider all of the following factors when making the determination:

  - The nature and cost of the action needed to achieve compliance
  - The overall financial resources of the agency or component involved
  - The number of employees at the agency or component
  - The type of operations of the agency or component
  - The impact of the action on the operations of the agency or component
  - The agency's total IT budget (not just the budget of a single program or office)

For your situation, relevant evidence to document would include:
  - The $3.5M remediation estimate (with vendor quotes or independent cost analysis)
  - Your total agency IT budget for the fiscal year
  - The ratio of remediation cost to overall IT budget (if $3.5M equals or exceeds total IT budget, this is strong evidence)
  - Whether the remediation cost would crowd out other mission-critical IT obligations
  - The age and technology stack of the 15-year-old system
  - Any scheduled replacement or modernization timeline

---

3. REQUIRED PROCESS: WRITTEN DETERMINATION
-------------------------------------------

E202.5 imposes mandatory procedural requirements. An agency cannot simply decide internally that compliance is too expensive — the exception requires formal documentation. The specific steps are:

  Step 1 — Document the ICT at issue
    Identify the specific system (grants management system), its functions, the user population, and the specific Section 508 provisions that cannot be met.

  Step 2 — Calculate the cost of full compliance
    Obtain formal vendor quotes or independent cost estimates. The $3.5M figure must be documented and defensible, not a rough estimate. Break down by remediation activity (e.g., front-end UI remediation, back-end accessibility, document remediation, testing).

  Step 3 — Assess agency resources
    Document your agency's total financial resources: IT budget, overall appropriations, and any competing capital obligations. Show the proportionality analysis.

  Step 4 — Obtain a signed written determination from the agency head (or properly delegated official)
    This is a hard requirement under E202.5. The determination must be:
      - In writing
      - Signed by the agency head or an official to whom the agency head has formally delegated this authority (typically the CIO or Deputy Administrator)
      - Specific to the ICT in question — a blanket agency-wide determination is not sufficient
      - Based on the documented factors above

  Step 5 — Retain documentation
    The written determination and supporting evidence must be retained and made available for audit by oversight bodies (e.g., OMB, GAO, agency Inspector General, or in the event of a complaint to the Access Board or DOJ).

  Step 6 — Re-evaluate when the ICT is altered or replaced
    The exception is not permanent. Under E202.5, the undue burden determination must be re-evaluated whenever the ICT is substantially altered or replaced. If your agency undertakes a modernization effort, the exception lapses and the new or altered system must achieve full 508 conformance.

---

4. ALTERNATIVE MEANS OF ACCESS — THIS IS NOT A FULL EXEMPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------

This is the most critical point: claiming an undue burden exception does NOT exempt your agency from all accessibility obligations. E202.5 explicitly requires that the agency still provide an alternative means of access to the information or functionality for individuals with disabilities.

The alternative means of access must be:
  - Genuinely equivalent in the information or service it provides — it cannot be a lesser or degraded substitute
  - Available without unreasonable delay (comparable to the time a non-disabled user can access the system)
  - Provided at no additional cost to the user with a disability
  - Documented and publicized so that users with disabilities know how to access it

Common acceptable alternatives for a grants management system may include:
  - A staffed accessible helpline (phone or video relay) where agency personnel complete transactions on behalf of the user
  - A fully accessible PDF or HTML alternative format for grant applications and status reporting
  - A dedicated accessibility contact (email/phone) with a documented service-level response commitment
  - In-person assistance at an accessible agency office

The alternative means must not simply tell users to "call us" without ensuring the person answering can actually complete the full transaction.

---

5. RE-EVALUATION OBLIGATION WHEN ICT IS ALTERED OR REPLACED
------------------------------------------------------------

Under E202.5 of 36 CFR Part 1194, the undue burden determination is tied to the current state of the ICT. If your agency:
  - Undertakes any substantial alteration of the grants management system (new modules, UI redesign, platform migration, API integration)
  - Replaces the system entirely through a modernization or re-procurement effort

...the exception must be re-evaluated. Any alteration that is within scope of the agency's resources must be made accessible at the time of that alteration, even if full system remediation remains an undue burden. In practice, this means your next procurement or upgrade project must include 508 conformance requirements from day one, and FAR clause 52.239-2 (for contract-based systems) must be applied.

---

6. STRATEGIC RECOMMENDATIONS
-----------------------------

Given your circumstances, here is a practical path forward:

  a) Pursue a partial remediation strategy alongside the undue burden claim. Even if full remediation is an undue burden, the agency should remediate the highest-impact barriers (e.g., keyboard accessibility, screen reader compatibility for core grant application workflows) with whatever resources are available. This demonstrates good faith and reduces legal risk.

  b) Document the system replacement timeline. If a system modernization is already planned (even in a 3–5 year horizon), include that timeline in the written determination and ensure the replacement procurement requires full 508 conformance from the outset via the VPAT/ACR requirement and FAR 52.239-2.

  c) Contact the U.S. Access Board. The Access Board provides free technical assistance (508@access-board.gov) and can advise on the adequacy of your written determination and alternative access plan before you finalize them.

  d) Review E202.7 (Legacy ICT exception). If the system was deployed before January 18, 2018 and has not been substantially altered, you may have an independent exemption under E202.7. Consult your agency counsel on whether this applies and whether it is simpler to document than a full undue burden analysis.

  e) Brief your agency's Section 508 Program Manager, General Counsel, CIO, and (if required) your IG on the determination before it is signed, to ensure it will withstand audit scrutiny.

---

SUMMARY TABLE
-------------

| Element                        | Requirement                                                      |
|-------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Legal authority                | 29 U.S.C. § 794d; E202.5 of 36 CFR Part 1194                   |
| Standard                       | "Significant difficulty or expense" — proportionality analysis  |
| Key factors                    | Cost of compliance, agency size, total financial resources       |
| Written determination required | Yes — signed by agency head or delegated official               |
| Full exemption from 508?       | No — alternative means of access must still be provided         |
| Re-evaluation trigger          | Any alteration or replacement of the ICT                        |
| Documentation retention        | Required — must be audit-ready                                   |

---

Cite these provisions in your written determination:
  - 29 U.S.C. § 794d(a)(1)(B) — undue burden authorization
  - 36 CFR § 1194, E202.5 — Revised Standards undue burden provision
  - 36 CFR § 1194, E202.7 — Legacy ICT exception (if applicable)
Baseline (No Skill)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Correctly states the legal basis: E202.5 of the Revised Section 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194) and/or 29 U.S.C. § 794d
✅States that undue burden means 'significant difficulty or expense' and lists factors to consider (agency resources, size, cost of compliance)
✅Requires a written determination signed by the agency head (or delegated official) documenting the undue burden finding
✅States that an alternative means of access must still be provided to people with disabilities — not a full exemption from accessibility obligations
✅Notes that the exception must be re-evaluated if the ICT is altered or replaced
SECTION 508 UNDUE BURDEN EXCEPTION: GUIDANCE FOR FEDERAL AGENCIES

QUESTION: Can a small federal agency claim an undue burden exception for a legacy grants management system with $3.5M remediation costs that exceed the entire IT budget?

SHORT ANSWER: Yes, an undue burden exception may be available, but it is not a full exemption from accessibility obligations. The agency must follow a specific process, document the determination formally, and still provide alternative means of access to people with disabilities.

---

1. LEGAL BASIS

The undue burden exception for federal agencies is grounded in two primary authorities:

a) Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, as amended — codified at 29 U.S.C. § 794d. This statute requires federal agencies to ensure that electronic and information technology (EIT) developed, procured, maintained, or used by the federal government is accessible to people with disabilities, but it includes provisions for exceptions where compliance would impose an undue burden.

b) E202.5 of the Revised Section 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194), published by the U.S. Access Board (effective January 18, 2018). This provision explicitly establishes the Undue Burden Exception, stating that an agency is not required to comply with Section 508 requirements where doing so would impose an "undue burden" on the agency. This is the operative regulatory provision governing the process and scope of the exception.

---

2. WHAT "UNDUE BURDEN" MEANS AND THE FACTORS CONSIDERED

Under E202.5 and the broader framework of Section 508, "undue burden" is defined as significant difficulty or expense. This is not a bright-line financial threshold — it is a fact-specific determination that must weigh multiple factors, including:

- The overall financial resources of the agency as a whole (not just the component or program office managing the system)
- The size of the agency, including number of employees
- The nature and cost of the specific accessibility compliance measures required
- The impact of the compliance cost relative to the agency's total IT budget and annual appropriations
- Whether alternative approaches exist that would reduce cost while still improving accessibility
- The type of operation of the agency and the financial impact on the agency's overall program

In your scenario, the $3.5M remediation cost relative to an entire IT budget is a strong factual predicate for an undue burden finding — but it must be documented and assessed against all available resources, not just the immediate IT budget line.

---

3. REQUIRED PROCESS: WRITTEN DETERMINATION BY AGENCY HEAD

The undue burden exception is NOT self-executing. Agencies cannot simply decide internally that compliance is too expensive and move on. The Revised Section 508 Standards require a formal process:

Step 1 — Document the Finding: The agency must prepare a written determination that explains the basis for the undue burden claim. This documentation must include:
  - A description of the ICT (Information and Communications Technology) at issue (the legacy grants management system)
  - The specific accessibility requirements that cannot be met
  - A detailed analysis of the costs of compliance (the $3.5M remediation estimate)
  - An analysis of the agency's available resources and why compliance would impose significant difficulty or expense
  - A description of the alternative means of access that will be provided

Step 2 — Signed by Agency Head or Designee: The written determination must be signed by the agency head or a duly authorized official to whom the agency head has formally delegated this authority. This cannot be a line manager or program officer decision alone — it requires senior leadership accountability.

Step 3 — Maintain the Documentation: The agency must retain this documentation and make it available as needed (e.g., for audits, congressional oversight, or complaints filed with oversight bodies).

Step 4 — Periodic Re-evaluation: The undue burden determination is not permanent. It must be revisited and re-evaluated when circumstances change — specifically, if the ICT is subsequently altered, upgraded, or replaced, the agency must reassess whether full compliance is now achievable.

---

4. ALTERNATIVE MEANS OF ACCESS IS STILL REQUIRED

This is a critical point: the undue burden exception does NOT excuse the agency from all accessibility obligations. Even where a valid undue burden determination is made, the agency must still provide an alternative means of access to the information or functionality for individuals with disabilities.

This means the agency must:
- Identify what the grants management system does and what outputs/functions people with disabilities need
- Provide a functionally equivalent alternative pathway — this could include:
  * Accessible document versions of key data outputs
  * A staffed help desk or point of contact who can assist users with disabilities in accessing grants information
  * Accessible alternative formats (e.g., accessible PDFs, data exports in accessible formats)
  * Phone-based or in-person assistance to complete grant-related tasks
- Document what alternative means of access will be provided in the written undue burden determination

The alternative access must be genuinely usable and not impose unreasonable burdens on the individual with a disability. Providing a phone number that goes unanswered, for example, would not satisfy this requirement.

---

5. RE-EVALUATION WHEN ICT IS ALTERED OR REPLACED

The undue burden exception is tied to the current state of the ICT and the agency's current resources. If the grants management system is:
- Significantly altered or upgraded
- Replaced with a new system
- Subject to a new procurement or modernization initiative

...then the agency must re-evaluate whether the undue burden exception still applies or whether the new system must be made fully Section 508 compliant from the outset. In practice, a modernization or re-procurement is an opportunity — and likely an obligation — to build in full accessibility compliance, since new systems do not inherit the undue burden exception of their predecessors.

---

6. PRACTICAL RECOMMENDATIONS

Given the scenario described:

a) Engage your agency's Section 508 Coordinator immediately — every federal agency is required to have one.

b) Commission a phased remediation analysis — the $3.5M figure may assume full immediate remediation; a phased approach over multiple budget cycles may reduce the annual burden and make compliance more achievable.

c) Consult with the agency's General Counsel before finalizing the undue burden determination — the written determination must withstand legal scrutiny.

d) Notify the Access Board and OMB if required — some agencies are required to report accessibility exceptions through established reporting mechanisms.

e) Document the alternative means of access in detail, and test it with actual users with disabilities where possible.

f) Include Section 508 compliance requirements in any future modernization planning or budget requests to ensure successor systems are fully compliant.

---

SUMMARY TABLE

| Element                     | Requirement                                                  |
|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| Legal authority             | 29 U.S.C. § 794d; E202.5, 36 CFR Part 1194                  |
| Standard                    | "Significant difficulty or expense"                          |
| Factors                     | Agency resources, size, cost vs. budget, nature of operation |
| Documentation               | Written determination required                               |
| Who signs                   | Agency head or formally delegated official                   |
| Full exemption?             | NO — alternative access must still be provided               |
| Duration                    | Must be re-evaluated if ICT is altered or replaced           |

---

This guidance is based on the Revised Section 508 Standards (2018) and the underlying statute. Agencies should consult legal counsel and their Section 508 Coordinator for agency-specific implementation guidance.

Test 1: Colour Contrast Audit (SC 1.4.3)

Prompt: Our website uses the following colour combinations. Which ones fail WCAG 2.1 AA contrast requirements and what should we replace them with? Body text #767676/#FFFFFF, Nav links #0066CC/#FFFFFF, Button text #FFFFFF/#3D9970, Placeholder #AAAAAA/#FFFFFF, Error messages #CC0000/#FFFFFF, Disabled button #999999/#CCCCCC.

With WCAG Skill

Identifies SC 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) Level AA. Flags button text (#FFFFFF/#3D9970, ~3.51:1 — fails 4.5:1 for normal text) and placeholder (#AAAAAA/#FFFFFF, ~2.32:1 — fails; not exempt). Confirms passing combinations (nav links 5.56:1, error messages 5.89:1). Notes disabled button exempt as inactive UI component. Provides specific replacement colours: #2E7A58 for button (~5.38:1) and #696969 for placeholder (~5.08:1).

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 6/6 assertions passed (100%)
✅Correctly identifies SC 1.4.3 as the applicable criterion with level AA
✅Correctly flags button text (#FFFFFF on #3D9970) as failing
✅Correctly flags placeholder text (#AAAAAA on #FFFFFF) as failing
✅Correctly identifies disabled button as exempt from contrast requirements
✅Provides specific replacement colour suggestions for failures
✅Correctly identifies all passing combinations
Baseline (No Skill)

Correctly identifies the two failing colour pairs and the disabled button exemption. Provides replacement colours. Does not cite SC 1.4.3 by number; references WCAG 2.1 AA only generically.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/6 assertions passed (83%)
❌Correctly identifies SC 1.4.3 as the applicable criterion with level AA
✅Correctly flags button text (#FFFFFF on #3D9970) as failing
✅Correctly flags placeholder text (#AAAAAA on #FFFFFF) as failing
✅Correctly identifies disabled button as exempt from contrast requirements
✅Provides specific replacement colour suggestions for failures
✅Correctly identifies all passing combinations

Test 2: WCAG 2.2 Upgrade Criteria

Prompt: We are currently conforming to WCAG 2.1 AA. What new success criteria do we need to meet to upgrade our conformance claim to WCAG 2.2 AA? For each new criterion, explain what it requires and give a concrete example of what might fail.

With WCAG Skill

Lists all 4 new AA criteria (2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured, 2.5.7 Dragging Movements, 2.5.8 Target Size, 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication), the 2 new Level A criteria that also apply (3.2.6 Consistent Help, 3.3.7 Redundant Entry), explicitly notes SC 4.1.1 Parsing removed, and distinguishes 3 AAA-only additions (2.4.12, 2.4.13, 3.3.9). Concrete failure examples for each criterion.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Lists all 4 new AA-level criteria added in WCAG 2.2
✅Lists new Level A criteria in 2.2 that also apply for AA conformance
✅Mentions SC 4.1.1 Parsing was removed in WCAG 2.2
✅Provides a concrete failure example for each new AA criterion
✅Notes AAA-level criteria added in 2.2 as optional
Baseline (No Skill)

Correctly lists all 6 required criteria (4 AA + 2 new Level A) and notes 4.1.1 removal. Does not mention the AAA-level additions (2.4.12, 2.4.13, 3.3.9) that are optional.

⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4/5 assertions passed (80%)
✅Lists all 4 new AA-level criteria added in WCAG 2.2
✅Lists new Level A criteria in 2.2 that also apply for AA conformance
✅Mentions SC 4.1.1 Parsing was removed in WCAG 2.2
✅Provides a concrete failure example for each new AA criterion
❌Notes AAA-level criteria added in 2.2 as optional

Test 3: React Modal ARIA Code Review

Prompt: Our React app has a custom modal dialog that appears when users click 'Edit Profile'. Review this code and identify all WCAG accessibility issues. [ProfileModal component using div with no role, span labels, no focus management, no Escape handler]

With WCAG Skill

Identifies 7 distinct violations with SC citations: missing role=dialog/aria-modal/aria-labelledby (SC 4.1.2), no focus movement on open (SC 2.4.3), no focus trap (SC 2.1.2), span instead of label (SC 1.3.1), no Escape handler (SC 2.1.1), focus not returned on close (SC 2.4.3), background content not made inert. Provides complete corrected React component with createPortal, useRef, focus trap, Escape handler, and label elements.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 6/6 assertions passed (100%)
✅Identifies missing role=dialog and aria-modal (SC 4.1.2)
✅Identifies missing focus management on open (SC 2.4.3)
✅Identifies no focus trap (SC 2.1.2)
✅Identifies span used instead of label (SC 1.3.1)
✅Identifies missing Escape key handler (SC 2.1.1)
✅Provides corrected code with proper ARIA attributes
Baseline (No Skill)

Correctly identifies all 5 WCAG violations (role, focus management, focus trap, label, Escape). Does not provide corrected code — only lists the issues as a checklist.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/6 assertions passed (83%)
✅Identifies missing role=dialog and aria-modal (SC 4.1.2)
✅Identifies missing focus management on open (SC 2.4.3)
✅Identifies no focus trap (SC 2.1.2)
✅Identifies span used instead of label (SC 1.3.1)
✅Identifies missing Escape key handler (SC 2.1.1)
❌Provides corrected code with proper ARIA attributes

Test 4: WCAG Legal Compliance by Region

Prompt: We are a SaaS company with customers in the US, EU, and UK. What WCAG version and conformance level do we need to meet for legal compliance in each region? We sell to both government agencies and private sector businesses.

With WCAG Skill

Covers all 5 jurisdiction segments: US federal (Section 508 → WCAG 2.0 AA min, 2.1 AA market standard), US state/local (ADA Title II DOJ rule → WCAG 2.1 AA, April 2026 deadline passed), US private (ADA Title III → WCAG 2.1 AA de facto), EU public/private (EN 301 549 → WCAG 2.1 AA; EAA June 2025 now in force), UK (PSBAR 2018 → WCAG 2.1 AA; Equality Act). Recommends WCAG 2.2 AA as unified target with 5-step roadmap.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Correctly states US federal requirement: Section 508 / WCAG 2.0 AA minimum
✅Correctly states EU requirement: EN 301 549 / WCAG 2.1 AA via EAA and WAD
✅Correctly states UK requirement: UK PSBAR 2018 / WCAG 2.1 AA
✅Covers both government and private sector distinctions in each region
✅Provides a practical unified recommendation
Baseline (No Skill)

Correctly identifies WCAG 2.1 AA as the cross-jurisdictional standard. Covers all three regions with appropriate law citations. Recommends targeting WCAG 2.1 AA and notes planning for 2.2 AA.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Correctly states US federal requirement: Section 508 / WCAG 2.0 AA minimum
✅Correctly states EU requirement: EN 301 549 / WCAG 2.1 AA via EAA and WAD
✅Correctly states UK requirement: UK PSBAR 2018 / WCAG 2.1 AA
✅Covers both government and private sector distinctions in each region
✅Provides a practical unified recommendation

Test 5: Accessibility Statement Drafting

Prompt: Write an accessibility statement for our e-commerce website 'ShopEasy'. We currently meet most WCAG 2.1 AA criteria but have three known issues: older PDFs (SC 1.1.1, 4.1.2), live chat not announcing messages (SC 4.1.3), video demos lack audio descriptions (SC 1.2.5). Last audit: March 15, 2024 using automated tools, NVDA, and VoiceOver.

With WCAG Skill

Produces a publication-ready accessibility statement with 10 structured sections: correctly uses "partially conforms" status, lists all 3 issues with SC citations (1.1.1, 4.1.2, 4.1.3, 1.2.5), includes March 15 2024 audit date and testing methodology, multi-jurisdiction complaints pathway (EU enforcement authority, UK EHRC, US ADA, Australian AHRC), and all EU Web Accessibility Directive mandatory elements (Decision 2018/1523).

Test 4: Ransomware Attack by Third-Party Supplier

Prompt: We just discovered that one of our IT suppliers had a ransomware attack and our Restricted data may have been compromised. What do we need to do under NZISM?

With NZISM Skill

Comprehensive incident response guide covering all five key areas: (1) NCSC NZ mandatory notification with specific contact details (website, 24/7 phone, email) and guidance that suspicion alone triggers reporting; (2) Privacy Commissioner notification under Privacy Act 2020 with harm assessment guidance; (3) Detailed evidence preservation steps including supplier instructions, chain of custody, and what to request; (4) Nine mandatory contractual clauses that should have been in the supplier contract (notification timeframes, right to audit, evidence preservation, regulatory cooperation, post-incident reporting, subcontractor flow-down, termination rights); (5) Post-incident review as a mandatory NZISM requirement with 4-6 week timing and specific scope requirements.

✅Evidence preservation steps provided — do not wipe systems, instruct supplier in writing, chain of custody
✅Supplier notification clause identified as a gap — mandatory contractual clauses listed
✅Post-incident review stated as mandatory NZISM requirement with timing and scope
Baseline (No Skill)

Covers all key obligations: declares incident under NZISM Chapter 5, escalates to Head of Agency and GCISO (DPMC), notifies NCSC for technical assistance, addresses Privacy Act 2020 and Privacy Commissioner notification, requires evidence preservation and forensic investigation, reviews supplier contract obligations, mandates post-incident review. Comprehensive response hitting all five assertion areas with good detail on notification hierarchy and process.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)

✅Privacy Commissioner notification required where personal information involved under Privacy Act 2020
✅Evidence preservation steps provided — preserve logs, do not rebuild systems before forensic preservation
✅Supplier contract reviewed for notification obligations — gaps identified
✅Post-incident review stated as mandatory NZISM requirement with root cause analysis and remediation plan

Test 1: Singapore SaaS Extraterritorial Scope

Prompt: Our company is based in Singapore and we provide a SaaS HR platform to Vietnamese enterprise clients. Does Vietnam's new Personal Data Protection Law apply to us and what do we need to do?

With VN-PDPL Skill

Confirms extraterritorial applicability under Law No. 91/2025/QH15 and Decree 356/2025/ND-CP (effective 1 January 2026). Specifies the 60-day cross-border impact assessment deadline to MPS with 6-month renewal cycle. States the 72-hour dual notification requirement (authority AND data subjects simultaneously). Lists all six data subject rights with specific response timeframes (2 working days to acknowledge, 10 days for access/correction, 20 days for deletion, 15 days for withdrawal). Explicitly classifies Vietnam Citizen Identification (CCCD) numbers as basic personal data, not sensitive data, with practical guidance on what triggers the sensitive data regime.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Extraterritorial applicability confirmed — Singapore SaaS processing Vietnamese data is squarely in scope
✅Cross-border impact assessment required within 60 days with 6-month renewal cycle
✅72-hour breach notification to authority AND data subjects simultaneously
✅Data subject rights and timeframes listed (acknowledge 2 days; access/correction 10 days; deletion 20 days; withdrawal 15 days)
✅National ID (CCCD) explicitly classified as basic personal data, not sensitive data
Baseline (No Skill)

Confirms extraterritorial applicability under Decree 13/2023/ND-CP. Covers cross-border transfer impact assessment and MPS filing requirement. States 72-hour breach notification to MPS. Lists eight data subject rights. Does not specifically address Vietnam's National ID (CCCD) classification as basic vs. sensitive data — discusses sensitive data categories broadly but does not confirm that national ID cards fall in the basic category.

⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4/5 assertions passed (80%)
✅Extraterritorial applicability confirmed
✅Cross-border impact assessment required and MPS filing mentioned
✅72-hour breach notification to authority mentioned
✅Data subject rights listed (right to know, access, correct, delete, restrict, portability, object, automated decisions)
❌National ID (CCCD) not specifically confirmed as basic data — sensitive data categories discussed without classifying national IDs

Test 2: Fintech Breach Notification

With VN-PDPL Skill

Provides a structured breach response under Law No. 91/2025/QH15 and Decree 356/2025/ND-CP. Confirms 72-hour MPS notification deadline with simultaneous data subject notification. Explicitly invokes the dual notification obligation for the finance sector: Decree 356/2025/ND-CP Article 8 requires concurrent notification to the State Bank of Vietnam (SBV) as the sector regulator. Baseline (No Skill)

Does not mention the requirement to maintain an internal breach register — focuses on external notification obligations and immediate response checklist without the internal recordkeeping requirement.

⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4/5 assertions passed (80%)

Test 3: Telemedicine DPIA with AI Behavioural Analysis

Prompt: We run a telemedicine platform in Vietnam that collects patient health records, facial biometrics for identity verification, and uses AI to analyse patient behaviour patterns. What DPIA obligations apply and what are patients' rights regarding the AI processing?

With VN-PDPL Skill

Identifies all three data categories (health records, facial biometrics, AI behavioural analysis) as sensitive personal data requiring three separate, explicit consent flows under Law No. 91/2025/QH15 and Decree 356/2025/ND-CP. States DPIA is mandatory within 60 days of first processing — SME exemption does not apply because sensitive data is processed. Covers the patient right to opt out of AI automated processing with requirement to provide human-reviewed alternative. Details all five required DPIA content elements (data categories, purpose, retention, security measures, risk assessment). Specifies ongoing periodic AI assessments required at least annually, triggered by model changes, bias findings, or new use cases.

✅AI opt-out/object right — patients may opt out of AI behavioural analysis with human-reviewed alternative required
✅DPIA content requirements specified: data categories, purpose, retention, security measures, risk assessment
✅Periodic AI assessments required — at least annually or upon material changes to model or data
Baseline (No Skill)

Identifies all three data types as sensitive requiring explicit consent (separate for each). Covers DPIA obligation under Article 24 with MPS submission requirement. Addresses AI transparency, automated decision-making rights, and right to human review. Details security requirements and biometric-specific obligations. Does not mention the requirement for ongoing periodic AI assessments — discusses the one-time DPIA and initial AI assessment without specifying the ongoing reassessment obligation at regular intervals.

⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4/5 assertions passed (80%)
✅Health, biometric, and behavioural data all classified as sensitive — three separate explicit consents required
✅DPIA mandatory before commencing processing with MPS submission
✅AI opt-out/object right — patients have right to human review of AI-generated health decisions
✅DPIA content requirements specified: processing activities, necessity, risks, risk measures
❌Periodic AI assessments not mentioned — only one-time DPIA addressed without ongoing reassessment obligation

Test 4: Micro-Enterprise SME Exemptions

Prompt: We're a micro-enterprise with 8 employees running an e-commerce store in Vietnam. We collect customer names, email addresses, and purchase history. What VN-PDPL obligations apply to us and are there any exemptions?

With VN-PDPL Skill

Confirms the 5-year SME/micro-enterprise exemption applies under Law No. 91/2025/QH15 and Decree 356/2025/ND-CP. Specifies the three exempted articles: Article 21 (DPIAs), Article 22 (mandatory security measures), Article 33(2) (certain processor obligations). States the exemption runs from 1 January 2026 to 31 December 2030. Clarifies that basic obligations still apply regardless of exemption: consent, privacy notice, data subject rights. Confirms DPO is not mandatory where only basic personal data is processed. Classifies names, emails, and purchase history as basic personal data (not sensitive), explaining what would trigger loss of the exemption.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅SME exemption exists — Articles 21, 22, and 33(2) are exempt for micro-enterprises processing basic data
✅5-year exemption period from 1 January 2026 (expires 31 December 2030) stated
✅Basic obligations still apply: consent, privacy notice, data subject rights
✅DPO not mandatory where only basic personal data is processed
✅Names and email addresses classified as basic personal data
Baseline (No Skill)

States that the PDPL applies to the business regardless of size and that no SME exemption exists (based on Decree 13/2023/ND-CP). Lists standard obligations: consent, privacy notice, data subject rights, data security, 72-hour breach notification, record-keeping. Correctly notes DPO is likely not mandatory for a small business processing only basic data. Classifies names and emails as basic personal data. Fundamentally incorrect on the SME exemption — the law it references predates the 2025 PDPL which introduced the exemption.

⭐⭐ 2/5 assertions passed (40%)
❌SME exemption stated NOT to exist — baseline incorrectly says the PDPL has no small-business exemption
❌5-year exemption period not mentioned — baseline unaware of transitional exemption window
❌Basic obligations described but not framed in context of what applies despite exemption
✅DPO not mandatory for small business processing basic data — correct
✅Names and email addresses classified as basic personal data — correct

Test 5: Pre-Ticked Consent and Withdrawal

Prompt: Our website currently uses pre-ticked consent boxes and one master checkbox that covers marketing, analytics, and sharing data with partners. A user wants to withdraw consent for marketing only. What do we need to change and how long do we have to act on their withdrawal?

With VN-PDPL Skill

Cites Decree 356/2025/ND-CP Article 6 explicitly — pre-ticked boxes and inaction do not constitute valid consent. Confirms bundled (master) consent is invalid and must be split into separate per-purpose unchecked checkboxes. States withdrawal must be honoured within 15 working days under Decree 356/2025/ND-CP. Clarifies that withdrawal does not affect prior lawful processing (retroactive lawfulness preserved). Requires three separate unchecked checkboxes for marketing, analytics, and partner sharing with each independently selectable.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Pre-ticked boxes invalid — Decree 356 Art. 6 cited: silence and inaction are not valid consent
✅Bundled consent invalid — master checkbox covering multiple purposes does not meet specificity requirement
✅Withdrawal must be honoured within 15 working days per Decree 356/2025/ND-CP
✅Withdrawal does not retroactively affect prior lawful processing — prior processing remains valid
✅Separate per-purpose unchecked checkboxes required (one each for marketing, analytics, partner sharing)
Baseline (No Skill)

Correctly identifies pre-ticked boxes as invalid and bundled consent as non-compliant. Requires separate per-purpose unchecked checkboxes. States withdrawal must be acted on "without undue delay" and sets a 30-day outside limit (referencing GDPR standards, not Vietnam PDPL's specific 15 working days). Does not mention that withdrawal does not affect prior lawful processing — focuses on forward-looking obligations without clarifying the retroactive lawfulness preservation principle.

⭐⭐⭐ 3/5 assertions passed (60%)
✅Pre-ticked boxes invalid — affirmative opt-in required
✅Bundled consent invalid — separate per-purpose checkboxes required
❌Withdrawal timeframe stated as "30 days" — VN-PDPL requires 15 working days per Decree 356
❌Withdrawal effect on prior processing not addressed — retroactive lawfulness preservation not mentioned
✅Separate per-purpose unchecked checkboxes required for marketing, analytics, and partner sharing

Test 1: Password Manager Classification

Prompt: We make a password manager sold as a subscription service in the EU. What product class does it fall into under the Cyber Resilience Act, what conformity assessment route do we need, and what are the key Annex I requirements we must meet?

With EU CRA Skill

Confirms password managers are explicitly listed as Class I under Annex III of Regulation (EU) 2024/2847. States Class I allows manufacturer's choice of self-assessment (Module A) OR third-party Notified Body — self-certification is available. Covers all Annex I Part I requirements: no known exploitable vulnerabilities, encryption at rest and in transit (AES-256, TLS 1.2+, zero-knowledge architecture alignment), secure by default (auto-lock, clipboard clearing), MFA support, signed updates with integrity verification, minimal data processing, and exploit mitigation. Covers Annex I Part II requirements: VDP publication, SBOM on request, ENISA 24/72-hour reporting for actively exploited vulnerabilities, free security updates throughout minimum 5-year support period.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Class I under Annex III — password managers explicitly listed
✅Self-assessment OR Notified Body — manufacturer's choice for Class I products
✅Encryption at rest and in transit required (AES-256, TLS 1.2+)
✅No known exploitable vulnerabilities at market placement — ongoing obligation
✅VDP, SBOM on request, ENISA 24/72-hour reporting from Annex I Part II
Baseline (No Skill)

Confirms Class I classification and password managers explicitly listed in Annex III. States manufacturer's choice of self-assessment (with harmonised standards) or Notified Body. Covers security-by-design requirements, SSDLC, supported period (5 years minimum), technical documentation, CE marking, and user information requirements. Covers VDP, handling discovered vulnerabilities, ENISA 24/72-hour and 14-day reporting, and SBOM. Complete and accurate response.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Class I under Annex III — password managers explicitly listed
✅Self-assessment OR Notified Body — manufacturer's choice stated
✅Encryption at rest and in transit required
✅No known exploitable vulnerabilities at market placement
✅VDP, SBOM, ENISA 24/72-hour and 14-day reporting covered

Test 2: Industrial PLC Class II Classification

Prompt: We manufacture industrial PLCs (programmable logic controllers) used in factory automation. How does the EU CRA classify our product and what conformity assessment process must we follow?

With EU CRA Skill

Confirms PLCs are explicitly Class II under Annex IV of Regulation (EU) 2024/2847 — the highest-risk category requiring mandatory Notified Body assessment with no self-assessment option. Details the 5-step Notified Body process (application, document review including threat model, technical evaluation, certificate issuance, ongoing surveillance). Covers 24/72-hour ENISA and national CSIRT reporting for actively exploited vulnerabilities plus 14-day final report. Technical documentation requirements include security risk assessment AND a specific threat model addressing OT/ICS adversaries, attack paths from IT to OT networks, physical access scenarios, supply chain compromise, firmware manipulation, and DoS affecting physical processes. States 5-year minimum support period. Specifies €15 million or 2.5% of global turnover penalty for Annex I violations.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Class II under Annex IV — mandatory Notified Body assessment, no self-assessment permitted
✅24-hour and 72-hour ENISA/CSIRT reporting for actively exploited vulnerabilities
✅Technical documentation requires security risk assessment AND specific threat model for OT/ICS environments
✅5-year minimum support period for security updates
✅€15 million or 2.5% of global turnover penalty for Annex I essential requirement violations
Baseline (No Skill)

Confirms Class II classification and mandatory Notified Body involvement. Describes assessment module options (B+C, B+D, B+E, B+F, or H). Covers Annex I security-by-design and vulnerability handling requirements. Mentions SBOM, vulnerability disclosure, and ENISA/CSIRT 24/72-hour reporting. States 5-year minimum support period. Does not specifically mention that technical documentation must include a threat model as a distinct named element — covers security risk assessment and gap analysis but not the specific OT/ICS threat modelling requirement. Does not mention the €15 million/2.5% penalty figure.

⭐⭐⭐ 3/5 assertions passed (60%)
✅Class II — mandatory Notified Body assessment confirmed
✅24-hour and 72-hour ENISA/CSIRT reporting for actively exploited vulnerabilities
❌Threat model not specifically named as a required technical documentation element — only security risk assessment mentioned
✅5-year minimum support period stated
❌€15 million/2.5% penalty not mentioned — no penalty figures provided

Test 3: Consumer IoT Vulnerability Reporting

Prompt: We sell connected consumer IoT devices and just discovered a vulnerability being actively exploited in the wild. Walk me through the exact reporting obligations and timeline under the EU CRA.

With EU CRA Skill

Provides a precise 5-step incident response walkthrough with exact timelines under Regulation (EU) 2024/2847 Annex I Part II: (1) 24-hour early warning to ENISA and national CSIRTs from moment of awareness; (2) 72-hour full report with detailed technical content requirements; (3) 14-day final detailed report with root cause analysis; (4) prompt user notification with interim mitigations and patch instructions; (5) free security update that cannot be disabled by users. Confirms default class for consumer IoT thermostats and specifies minimum 5-year support period for free security updates.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅24-hour early warning to ENISA and national CSIRT(s) from awareness of active exploitation
✅72-hour full report to ENISA and national CSIRT(s) with detailed technical content
✅14-day final detailed report with root cause analysis and remediation details
✅Notify affected users promptly with interim mitigations and patch availability
✅Free security update required — cannot be disabled by users, minimum 5-year support period
Baseline (No Skill)

Covers 24-hour early warning to ENISA and national CSIRT, 72-hour full incident notification, publishing a security advisory and coordinated disclosure, and a final report to ENISA/CSIRT. States the final report deadline as "no later than one month" after the 72-hour notification — which does not match the CRA's 14-day requirement. Covers user notification and free security update obligation. The "1 month" final report deadline is materially different from the actual 14-day requirement.

⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4/5 assertions passed (80%)
✅24-hour early warning to ENISA and national CSIRT(s)
✅72-hour full incident notification to ENISA and national CSIRT(s)
❌Final report stated as "1 month" — CRA requires 14 days from awareness, not 1 month from 72-hour notification
✅Notify affected users — security advisory and user notification covered
✅Free security update required — mandatory and free of charge

Test 4: SBOM Requirements

Prompt: What are our SBOM obligations under the EU CRA? What format should we use, what must it contain, and how long do we need to maintain it?

With EU CRA Skill

Comprehensive SBOM guidance under Regulation (EU) 2024/2847: SBOMs are on-request to authorities and users, NOT mandatory public disclosure. Minimum content is top-level open-source dependencies (with encouragement to go further to transitive dependencies and commercial components). Recommends SPDX (ISO/IEC 5962:2021, Linux Foundation) and CycloneDX (OWASP, VEX-compatible) as the two CRA-aligned formats. Explicitly states SBOMs must be kept current throughout the support period — static one-time generation at release is insufficient. Retention for the full support period (minimum 5 years), with historical SBOM versions preserved for demonstration of due diligence. Recommends CI/CD integration for automated SBOM generation.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅On-request to authorities only — NOT mandatory public publication
✅Minimum content: top-level open-source dependencies at minimum
✅SPDX and CycloneDX formats recommended — machine-readable, both CRA-aligned
✅Must be kept current throughout support period — static generation insufficient
✅Maintained for minimum 5-year support period — historical versions archived
Baseline (No Skill)

Correctly states SBOMs are not required to be publicly published — internal documentation available to market surveillance authorities on request. Minimum content covers top-level dependencies. Recommends SPDX and CycloneDX as CRA-compliant formats. States SBOMs must be "kept up to date" as part of ongoing technical documentation maintenance. However, does not explicitly state the SBOM must be maintained for the full 5-year minimum support period — mentions general retention without specifying the support-period-length retention obligation and does not note that historical SBOM versions must be preserved.

⭐⭐⭐ 3/5 assertions passed (60%)
✅On-request to authorities only — NOT mandatory public publication
✅Minimum content: top-level dependencies covered
✅SPDX and CycloneDX formats recommended
❌Currency obligation not explicitly stated — "kept up to date" mentioned without specifying ongoing requirement vs. static generation
❌5-year minimum retention period not specifically stated — general documentation retention mentioned without the support-period-length obligation

Test 5: Importer Obligations for Non-Compliant Security Cameras

Prompt: We import connected security cameras from a Chinese manufacturer to sell in the EU. The cameras ship with a default 'admin/admin' password and have several unnecessary open ports. What are our obligations as an importer, and what's our liability?

With EU CRA Skill

Confirms that the default "admin/admin" password violates Annex I Part I secure-by-default requirement and unnecessary open ports violate the minimal attack surface requirement. Identifies importer obligations: verify manufacturer conformity assessment before market placement, do not place non-compliant product on market, notify manufacturer AND relevant competent authorities of non-compliance, cooperate with market surveillance authorities, withdraw or recall if already on market. States the maximum penalty for Annex I essential requirement violations: €15 million or 2.5% of global annual turnover (whichever is higher), with additional penalties for procedural violations. Notes that importer can be held directly liable regardless of manufacturer's China location.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Default admin password violates secure-by-default requirement under Annex I Part I
✅Unnecessary open ports violate minimal attack surface requirement under Annex I Part I
✅Importer must verify manufacturer compliance before placing product on EU market
✅Must notify manufacturer AND competent authorities of identified non-compliance
✅€15 million or 2.5% of global annual turnover penalty for Annex I violations
Baseline (No Skill)

Confirms default "admin/admin" violates Annex I Essential Requirement 1.2(b) secure-by-default and unnecessary open ports violate 1.2(c) minimal attack surface. Details importer obligations under Article 19: pre-market due diligence, verification of CE marking and conformity assessment, and do not place non-compliant product on market. Covers notification to manufacturer and market surveillance authorities, corrective action, withdrawal and recall. States €15 million or 2.5% penalty for Annex I violations. Complete and accurate response.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5 assertions passed (100%)
✅Default admin password violates secure-by-default requirement under Annex I
✅Unnecessary open ports violate minimal attack surface requirement under Annex I
✅Importer must verify manufacturer compliance before placing product on EU market
✅Must notify manufacturer and market surveillance authorities of non-compliance
✅€15 million or 2.5% of global annual turnover penalty for Annex I violations
